topics.title: General principles for location and layout of shipyards: Functional and geographic aspects, transport, supply industry and human resource
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST631 - SHIP PRODUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** General principles for location and layout of shipyards: Functional and geographic aspects, transport, supply industry and human resource

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for **Ship Production Engineering (PENST631)**, the *General Principles for Location and Layout of Shipyards* is defined as the systematic engineering, logistical, and economic study that determines the optimal **geographical positioning** and the **internal functional arrangement** of an industrial facility dedicated to the construction, repair, conversion, and dismantling of marine vessels. It integrates industrial engineering, marine hydrodynamics, civil engineering, and supply chain management to maximize productivity, minimize production cost per compensated gross ton (CGT), and ensure occupational safety.

The **layout** of a shipyard refers to the spatial organization of its physical assets — building docks, slipways, outfitting berths, fabrication shops, material storage yards, and auxiliary facilities — arranged along a defined production flow. The **location** refers to the macro-level geographical site selection based on natural, infrastructural, and socio-economic factors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The general principles are broadly classified into **four interdependent pillars**:
> 1. Functional and Geographic Aspects
> 2. Transport Infrastructure
> 3. Supply Industry Proximity
> 4. Human Resource Availability
>
> Examiners frequently frame Part B (14-mark) questions asking students to *evaluate* or *compare* these principles for a hypothetical shipyard project.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine building a **massive LEGO city**. Before you start placing bricks, you must decide:
- **Where** to place the city (near a river? near a forest? near suppliers?) — this is **LOCATION**.
- **Inside the city**, where do you put the brick factory, the storage warehouse, the testing track, and the shipping dock? — this is **LAYOUT**.

A shipyard is essentially a giant industrial LEGO city for ships. Ships are too large to be assembled in one place and moved; they are built in stages across different zones. So, just like LEGO, if the warehouse is too far from the dock, you waste time and fuel moving bricks (steel plates). If the city is built in a desert with no water, ships cannot be launched!

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industrial Metric:** Modern shipyards measure efficiency using the **Hull-Block Outfitting Ratio (HBOR)** and the **Steel Throughput per Meter of Quay Wall (tons/m)**. A well-planned layout minimizes the **"distance × tonnage"** product for every plate moved.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following engineering standards govern shipyard planning (as per ISO 9001:2015 quality systems, ISO 14001 environmental systems, and OHSAS 18001 / ISO 45001 safety systems):

- **Minimum Water Depth at Building Berth:** $D_{min} \geq d_{ship} + 1.5 \text{ m}$ (under-keel clearance)
- **Quay Wall Load Capacity:** typically **40 to 80 kN/m²** for outfitting berths
- **Goliath / Heavy-Lift Crane Capacity:** **300 tonnes to 1,200 tonnes** for modern mega-block assembly
- **Standard Plot Size (Panel Line):** $\sim 24 \text{ m} \times 24 \text{ m}$ to $30 \text{ m} \times 30 \text{ m}$
- **Workforce Density:** Approximately **0.8 to 1.2 workers per m²** of covered fabrication area
- **Land Area Rule of Thumb:** **0.4 to 0.6 hectares per 1,000 CGT/year** annual output

## 1.4 Functional Aspects — Definition

**Functional aspects** concern the *internal organization* of the shipyard into purpose-specific zones, each optimized for a particular stage of the **Integrated Production Process (IPP)**:

1. **Steel Stockyard & Blasting/Priming Shop** — receipt, storage, surface preparation
2. **Panel Fabrication Shop** — cutting, bending, forming of flat panels
3. **Block Assembly Shop (Tertiary Blocks)** — joining of panels into small 3D units
4. **Unit / Mega-Block Assembly Area** — assembly of large structural units
5. **Erection Berth / Building Dock** — final joining of blocks into the complete hull
6. **Outfitting Quay** — installation of machinery, piping, electrical, HVAC
7. **Launching / Floating Facility** — graving dock, inclined slipway, or floating dock
8. **Commissioning & Sea Trial Berth** — final testing
9. **Auxiliary Zones** — offices, canteen, medical, fire station, training center

## 1.5 Geographic Aspects — Definition

**Geographic aspects** govern the *external* suitability of the site:

- **Coastal Configuration** (deep sheltered harbour preferred)
- **Tidal Range** (lower tidal range simplifies launching)
- **Soil Bearing Capacity** (soft alluvium → expensive pile foundations)
- **Seismicity** (earthquake zones require special design)
- **Climate** (extreme weather reduces working days)
- **Land Availability for Future Expansion**
- **Proximity to Shipping Lanes and Trade Routes**

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Example (India):** Cochin Shipyard Limited (CSL) is located on the Willingdon Island because of the **natural deep harbour**, **sheltered waters of the Arabian Sea**, and **proximity to the Suez–Far East trade route**. This makes its geographic location strategically ideal.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: The Five Pillars of Shipyard Site Selection

The selection of a shipyard location and its internal layout is a **multi-criteria decision-making (MCDM)** problem, typically evaluated using the **Weighted Sum Model (WSM)** or the **Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP)** developed by Saaty.

The five core pillars, as required by the KTU 2024 syllabus, are:

### Pillar 1 — Functional Aspects (Internal Layout Philosophy)

The internal layout must follow the **principle of progressive assembly with minimum backtracking** (also called the *"one-way flow"* or *"river-flow"* principle). Material moves in a **single, logical direction** from raw steel stock → finished vessel.

**Key Functional Zones (with engineering rationale):**

| Zone | Function | Typical Area | Engineering Reason |
|------|----------|--------------|-------------------|
| Stockyard | Steel plate storage | 15–20% of total area | Buffer between supplier and shop |
| Blasting Shop | Surface preparation | 5% | Health & environment control |
| Panel Shop | Flat panel fabrication | 20% | High automation, requires large span |
| Block Assembly | 3D block fabrication | 25% | Crane access on all sides |
| Erection Berth | Final hull joining | 15% | Close to launching facility |
| Outfitting Quay | Equipment installation | 10% | Adjacent to deep water |
| Auxiliary | Offices, services | 10% | Buffer zone from heavy work |

> [!NOTE]
> The **minimum total plot area** $A_{total}$ for a shipyard producing $P$ CGT/year is given by the empirical formula:
>
> $$A_{total} \approx 0.4 \text{ to } 0.6 \times P \quad \text{[hectares]}$$
>
> where $P$ is in thousands of CGT/year.

### Pillar 2 — Geographic Aspects (Macro Site Selection)

Geographic suitability is evaluated against **natural** and **climatic** criteria:

- **Water Depth:** $D_{water} \geq d_{max\_ship} + U_{keel} + T_{tide}$
- **Soil Bearing Capacity (SBC):** $\geq 100 \text{ kN/m}^2$ for ordinary foundations, $\geq 200 \text{ kN/m}^2$ for gantry crane rails
- **Tidal Range:** Ideally $< 2 \text{ m}$ (large tidal range forces use of graving docks)
- **Wave Height:** Significant wave height $H_s < 0.5 \text{ m}$ at the berth
- **Seismic Zone:** Must comply with IS 1893 (India) or equivalent code

### Pillar 3 — Transport (Inbound & Outbound Logistics)

A shipyard is a **high-tonnage, low-value-density** industry. Steel plates weigh ~25 tonnes per plate; engines weigh 200–2,000 tonnes. Therefore, **transport cost is a critical driver**.

- **Road access:** For 40–80 tonne trailers
- **Rail siding:** Direct rail into stockyard saves handling cost
- **Inland waterway / coastal shipping:** For very heavy items (e.g., main engine, propeller shaft)
- **Air connectivity:** For international experts and urgent spares

The **logistic cost per tonne-km** follows:

$$C_{logistic} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i \times m_i \times r_i$$

where $d_i$ = distance (km), $m_i$ = mass (tonnes), $r_i$ = rate (₹/tonne-km) for mode $i$.

### Pillar 4 — Supply Industry (Vendor Ecosystem)

A modern ship consumes **> 1,500 major components** and **> 100,000 line items**. A shipyard cannot be self-sufficient. The location must be near:

- **Steel mills** (primary source of HR plates, sections)
- **Marine equipment manufacturers** (engines, pumps, valves, electricals)
- **Foundries & forging units** (propellers, shaft brackets, stern tubes)
- **Paint & coating suppliers**
- **Class society surveyors** (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS, IRS offices)
- **Classification and certification bodies**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Just-In-Time (JIT) Supply Principle:** Modern Japanese and Korean shipyards (e.g., Hyundai Heavy Industries, Ulsan) maintain a **vendor park within 5 km** of the yard to enable JIT delivery, reducing in-yard inventory by up to 30%.

### Pillar 5 — Human Resource

A shipbuilding workforce is a mix of:

- **Skilled welders** (the largest single category — ~25–30% of direct labor)
- **Fitters, machinists, plumbers, electricians**
- **Engineers (production, design, quality)**
- **Supervisors and foremen**
- **Crane operators and riggers**

Selection criteria for human resource:

- **Availability of skilled workforce** within 50 km radius
- **Existing training infrastructure** (ITIs, polytechnics, engineering colleges)
- **Wage levels** vs. national/regional average
- **Labour law stability** and industrial relations climate
- **Cost of living index** for the locality

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Equation | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Plot Area | $A_{total} \approx k \cdot P$ | hectares | $P$ = production in 1000 CGT/yr; $k = 0.4$–$0.6$ |
| 2 | Minimum Water Depth | $D_{min} = d_{ship} + U_{keel} + T_{tide}$ | metres | Berth and approach channel design |
| 3 | Logistic Cost | $C_{logistic} = \sum d_i m_i r_i$ | ₹ | Vendor location optimization |
| 4 | Production Rate (Hull Steel) | $R_h = m_{steel} / t_{cycle}$ | tonnes/day | Capacity planning |
| 5 | Outfitting Density | $\rho_{out} = V_{out} / L_{quay}$ | m³/m | Quay length estimation |
| 6 | Crane Utilization | $U_{crane} = t_{lift} / t_{total}$ | % | Layout efficiency check |
| 7 | Manning Density | $n_{workers} = \alpha \cdot A_{covered}$ | workers | $A_{covered}$ = covered area (m²); $\alpha = 0.8$–$1.2$ |
| 8 | Shipyard Capacity Index | $C_{yard} = L_{max} \times B_{max} \times D_{max}$ | m³ | Maximum vessel size buildable |
| 9 | Tide-dependent Launching Force | $F_{launch} = W_{ship} \cdot \sin(\theta) - F_{buoy}$ | N | Inclined slipway design |
| 10 | Vendor Proximity Score | $S_v = 1 / (1 + d_v / d_{ref})$ | dimensionless | Supply chain evaluation |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** In Part B questions, students are often asked to *"Justify the selection of a site for a shipyard"*. Always structure the answer using the **5-pillar framework** above, and supplement with **numerical justification** (e.g., compute $D_{min}$ for a given ship draft).

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

These principles are not academic — they are used by **shipyard design consultants** (e.g., Bassoe, Haskoning, Royal HaskoningDHV) while planning greenfield projects. For example:

- **Larsen & Toubro Shipbuilding, Kattupalli** (near Chennai) was located close to the **Ennore port** to leverage deep water and proximity to the **Sriperumbudur industrial belt** (automobile ancillaries → precision engineering skills for marine use).
- **Hindustan Shipyard Ltd, Visakhapatnam** leverages the **deep natural harbour** of the Bay of Bengal and the **steel supply from RINL** (Rashtriya Ispat Nigam Limited).
- **Mazagon Dock, Mumbai** uses the **sheltered Mumbai harbour** and proximity to the **defence procurement ecosystem**.

In short, the *general principles* are **the economic DNA** of every successful shipyard in the world.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Plot Area for a Greenfield Shipyard

**Problem Statement:** A shipyard is to be designed for an annual production of 240,000 CGT. Estimate the minimum land area required, the number of workers, and the total steel throughput capacity.

### Step 1: Identify the production target

$$P = 240{,}000 \text{ CGT/yr} = 240 \text{ (in thousands of CGT/yr)}$$

### Step 2: Apply the empirical land-area rule

Using the lower bound of the empirical formula for a *greenfield* yard with adequate space for future expansion:

$$A_{total} = k \cdot P$$

where $k = 0.5$ hectares per 1000 CGT/yr (mid-value).

$$A_{total} = 0.5 \times 240 = 120 \text{ hectares}$$

### Step 3: Calculate the workforce required

The total workforce $W$ is empirically related to the annual output and man-hours per CGT. For a modern yard:

$$W = \frac{P_{CGT} \times H_{CGT}}{T \times \eta}$$

where:
- $H_{CGT} = 35$ man-hours per CGT (modern benchmark, lower = better)
- $T = 2{,}000$ working hours per year per worker
- $\eta = 0.85$ (efficiency factor for absenteeism, idle time)

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= \frac{240{,}000 \times 35}{2{,}000 \times 0.85} \\
  &= \frac{8{,}400{,}000}{1{,}700} \\
  &= 4{,}941 \text{ workers (direct, in-house)}
\end{aligned}$$

Including indirect staff (admin, design, quality) at 30% of direct:

$$W_{total} = W \times 1.30 = 6{,}423 \approx 6{,}500 \text{ workers}$$

### Step 4: Compute the annual steel throughput

For merchant vessels, the **steel coefficient** (tonnes of steel per CGT) ranges from **0.15 to 0.25**. Using 0.20:

$$M_{steel} = P_{CGT} \times s_{CGT}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{steel} &= 240{,}000 \times 0.20 \\
          &= 48{,}000 \text{ tonnes/year}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Daily steel consumption

$$m_{daily} = \frac{M_{steel}}{N_{working\_days}} = \frac{48{,}000}{300} = 160 \text{ tonnes/day}$$

(Assuming 300 working days/year, accounting for holidays and weather)

### Step 6: Verify the stockyard capacity

The stockyard must hold at least **30 days** of inventory:

$$M_{stock} = m_{daily} \times 30 = 160 \times 30 = 4{,}800 \text{ tonnes}$$

If average plate weight = 8 tonnes/plate, number of plates in stock:

$$N_{plates} = \frac{4{,}800}{8} = 600 \text{ plates}$$

If each plate occupies a footprint of $12 \text{ m} \times 3 \text{ m} = 36 \text{ m}^2$ (with handling aisle):

$$A_{stockyard} = 600 \times 36 = 21{,}600 \text{ m}^2 \approx 2.16 \text{ hectares}$$

This represents $\sim 1.8\%$ of the total land, which is consistent with the standard ratio (15–20% of total area, but a fraction of that is the *active storage* zone).

## 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Water Depth at Outfitting Berth

**Problem Statement:** The largest vessel to be built has a loaded draft of 14.5 m. The site experiences a maximum tidal range of 1.8 m and a maximum significant wave height of 0.4 m. Compute the required minimum water depth at the outfitting quay, considering a safety under-keel clearance (UKC) of 15% of the draft.

### Step 1: Compute the safety under-keel clearance

$$U_{keel} = 0.15 \times d_{ship}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
U_{keel} &= 0.15 \times 14.5 \\
         &= 2.175 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2: Account for the wave-induced vertical motion (squat + heave)

For a vessel at berth, a conservative estimate is $H_{motion} \approx 0.5 \times H_s$:

$$H_{motion} = 0.5 \times 0.4 = 0.2 \text{ m}$$

### Step 3: Account for tidal variation

Use the *worst-case low water* scenario. The water depth must be maintained at the **lowest astronomical tide (LAT)**. Hence, no additional tide term is added at the worst case, but design must verify at all tides.

### Step 4: Total minimum water depth

$$D_{min} = d_{ship} + U_{keel} + H_{motion} + S_{sediment}$$

where $S_{sediment} = 0.5$ m is the sediment accumulation allowance between dredging cycles.

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{min} &= 14.5 + 2.175 + 0.2 + 0.5 \\
        &= 17.375 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Engineering Conclusion:** The berth must be dredged to at least **17.4 m** below LAT. This is one of the *first parameters* that disqualifies many otherwise attractive sites.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Site Evaluation

The following **Weighted Sum Model (WSM)** is the classical method used by KTU examiners for "site selection" Part B questions. Assume three candidate sites — A (Cochin), B (Mumbai), C (Paradip) — and the following data:

| Criterion (Weight $w_j$) | Site A | Site B | Site C |
|--------------------------|:------:|:------:|:------:|
| Water depth adequacy (0.20) | 9 | 8 | 9 |
| Soil bearing capacity (0.15) | 7 | 6 | 8 |
| Transport connectivity (0.15) | 8 | 9 | 7 |
| Supply industry proximity (0.20) | 9 | 9 | 6 |
| Human resource availability (0.15) | 9 | 8 | 6 |
| Climate & working days (0.10) | 7 | 7 | 8 |
| Expansion potential (0.05) | 6 | 5 | 9 |

The composite score for each site is:

$$S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot r_{ij}$$

where $r_{ij}$ is the rating of site $i$ on criterion $j$ (scale 0–10).

### Computation for Site A:

$$\begin{aligned}
S_A &= (0.20 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 9) + (0.10 \times 7) + (0.05 \times 6) \\
    &= 1.80 + 1.05 + 1.20 + 1.80 + 1.35 + 0.70 + 0.30 \\
    &= 8.20
\end{aligned}$$

### Computation for Site B:

$$\begin{aligned}
S_B &= (0.20 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 6) + (0.15 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 7) + (0.05 \times 5) \\
    &= 1.60 + 0.90 + 1.35 + 1.80 + 1.20 + 0.70 + 0.25 \\
    &= 7.80
\end{aligned}$$

### Computation for Site C:

$$\begin{aligned}
S_C &= (0.20 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 6) + (0.15 \times 6) + (0.10 \times 8) + (0.05 \times 9) \\
    &= 1.80 + 1.20 + 1.05 + 1.20 + 0.90 + 0.80 + 0.45 \\
    &= 7.40
\end{aligned}$$

### Final Ranking:

| Site | Score | Rank |
|------|:-----:|:----:|
| **A (Cochin)** | **8.20** | **1st** |
| B (Mumbai) | 7.80 | 2nd |
| C (Paradip) | 7.40 | 3rd |

**Engineering Interpretation:** Site A wins primarily due to its superior **human resource** and **supply industry** scores, even though Site C has a slight edge in **water depth** and **expansion potential**. The KTU examiner would mark this question highly if the student shows the *step-by-step calculation* and *concludes with a justified choice*.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Shipyard Site-Selection Decision Flow (Mermaid)

The following flow diagram captures the **systematic decision logic** that a shipyard planner follows when evaluating a site. It starts with broad macro-criteria and progressively narrows down to the final selection.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Shipyard Capacity] --> B[Identify Candidate Sites]
    B --> C[Evaluate Geographic Criteria]
    C --> C1[Water Depth]
    C --> C2[Soil Bearing Capacity]
    C --> C3[Tidal Range]
    C --> C4[Wave Climate]
    C1 --> D[Pass Geographic Filter]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E[Evaluate Functional Criteria]
    E --> E1[Total Land Area]
    E --> E2[Plot Geometry]
    E --> E3[Quay Line Length]
    E1 --> F[Pass Functional Filter]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Evaluate Transport Infrastructure]
    G --> G1[Road Connectivity]
    G --> G2[Rail Siding]
    G --> G3[Port Connectivity]
    G1 --> H[Pass Transport Filter]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[Evaluate Supply Industry]
    I --> I1[Steel Mills]
    I --> I2[Equipment Suppliers]
    I --> I3[Vendor Park Possibility]
    I1 --> J[Pass Supply Filter]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    J --> K[Evaluate Human Resource]
    K --> K1[Skilled Labour Availability]
    K --> K2[Training Institutes]
    K --> K3[Wage Level]
    K1 --> L[Compute Weighted Score WSM]
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L
    L --> M{Rank the Sites}
    M --> N[Select Site with Highest Score]
    N --> O[End: Final Site Selected]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Functional Block Layout of a Modern Shipyard

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **internal functional architecture** of a typical modern shipyard, mapped as a left-to-right material flow ("river-flow" principle). Each block represents a functional zone.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    raw1[Raw Steel Stockyard] --> raw2[Blasting and Priming Shop]
    raw2 --> fab1[Panel Fabrication Shop]
    fab1 --> fab2[Sub-Block Assembly Area]
    fab2 --> fab3[Block Assembly Hall]
    fab3 --> fab4[Mega-Block Assembly Area]
    fab4 --> erect[Erection Berth or Building Dock]
    erect --> launch[Launching Facility]
    launch --> outfit[Outfitting Quay]
    outfit --> comm[Commissioning Berth]
    comm --> trial[Sea Trial and Delivery]
    fab1 -.pre-outfitting.-> outfit
    fab3 -.pre-outfitting.-> outfit
    raw1 -.supply.-> fab1
    supp[Supply Industry Vendor Park] -.JIT.-> raw1
    supp -.JIT.-> erect
    supp -.JIT.-> outfit
    office[Admin and Design Office] -.engineering support.-> fab1
    office -.engineering support.-> erect
    training[Training Centre] -.manpower.-> fab3
    training -.manpower.-> erect
```

**Layout Interpretation (KTU Examiner's Read):**
- **Top row (left → right):** Main material flow from raw steel to sea trial — the *spine* of the yard.
- **Bottom row (pre-outfitting arrows):** Demonstrate modern *zone-outfitting* philosophy where outfitting work is done in parallel on blocks/sub-assemblies, *before* they reach the erection berth. This is the heart of the **"Outfitting-First" / "Pre-Outfitting"** strategy taught in modern ship production.
- **Auxiliary services (admin, vendor park, training):** Provide cross-cutting support to the entire flow.

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Cause-Effect (Ishikawa) Diagram for Shipyard Location

This is a **fishbone (Ishikawa) diagram** showing the five main categories of factors that influence shipyard location. This is a frequently asked KTU question type.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    goal[Optimal Shipyard Location and Layout] --> cat1[Geographic]
    goal --> cat2[Functional]
    goal --> cat3[Transport]
    goal --> cat4[Supply Industry]
    goal --> cat5[Human Resource]
    goal --> cat6[Policy and Environment]
    cat1 --> g1[Water Depth]
    cat1 --> g2[Soil Bearing]
    cat1 --> g3[Tidal Range]
    cat1 --> g4[Climate]
    cat2 --> f1[Plot Area]
    cat2 --> f2[Quay Length]
    cat2 --> f3[Building Dock Size]
    cat3 --> t1[Highway Access]
    cat3 --> t2[Rail Siding]
    cat3 --> t3[Inland Waterway]
    cat4 --> s1[Steel Mills]
    cat4 --> s2[Equipment Vendors]
    cat4 --> s3[Class Surveyors]
    cat5 --> h1[Skilled Welders]
    cat5 --> h2[Engineers]
    cat5 --> h3[Training Infra]
    cat6 --> p1[Government Policy]
    cat6 --> p2[SEZ Status]
    cat6 --> p3[Tax Holidays]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip for Students:** In a KTU 14-mark question, the Ishikawa approach gives you a *ready-made structure* to organize a 2-page answer into six clear sections. Use the same headings in your answer as in the diagram — examiners reward structural clarity.
