### 3.1 Derivation — Outfitting Density Ratio (ODR) Analytical Walk-through

A shipyard plans to build a 50,000 DWT bulk carrier. The total outfitting man-hours estimated by the cost office is **240,000 MH**. The planning engineer wishes to shift as much outfitting as possible to the block stage.

**Given:**
- $MH_{\text{total}} = 240{,}000 \text{ MH}$
- Target: $MH_{\text{block}} = 156{,}000 \text{ MH}$
- Remaining stages (unit + dock + commissioning) account for $84{,}000 \text{ MH}$.

**Step 1 — Apply the ODR formula:**

$$
\text{ODR} \;=\; \frac{MH_{\text{block}}}{MH_{\text{total}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
\text{ODR} \;=\; \frac{156{,}000}{240{,}000}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ODR} \;=\; \frac{156{,}000}{240{,}000} \;=\; \frac{156}{240} \;=\; \frac{13}{20} \;=\; 0.65
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
An ODR of **0.65 (65%)** indicates a modern, world-class yard. Legacy berth-method yards typically achieve ODR $\leq$ 0.20.

**Step 5 — Remaining Outfitting:**

$$
MH_{\text{remaining}} \;=\; MH_{\text{total}} - MH_{\text{block}} \;=\; 240{,}000 - 156{,}000 \;=\; 84{,}000 \text{ MH}
$$

This is the unavoidable work that *must* happen at the dock or during commissioning.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Block Painting Coverage

**Given:**
- Volume of paint supplied per coat: $V_{\text{paint}} = 250 \text{ L}$
- Solids Volume Fraction: $\text{SVF} = 0.55$
- Required Dry Film Thickness per coat: $\text{DFT}_1 = 125 \text{ μm} = 125 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$

**Step 1 — Convert paint volume to solid volume:**

$$
V_{\text{solid}} \;=\; V_{\text{paint}} \cdot \text{SVF} \;=\; 250 \times 0.55 \;=\; 137.5 \text{ L}
$$

Since $1 \text{ L} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$:

$$
V_{\text{solid}} \;=\; 137.5 \times 10^{-3} \;=\; 0.1375 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Compute coating area (volume / thickness):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{coat}} \;=\; \frac{V_{\text{solid}}}{\text{DFT}_1} \;=\; \frac{0.1375}{125 \times 10^{-6}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical evaluation:**

$$
A_{\text{coat}} \;=\; \frac{0.1375}{0.000125} \;=\; 1{,}100 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Total DFT for 3-coat system:**

$$
\text{DFT}_{\text{total}} \;=\; 3 \times 125 \;=\; 375 \text{ μm}
$$

**Step 5 — Total paint consumption for $A = 4{,}000 \text{ m}^2$ of underwater hull:**

$$
V_{\text{required}} \;=\; \frac{A \cdot \text{DFT}_{\text{total}}}{\text{SVF}}
$$

$$
V_{\text{required}} \;=\; \frac{4{,}000 \times 375 \times 10^{-6}}{0.55}
$$

$$
V_{\text{required}} \;=\; \frac{1.5}{0.55} \;\approx\; 2.727 \text{ m}^3 \;\approx\; 2{,}727 \text{ L}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Pipe Family Reduction Ratio

**Given:**
- A vessel's piping BOM contains $N_{\text{unique}} = 12{,}000$ distinct pipe pieces.
- After Opitz classification, these collapse into $N_{\text{families}} = 480$ manufacturing families.

**Step 1 — Apply family reduction formula:**

$$
\eta_f \;=\; \frac{N_{\text{unique}}}{N_{\text{families}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
\eta_f \;=\; \frac{12{,}000}{480}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$
\eta_f \;=\; 25
$$

**Interpretation:** Each manufacturing family represents **25 pipe pieces on average** — a 96% reduction in production complexity. This enables NC family-programming, common fixturing, and batch-testing.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Zone Construction vs. Outfitting Progression

| Construction Stage | Outfitting Intensity | Painting Done? | Crane Required (t) |
|--------------------|---------------------|---------------|-------------------|
| Steel cutting/plate | Very Low | No | 0 |
| Panel fabrication | Low (Pre-eq.) | No | 10–30 |
| Curved-block assembly | Medium (Pre-eq.) | No | 50–100 |
| Hull-block (straight) | High | **YES (primer)** | 100–200 |
| Mega-block | Very High | **YES (full)** | 200–400 |
| **Zone assembly** | **Highest** | **Touch-up** | **400–1,200** |
| Erection on berth | Final connections | Final coats | 600–1,200 |
| Outfitting afloat | Commissioning only | Trim | Floating |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Zone Construction — Master Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[GA Drawing and Zone Planning] --> A2[Steel Plate Blasting and Cutting]
    A2 --> A3[Panel Assembly Line]
    A3 --> A4[Curved Block Welding]
    A4 --> A5[Block Stage Outfitting Pre-eq]
    A5 --> A6[Surface Prep Sa 2.5 Blasting]
    A6 --> A7[Primer and Anti-corrosive Painting]
    A7 --> A8[Mega Block Joining into Zone]
    A8 --> A9[Zone Stage Outfitting Engines and Pipes]
    A9 --> A10[Final Painting and Insulation]
    A10 --> A11[Zone Lifting 600t Crane]
    A11 --> A12[Erection on Building Dock]
    A12 --> A13[Dock Outfitting Final Connection]
    A13 --> A14[Launch and Sea Trials]
```

### 4.2 Outfitting Hierarchy (Five-Stage Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1 Pre-outfitting on Plate] --> S2[Stage 2 Unit Outfitting]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3 Curved Block Outfitting]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4 Hull Block Outfitting]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5 Zone Outfitting]
    S5 --> S6[Stage 6 Dock Outfitting]
    S6 --> S7[Stage 7 Afloat Commissioning]
```

### 4.3 Pipe Piece Family Manufacturing — GT Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Pipe Piece Inventory BOM 12000 pieces] --> B2[Geometric Feature Extraction]
    B2 --> B3{Opitz 9 Digit Code Assignment}
    B3 --> B4[Sort into Manufacturing Families]
    B4 --> B5[Family Cell Setup Common Fixture]
    B5 --> B6[NC Family Programming]
    B6 --> B7[Batch Cutting Beveling Welding]
    B7 --> B8[Hydrostatic Pressure Test]
    B8 --> B9[Family Kitting to Zone]
```

### 4.4 Block Painting Process Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Steel Surface Arrives at Block] --> C2[Degreasing and Pre-cleaning]
    C2 --> C3[Abrasive Blasting ISO 8501 Sa 2.5]
    C3 --> C4[Dust Removal and Inspection]
    C4 --> C5[Stripe Coat on Edges]
    C5 --> C6[Primer Spray 50 to 75 um]
    C6 --> C7[Intermediate Epoxy 125 to 150 um]
    C7 --> C8[Anti-fouling or Top Coat 100 to 150 um]
    C8 --> C9[DFT Verification and Holiday Detection]
    C9 --> C10[Block Ready for Zone Assembly]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the Zone Construction Method for ship hulls. Why is it preferred over the older berth-method construction?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
*(1 mark)* Zone Construction Method is a shipbuilding strategy in which the hull is divided into **3 to 5 independent zones**, with each zone being fully fabricated, outfitted, and painted at ground level before final erection on the building dock.  
*(1 mark)* It is preferred because it enables **parallel production of multiple zones**, maximizes work content at ground level, reduces dependence on weather, and dramatically improves safety and quality.  
*(1 mark)* It achieves an outfitting density ratio (ODR) of 60–70% compared to only 10–20% in the berth method, thereby shortening total shipbuilding cycle time by 30–40%.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is meant by "Pipe Piece Family Manufacturing" in shipbuilding? Name any two classification systems used.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
*(1 mark)* Pipe Piece Family Manufacturing is the application of **Group Technology (GT)** to shipyard piping, where thousands of unique pipe pieces are classified into a small number of families based on geometric similarity and manufacturing similarity.  
*(1 mark)* This allows common fixturing, common NC programs, and batch processing within each family.  
*(1 mark)* Two classification systems are: **(i) Opitz 9-Digit Code**, and **(ii) MICLASS** (also acceptable: DCLASS, KK-3).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]*

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **Zone Construction Method** for hull blocks. With the help of a flow diagram, describe each stage from plate cutting to zone erection. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Stage 1 — Zone Planning:** The ship's GA drawing is analyzed and the hull is divided into zones — typically *Forward, Midship, Engine Room, and Stern* — at transverse bulkheads or low-stress transverse planes. The boundaries are chosen to align with structural reinforcement. **(1 Mark)**

**Stage 2 — Steel Preparation:** Steel plates are blasted, cut, and shaped using NC cutting machines. Pre-outfitting such as pipe sleeves, brackets, and grounding points is welded onto plates at this stage. **(1 Mark)**

**Stage 3 — Panel and Unit Fabrication:** Plates are welded into flat panels on sub-assembly lines. Units (small 3D sub-assemblies) are produced. **(1 Mark)**

**Stage 4 — Curved and Straight Block Fabrication:** Units are joined into curved and straight blocks on the block assembly line. Block-level outfitting (cable trays, small valves, pipe hangers) is performed. **(1 Mark)**

**Stage 5 — Block Painting:** After ISO 8501 Sa 2.5 blasting, primer, anti-corrosive epoxy, and tie coats are applied. This is the **single largest contributor** to reducing dock-time. **(1 Mark)**

**Stage 6 — Mega-Block and Zone Joining:** Blocks are welded into mega-blocks; mega-blocks are joined to form the complete zone on the ground. **(1 Mark)**

**Stage 7 — Zone Outfitting & Final Painting:** Main engines, pumps, generators, full pipe systems, HVAC, and switchboards are installed and tested. Final coats and insulation are applied. **(0.5 Mark)**

**Stage 8 — Erection:** The completed zone (often weighing 400–1,200 tonnes) is lifted by a gantry crane onto the building dock and welded to the adjacent zone. **(0.5 Mark)**

*(Flow diagram must be drawn — full marks awarded for clean directional flowchart with 6+ stages.)*

---

**Q. (b)** Describe the **five stages of outfitting** in modern shipbuilding. Explain how the Outfitting Density Ratio (ODR) is calculated and state its typical modern value. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The five stages are: **(2 Marks)**
1. **Pre-outfitting** — done on plates/sections before assembly.
2. **Unit outfitting** — done on small 3D units.
3. **Block outfitting** — done on assembled curved/straight blocks.
4. **Zone outfitting** — done after zone assembly, includes main machinery.
5. **Dock outfitting** — final connections on the building dock.

**ODR Formula:** **(2 Marks)**

$$
\text{ODR} \;=\; \frac{\text{Outfitting Man-Hours at Block Stage}}{\text{Total Outfitting Man-Hours}}
$$

**Numerical Example:** **(2 Marks)**
For a total outfitting content of 240,000 MH and block-stage outfitting of 156,000 MH:

$$
\text{ODR} \;=\; \frac{156{,}000}{240{,}000} \;=\; 0.65
$$

**Typical Modern Value:** **0.55–0.70**, indicating 55–70% of outfitting is completed before erection.

**Significance:** A high ODR directly reduces berth occupancy time and accelerates delivery. **(1 Mark)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**Q. (a)** Explain the concept of **Pipe Piece Family Manufacturing** using Group Technology. With reference to the **Opitz 9-digit coding system**, describe how pipe pieces are classified. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept of Pipe Piece Family Manufacturing (3 Marks):**
Pipe piece family manufacturing applies **Group Technology (GT)** to shipyard pipe production. Thousands of unique pipe pieces (spools) are classified into a manageable set of families using similarity in geometry, material, and manufacturing route. Each family is then processed through a common *family-cell* production line — common fixturing, common NC programs, common welding parameters, and common pressure testing. This reduces setup time, improves repeatability, and enables automation.

**Opitz 9-Digit Code (4 Marks):**
The Opitz code consists of **5 form digits + 4 supplementary digits**:

| Digits $D_1$ to $D_5$ | Represent geometric form (rotational vs. prismatic, internal cavity, plane machining, holes, threads). |
| Digits $D_6$ to $D_9$ | Represent supplementary features (material, raw stock shape, tolerance class, surface finish). |

**Example:** A straight steel pipe piece with flanged ends might code as: **0 0 0 0 0  1 1 2 3**, where the first five digits indicate a simple rotational hollow form, $D_6 = 1$ (steel), $D_7 = 1$ (bar stock), $D_8 = 2$ (medium tolerance), $D_9 = 3$ (standard surface finish).

Pieces sharing the first 3–4 digits are grouped into the same family for manufacturing.

---

**Q. (b)** Discuss the **painting procedure** adopted at the block stage in zone construction. List the typical coating system for the underwater hull and state the role of each layer. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Painting Procedure (3 Marks):**
1. **Surface Preparation** — abrasive blasting to ISO 8501 Sa 2.5 ("commercial blast") standard; surface roughness 50–85 μm.
2. **Dust Removal & Inspection** — vacuum cleaning; visual check for rust, oil, salts.
3. **Stripe Coating** — brush-applied on edges, weld seams, and corners to ensure film build.
4. **Primer Spray** — epoxy zinc-rich primer (50–75 μm DFT).
5. **Intermediate Coats** — anti-corrosive epoxy (125–150 μm each, typically 2 coats).
6. **Anti-fouling Coat** — self-polishing copolymer (SPC) for underwater hull (100–150 μm DFT).
7. **Inspection** — DFT verification with magnetic gauge; holiday detection (spark test) at 67.5 V per μm.

**Underwater Hull Coating System (3 Marks):**

| Layer | DFT (μm) | Role |
|-------|----------|------|
| Primer (Epoxy Zn-rich) | 50–75 | Cathodic / sacrificial protection |
| Anti-corrosive Epoxy Tie | 125–150 | Sealing primer, adhesion |
| Anti-corrosive Epoxy Build | 125–150 | Total corrosion barrier |
| Anti-fouling (SPC) | 100–150 | Biocide release against marine growth |
| **Total** | **400–525** | Full system |

**Advantages of block-stage painting (1 Mark):**
- Easier access → better quality.
- Weather-independent → controlled shop environment.
- Reduces dock-time → earlier delivery.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:**
> - Students **often confuse** "Pre-outfitting" with "Pre-erection outfitting." Pre-outfitting = on steel plates/sections; Pre-erection outfitting = on blocks just before lifting.
> - Many forget that **zone construction is NOT the same as unit construction.** Units are *sub-assemblies*; zones are *near-complete hull sections*.
> - In Opitz code questions, students sometimes write **only 5 digits** — the examiner expects **9 digits** unless specified.
> - When calculating ODR, **always divide block MH by TOTAL MH** (not by remaining MH). Common mistake!
> - For DFT/WFT conversion, students often forget the **Solids Volume Fraction (SVF)** in the denominator — leading to overestimated coverage.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Zone Construction** = Partition hull into 3–5 zones, complete outfitting and painting at ground level, then erect.
- The **five outfitting stages** are: Pre → Unit → Block → Zone → Dock (remember "PUNCH-B" or simply list them in order).
- **Block-stage painting** is performed at ISO 8501 **Sa 2.5** standard.
- **ODR (Outfitting Density Ratio)** is the single most important KPI of modern shipbuilding productivity.
- **Total DFT** of an underwater hull coating system is typically **400–525 μm** across 4 coats.
- **Pipe Piece Family Manufacturing** uses **Group Technology (GT)** — most common classifier is the **Opitz 9-digit code**.
- A modern ship contains **40,000–80,000 pipe pieces** that collapse into **40–60 manufacturing families** via GT.
- **Zone cycle time** is governed by the **slowest block**, not the sum.
- **Block tonnage** is computed from $\rho_{\text{steel}} \cdot V \cdot g$ with $\rho_{\text{steel}} = 7.85 \text{ t/m}^3$.
- **Family reduction ratio** $\eta_f = N_{\text{unique}} / N_{\text{families}}$ — a typical value is **20–30**.
- **Berth occupancy time** = Erection time + Dock-outfitting time — directly impacts delivery date.
- **WFT to DFT**: $\text{WFT} = \text{DFT} / \text{SVF}$.
- **Always remember:** the goal of zone construction is to push work **downstream in space but upstream in time** — i.e., done earlier on land, not later on the berth.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Material handling, storage and transport systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST631 - SHIP PRODUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Material handling, storage and transport systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Material Handling** in a shipyard refers to the systematic movement, protection, storage, and control of all raw materials, semi-finished sub-assemblies, and finished components throughout the ship production cycle — from receipt of mill-scale steel plates at the stockyard to the final launching of the vessel.

**Storage Systems** refer to the engineered infrastructure (stockyards, covered warehouses, racks, bins, and specialized enclosures) used to receive, classify, preserve, and issue materials such that **traceability**, **preservation of mechanical properties**, and **operational throughput** are maintained.

**Transport Systems** denote the in-plant and inter-plant conveyance networks — road, rail, waterway, and specialized self-propelled modular transporters (SPMTs) — used to physically relocate shipbuilding materials between workstations, between blocks, and ultimately to the launching basin or dry dock.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Mapping (CO1 / K1–K3):**
> This topic directly addresses **Course Outcome 1**: *"Understand the fundamental stages and modern workflow of ship production."* Expect definition-based short questions (3 marks) and applied reasoning questions (14 marks) on yard layout and handling philosophy.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Assembly Line" Intuition

Imagine a large restaurant kitchen preparing a banquet for **1,000 guests**:

- **Raw ingredients** (vegetables, meat, spices) arrive at a **receiving dock** — this is the **stockyard** of a shipyard.
- A **cold storage** holds perishables — analogous to **preserved steel plates** under controlled atmosphere.
- A **prep station** (cutting vegetables) is the **steel cutting workshop**.
- **Runners** carry trays to the cooking line — these are the **overhead cranes and forklift fleets**.
- Finally, a **plated dish** goes out on a cart — analogous to a **block assembly moved by SPMT** to the dry dock.

In the same way, a shipyard must orchestrate the seamless flow of materials — sometimes **hundreds of tons per lift** — through multiple workstations without damaging the workpiece, without losing traceability, and without ever stopping the production line.

## 1.3 The Four Pillars of Material Flow in Ship Production

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Terminology — Memorize These Four Categories:**
>
> 1. **Bulk Materials** — steel plates (6–50 mm), sections, bars, pipes.
> 2. **Sub-Assemblies** — panel lines, curved shells, brackets.
> 3. **Outfit Items** — engines, pumps, valves, cables, furniture.
> 4. **Hazardous / Controlled Stock** — paints, welding consumables, gases, oils.

## 1.4 The Strategic Importance of Handling in Shipbuilding

Ship production differs fundamentally from car or aircraft production because:

| Parameter | Car / Aircraft | Ship |
|---|---|---|
| Unit weight of workpiece | kg – tonnes | tonnes – **hundreds of tonnes** |
| Production volume | millions / year | **1–10 units / year** |
| Yard footprint | 10–50 acres | **100–1,000 acres** |
| Working environment | enclosed factory | mostly **open-air stockyards** |
| Material handling philosophy | conveyor-dominant | **crane-dominant** |

This makes material handling a **direct determinant of shipbuilding productivity (tonnage/man-hour)** rather than a peripheral support function.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Material Flow Hierarchy in a Modern Shipyard

A typical integrated shipyard organizes material flow into **six hierarchical zones**, each governed by its own handling philosophy:

1. **Stockyard Zone (Receiving → Issue)**
2. **Steel Processing Zone (Cutting → Blasting → Priming)**
3. **Panel & Sub-Assembly Zone**
4. **Block Assembly Zone**
5. **Erection / Outfitting Zone**
6. **Launching & Delivery Zone**

The logical flow is:

> Raw Steel → Cutting → Flat Panel → Curved Panel → 3-D Sub-Assembly → Block → Mega-Block → Ship

Each transition represents a **unit weight escalation** (e.g., 5 t plate → 50 t panel → 500 t block → 50,000 t ship).

## 2.2 Detailed Equipment Classification

### 2.2.1 Cranes — The Backbone of Shipyard Handling

Cranes dominate shipyard handling because of their ability to handle **irregularly shaped, very heavy, and large-volume** loads.

**(a) Overhead (EOT) Cranes** — installed in workshops; capacity typically **5 t to 80 t**; used for steel processing and panel fabrication halls.

**(b) Gantry Cranes** — straddling the work area; capacities **50 t to 1,500 t**; used in stockyards and block fabrication halls.

**(c) Goliath Cranes** — outdoor gantries that straddle stockyard stacks; often rail-mounted.

**(d) Jib Cranes** — fixed or wall-mounted; used for repetitive localized lifting (<10 t).

**(e) Tower Cranes** — vertical reach for high erection; often seen in modular construction shipyards.

**(f) Mobile Cranes (Hydraulic / Crawler)** — flexible, road-runnable; capacities up to **750 t single lift** and higher when tandem-lifted.

**(g) Floating Cranes / Sheerlegs** — used for transport from jetty to graving dock; capacities up to **10,000 t** in modern yards.

### 2.2.2 Transport Equipment

- **Self-Propelled Modular Transporters (SPMTs)** — hydraulic multi-axle wheeled platforms; **payload 40 t per axle line**; can be combined into large arrays to move **blocks of 5,000–20,000 t**.
- **Heavy-Duty Trailers** — for moving assemblies between halls.
- **Forklifts** — up to 25 t capacity for unit loads of outfit items.
- **Battery-Powered Trolleys** — for small-parts movement.
- **Conveyors & Rollers** — for plate movement in cutting halls.

### 2.2.3 Storage Infrastructure

- **Open Stockyards** — for corrosion-tolerant plates; with sleepers, pads, and drainage.
- **Covered Warehouses** — for primed plates, profiles, and small parts.
- **Rack Systems** — heavy-duty cantilever racks for long sections and pipes.
- **Hazardous Goods Stores** — segregated, fire-rated, with bunding for oil and paint.
- **Climate-Controlled Stores** — for electronics, instruments, and rubber goods.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **standard engineering formulas** expected to be applied (not memorized) in ship-production problems.

| # | Formula / Relation | Variables | Typical Use in Shipyard |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W = m \cdot g$ | W = weight (N), m = mass (kg), g = 9.81 m/s² | Load calculation for crane selection |
| 2 | $L_{SWL} \geq W_{max} \cdot n_{sf}$ | L_SWL = Safe Working Load, n_sf = safety factor (typically 1.25–1.5) | Crane SWL selection |
| 3 | $n_{axles} = \dfrac{W_{block}}{P_{axle}}$ | n_axles = number of SPMT axles, P_axle = payload per axle | SPMT fleet sizing |
| 4 | $C_{h} = \dfrac{N_{lifts}}{T_{cycle}}$ | C_h = handling capacity, N = lifts/hr, T = cycle time | Yard throughput analysis |
| 5 | $T_{cycle} = T_{hoist} + T_{traverse} + T_{swing} + T_{attach} + T_{detach}$ | Sum of crane motion times | Crane productivity |
| 6 | $A_{yard} = \dfrac{M_{inv}}{h_{s} \cdot \rho_{s}}$ | A_yard = stockyard area, M_inv = inventory (t), h_s = stack height, ρ_s = stack density | Stockyard sizing |
| 7 | $\eta_{handling} = \dfrac{W_{useful}}{W_{input}} \times 100\%$ | η = handling efficiency, W = work | Productivity metric |
| 8 | $Q_{crane} = \dfrac{W_{avg}}{T_{cycle}}$ | Q_crane = crane throughput (t/hr) | Single-crane productivity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** For 14-mark questions, examiners expect students to **state assumptions**, **substitute numerical values**, and **box the final answer with correct units**. Even qualitative questions like "Discuss the suitability of gantry cranes in stockyards" carry mark allocations for: (i) definition, (ii) advantages, (iii) limitations, (iv) sketch/description, (v) conclusion.

## 2.4 Selection Logic — Which Equipment for Which Task?

```
Lift Weight < 5 t      → Forklift / Jib Crane
Lift Weight 5–80 t     → EOT Crane / Mobile Crane
Lift Weight 80–500 t   → Gantry / Goliath Crane
Lift Weight > 500 t    → Floating Crane / Tandem Lift / SPMT Array
```

## 2.5 Material Storage Philosophy — FIFO and JIT in a Shipyard

- **FIFO (First-In-First-Out):** Mandatory for **outfit items with shelf life** (electronics, chemicals).
- **JIT (Just-In-Time):** Increasingly adopted for **outfitting parts** to reduce inventory carrying cost.
- **Kanban-driven requisition:** Links production schedule directly to material issue.
- **WIP Buffer Stocks:** Limited, controlled blocks of sub-assemblies between zones.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Industrial Application:**
> In modern Korean mega-yards (Hyundai HI, Samsung HI, Daewoo), the integration of **automated overhead cranes, RFID-tagged material tracking, and SPMT-driven block transport** has reduced block erection cycle time from ~14 days (1980s) to ~3 days (2020s). This is **directly attributable to material-handling efficiency**, not welding speed.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem — Crane Throughput Estimation

> **Problem (Model Question Style):**
> A shipyard gantry crane is used to load steel plates (mass per plate = **8 tonnes**) onto a cutting-shop trolley. The crane has the following cycle parameters:
> - Hoist time: $T_{hoist} = 30$ s
> - Traverse time: $T_{traverse} = 25$ s
> - Slew time: $T_{swing} = 15$ s
> - Attach (sling) time: $T_{attach} = 60$ s
> - Detach (unsling) time: $T_{detach} = 45$ s
>
> **(a)** Calculate the cycle time $T_{cycle}$.
> **(b)** Calculate the hourly throughput $Q_{crane}$ in tonnes/hour.
> **(c)** If the yard requires a daily throughput of **2,400 tonnes** with 2-shift operation (8 hrs/shift), how many such cranes are needed?

### 3.1.1 Solution — Part (a): Cycle Time

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle} &= T_{hoist} + T_{traverse} + T_{swing} + T_{attach} + T_{detach} \\
T_{cycle} &= 30 + 25 + 15 + 60 + 45 \\
T_{cycle} &= 175 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Cycle time is the **sum of all sequential operations** for one full pick-and-place cycle. Even non-movement periods (slinging) must be included because the crane is occupied during sling work.

### 3.1.2 Solution — Part (b): Hourly Throughput

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{lifts} &= \frac{3600}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{3600}{175} = 20.57 \text{ lifts/hr} \\
Q_{crane} &= N_{lifts} \times W_{avg} \\
Q_{crane} &= 20.57 \times 8 \\
Q_{crane} &= 164.57 \text{ t/hr} \quad \boxed{Q_{crane} \approx 164.6 \text{ t/hr}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Solution — Part (c): Number of Cranes Required

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Daily capacity of 1 crane} &= 164.57 \times 16 = 2633 \text{ t/day} \\
\text{Daily requirement} &= 2400 \text{ t/day} \\
N_{cranes} &= \frac{2400}{2633} = 0.91
\end{aligned}
$$

> Since we cannot deploy **0.91 of a crane**, we round up: **N_cranes = 1 crane** (with ~9% spare capacity for breakdowns and non-productive time).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Exam Mistake:** Students frequently **forget to multiply by 2 shifts** (i.e., 16 hrs, not 8 hrs) when the problem says "2-shift operation." Re-read the problem statement before substituting.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — SPMT Fleet Sizing

> **Problem:**
> A shipyard must transport a **2,400-tonne double-bottom block** from the assembly hall to the graving dock using SPMTs with **40 t per axle line** payload capacity. How many axle lines are required? Assume a **safety factor of 1.25**.

### Solution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{axle} &= 40 \text{ t} \\
W_{block} &= 2400 \text{ t} \\
W_{design} &= W_{block} \times n_{sf} = 2400 \times 1.25 = 3000 \text{ t} \\
n_{axles} &= \frac{W_{design}}{P_{axle}} = \frac{3000}{40} \\
n_{axles} &= 75 \text{ axle lines} \quad \boxed{n_{axles} = 75}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** A safety factor accounts for **load asymmetry, dynamic effects during braking, and uneven weight distribution**. KTU examiners will **deduct 1–2 marks** if you ignore the safety factor.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Equipment Selection

| Application / Load Type | Recommended Handling System | Recommended Storage | Why? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Steel plates (8–20 t) | Gantry / Goliath crane + magnet/spreader beam | Open stockyard with sleepers | High lifting height, magnetic gripping, low-cost storage |
| Long sections (I-beams, T-bars) | Cantilever rack + overhead crane | Cantilever rack outdoor | Easy picking of long items |
| Pipes & small bars | Pallet + forklift | Racked warehouse | Volume efficiency |
| Painted panels | Overhead crane with soft sling | Indoor rack hall | Prevent paint damage, weather protection |
| Block assembly (300–800 t) | SPMT + Goliath | Block field with hard standing | Controlled movement, no shock |
| Mega-block (>1000 t) | SPMT array + strand jacks | Skid track to dock | Only feasible transport method |
| Outfit machinery (engines) | Mobile crane + heavy-duty trailer | Indoor warehouse on rails | Weather protection, accuracy |
| Hazardous chemicals | Manual trolley in dedicated path | Fire-rated segregated store | Safety compliance |

## 3.4 Storage Area Sizing (Derivation)

Given a monthly steel throughput of **M_t tonnes** and an **average inventory dwell time of D days**, with a **stack density of ρ_s (t/m²)** and an **average stack height of h_s (m)**, the required stockyard area is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{inv} &= M_{t} \times \frac{D}{30} \\
A_{yard} &= \frac{M_{inv}}{h_{s} \cdot \rho_{s}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Illustration:**

Given: $M_t = 30{,}000$ t/month, $D = 20$ days, $\rho_s = 4$ t/m², $h_s = 1.5$ m

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{inv} &= 30000 \times \frac{20}{30} = 20000 \text{ t} \\
A_{yard} &= \frac{20000}{1.5 \times 4} = \frac{20000}{6} \\
A_{yard} &= 3333.33 \text{ m}^2 \quad \boxed{A_{yard} \approx 3333 \text{ m}^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The **average inventory** is proportional to **throughput × dwell time**. The **land footprint** is inversely proportional to **stack density and stack height** — which is why yards invest in **gantry cranes with high hook height** to allow taller stacking.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Material Flow Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MILL SUPPLIER] --> B[RECEIVING DOCK]
    B --> C[QUALITY INSPECTION BAY]
    C --> D[STOCKYARD OPEN STORAGE]
    C --> E[COVERED WAREHOUSE]
    C --> F[HAZARDOUS GOODS STORE]
    D --> G[STEEL CUTTING SHOP]
    E --> G
    G --> H[SHOT BLASTING AND PRIMING]
    H --> I[PANEL FABRICATION LINE]
    I --> J[SUB ASSEMBLY SHOP]
    J --> K[BLOCK ASSEMBLY HALL]
    K --> L[BLOCK FIELD STORAGE]
    L --> M[ERECTION BERTH OR DRY DOCK]
    M --> N[OUTFITTING QUAY]
    N --> O[SEA TRIALS AND DELIVERY]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of Crane Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LOAD[LIFT WEIGHT W] --> Q1{W less than 5 t}
    Q1 -- YES --> FK[FORKLIFT OR JIB CRANE]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{W between 5 and 80 t}
    Q2 -- YES --> EOT[EOT CRANE OR MOBILE CRANE]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{W between 80 and 500 t}
    Q3 -- YES --> GAN[GANTRY OR GOLIATH CRANE]
    Q3 -- NO --> Q4{W greater than 500 t}
    Q4 -- YES --> SPMT[SPMT ARRAY OR FLOATING CRANE]
```

## 4.3 Storage and Transport Integration Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SY[OPEN STOCKYARD] -->|GOLIATH CRANE| CUT[STEEL CUTTING HALL]
    CUT -->|EOT CRANE| BLAST[SHOT BLAST AND PRIME SHOP]
    BLAST -->|EOT CRANE| PANEL[PANEL LINE]
    PANEL -->|GOLIATH CRANE| SUB[SUB ASSEMBLY]
    SUB -->|GANTRY CRANE| BLOCK[BLOCK ASSEMBLY HALL]
    BLOCK -->|GOLIATH CRANE| BF[BLOCK FIELD]
    BF -->|SPMT| DOCK[GRAVING DOCK OR BERTH]
    DOCK -->|MOBILE CRANE| OUT[OUTFITTING]
    OUT -->|TOW| DELIV[SEA TRIAL DELIVERY]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Storage Type Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[MATERIAL INCOMING] --> S1{CORROSION SENSITIVE}
    S1 -- YES --> S2[COVERED WAREHOUSE]
    S1 -- NO --> S3{HIGH VALUE OR TRACEABLE}
    S3 -- YES --> S4[RACKED STORAGE WITH RFID]
    S3 -- NO --> S5{HAZARDOUS CLASS}
    S5 -- YES --> S6[FIRE RATED SEGREGATED STORE]
    S5 -- NO --> S7[OPEN STOCKYARD ON SLEEPERS]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**Define material handling in the context of ship production. List any four types of material handling equipment used in a modern shipyard.**

**Model Answer (≈120 words):**

Material handling in ship production refers to the **systematic movement, storage, and control of all materials and sub-assemblies** through the various stages of shipbuilding — from receipt of raw steel to delivery of the finished vessel.

Four types of material handling equipment commonly used in modern shipyards are:

1. **Overhead (EOT) Cranes** — for indoor workshops.
2. **Gantry / Goliath Cranes** — for stockyards and block fields.
3. **Self-Propelled Modular Transporters (SPMTs)** — for moving mega-blocks.
4. **Forklifts and Battery Trolleys** — for outfit items and small parts.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do **not** write vague answers like "cranes and forklifts are used." Always **name the specific type** (e.g., Goliath, EOT, SPMT) and **state its function**. Generic answers cap your mark at 2/3.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]
**Differentiate between an open stockyard and a covered warehouse in a shipyard. When is each preferred?**

**Model Answer (≈120 words):**

| Feature | Open Stockyard | Covered Warehouse |
|---|---|---|
| Location | Outdoor, weather exposed | Indoor, enclosed |
| Materials | Steel plates, sections, bars | Primed panels, outfit items, electronics |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Preservation | Limited | High |
| Capacity | Very high | Limited |

**Open stockyards** are preferred for **mill-scale and corrosion-tolerant bulk steel**. **Covered warehouses** are preferred for **primed plates, painted panels, electronics, and rubber goods** that are sensitive to humidity and sunlight.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **classification of cranes** used in a shipyard. Explain their suitability for different material handling operations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shipyard requires to transport a **1,800-tonne engine room block** using SPMTs with **30 t per axle line** capacity. Calculate the number of axle lines required, applying a **safety factor of 1.25**. If 4 SPMT modules each having **6 axle lines** are available, how many such modules are required? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Classification of Cranes in Shipyards:**

**(i) Overhead EOT Cranes:** Installed on runway beams inside workshops; capacities **5–80 t**; used in cutting halls, blasting shops, and panel lines.

**(ii) Goliath / Gantry Cranes:** Outdoor rail-mounted gantries straddling stockyards and block fields; capacities **50–1,500 t**; ideal for moving large plates and blocks.

**(iii) Jib Cranes:** Wall-mounted or pillar-mounted; small radius; for repetitive sub-assembly lifting.

**(iv) Tower Cranes:** Used in modular construction yards where blocks are stacked vertically.

**(v) Mobile Hydraulic Cranes:** Road-runnable; flexible but limited ground-bearing.

**(vi) Crawler Cranes:** Track-mounted; suitable for soft ground and heavy lifts.

**(vii) Floating Cranes / Sheerlegs:** Pontoon-mounted; used for offloading from sea transport and for graving-dock work; very high capacity (up to 10,000 t).

**Suitability:**

- For **plate movement**: Goliath crane with magnet.
- For **panel fabrication**: EOT crane.
- For **block transport**: SPMT array + Goliath.
- For **erection**: Tower or crawler crane.
- For **dock work**: Floating crane.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{block} &= 1800 \text{ t} \\
n_{sf} &= 1.25 \\
W_{design} &= 1800 \times 1.25 = 2250 \text{ t} \\
P_{axle} &= 30 \text{ t} \\
n_{axles} &= \frac{W_{design}}{P_{axle}} = \frac{2250}{30} = 75 \text{ axle lines}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Axles per module} &= 6 \\
\text{Modules required} &= \frac{75}{6} = 12.5 \rightarrow \boxed{13 \text{ modules}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Always **round up** the final number (cannot have half a module). Also, write "**design load including safety factor**" — students often forget this and lose 2 marks.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **layout philosophy of a modern integrated shipyard** with respect to material flow. Sketch a **functional block diagram** showing the relationship between stockyard, cutting shop, panel line, block assembly hall, and graving dock. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A gantry crane handles **10-tonne plates**. The cycle consists of: hoist = 25 s, traverse = 30 s, slew = 20 s, attach = 50 s, detach = 40 s. Calculate: (i) cycle time, (ii) lifts per hour, (iii) hourly throughput, and (iv) number of cranes required to move **3,000 tonnes/day** with **single-shift 8-hour operation**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Layout Philosophy:** A modern shipyard is laid out on the **U-flow** or **T-flow principle** to ensure unidirectional material movement with **minimum backtracking**.

- **Stockyard** is positioned near the **receiving jetty**.
- **Cutting shop** is adjacent to the stockyard to minimize plate travel.
- **Panel line** follows the cutting shop.
- **Block assembly hall** is downstream of the panel line.
- **Graving dock / berth** is at the downstream end of the yard.

The principle is: **minimize material travel distance, avoid crossing flows, and use gravity-fed layout where possible.**

*(Functional block diagram — draw the Mermaid Section 4.3 as the answer sketch.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**(i) Cycle Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle} &= 25 + 30 + 20 + 50 + 40 \\
T_{cycle} &= 165 \text{ s} \quad \boxed{T_{cycle} = 165 \text{ s}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Lifts per Hour:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{lifts} &= \frac{3600}{165} = 21.82 \text{ lifts/hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Hourly Throughput:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{crane} &= 21.82 \times 10 = 218.2 \text{ t/hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Number of Cranes:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Daily per crane} &= 218.2 \times 8 = 1745.6 \text{ t/day} \\
N_{cranes} &= \frac{3000}{1745.6} = 1.72 \rightarrow \boxed{2 \text{ cranes}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** When the answer is a **fractional crane**, always **round up to the next whole number**. Also, do **not** confuse "**lifts/hour**" with "**tonnes/hour**" — they are different units. Marks are lost if you skip unit declarations.

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Read This Twice Before the Exam:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Material handling = movement + storage + control of all materials through ship production stages.
- ✅ **Four Material Categories:** Bulk steel, sub-assemblies, outfit items, hazardous stock.
- ✅ **Crane Hierarchy:** Jib < EOT < Mobile < Goliath < Tower < Floating (by typical capacity).
- ✅ **SPMT Payload:** ≈ 40 t per axle line; total = axle lines × per-axle capacity × 1/n_sf.
- ✅ **Stockyard Area Formula:** $A_{yard} = M_{inv} / (h_s \cdot \rho_s)$.
- ✅ **Crane Throughput:** $Q_{crane} = W_{avg} \times (3600 / T_{cycle})$.
- ✅ **Storage Philosophy:** FIFO for perishables / electronics, JIT for outfit items, segregated storage for hazardous goods.
- ✅ **Yard Layout:** Unidirectional flow, minimize backtracking, stockyard at receiving end, dock at delivery end.
- ✅ **Safety Factor:** Always include in SPMT and crane SWL calculations (typical 1.25–1.5).
- ✅ **Round Up Rule:** Never report fractional cranes / SPMTs — round up to the next integer.
- ✅ **Modern Trends:** RFID tracking, automated EOTs, mega-block strategy, SPMT-driven erection, Industry 4.0 yard digitalization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Scheme Examiner Note:** For any 14-mark question, structure your answer as: **(i) opening definition, (ii) classification / enumeration, (iii) detailed explanation with engineering reasoning, (iv) numerical or diagrammatic illustration, (v) conclusion or industrial relevance**. This structure alone guarantees **80%+ marks** even if some sub-points are partially recalled.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Material preparation: Straightening of plates, sand blasting, painting, lofting, nesting
Production management: Pre-production, production and post production stages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST631 - SHIP PRODUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Production management: Pre-production, production and post production stages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Production Management in Shipbuilding** is the systematic planning, coordination, execution, and control of all activities involved in converting a ship design (contract specification) into a fully operational vessel, delivered to the client within stipulated time, cost, and quality constraints. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, it is broadly classified into three chronological macro-phases:

1. **Pre-Production Stage (Stage 0 / Engineering Phase):** All activities that occur *before* the first steel is cut. This includes design finalization, contract administration, work planning, materials procurement, process engineering, and shop-floor preparation.
2. **Production Stage (Stage 1 / 2 / 3 — Construction Phase):** The physical realization of the ship. This encompasses steel stock cutting, sub-assembly, block fabrication, grand block erection on the berth, launching, and outfitting.
3. **Post-Production Stage (Stage 4 / 5 / 6 — Commissioning & Delivery Phase):** All activities after the ship leaves the building berth until final handover. It includes quay outfitting, dock trials, sea trials, snag rectification, classification society survey, and warranty support.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note (PENST631 Module 2):**
> Ship production management is a **process-oriented discipline** where each stage acts as a *predecessor* and *successor* to the adjacent stage. The interface between Pre-Production and Production is the **Steel Cutting Date (SCD)**. The interface between Production and Post-Production is the **Launching Date (LD)**. The terminal milestone of Post-Production is the **Delivery Date (DD)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Building a Custom House"**
> Imagine you hire an architect to build a custom house:
> - **Pre-Production** = You meet the architect, finalize blueprints (design), get permits, order bricks/cement/wires (procurement), hire plumbers/electricians (manpower planning), and prepare the site (workshop readiness). *Nothing is built yet, but everything is ready to build.*
> - **Production** = The actual construction — laying the foundation, raising walls, installing the roof, plastering, wiring, plumbing. This is where the "metal hits the metal."
> - **Post-Production** = Final painting, cleaning, inspection by municipal authorities (classification), moving in (delivery), and fixing any leakages during the first monsoon (warranty).
>
> A ship, however, is a "floating house" weighing 50,000+ tonnes, so each of these phases is exponentially more complex and must follow a **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)** rigorously.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield Terms)

The following standard shipbuilding metrics appear frequently in KTU examinations and must be memorized:

- **Steel Cutting Date (SCD)** — First cut on the first plate of the vessel.
- **Keel Laying Date (KLD)** — Date of keel block assembly or equivalent major assembly.
- **Launching Date (LD)** — Date the ship slides into water.
- **Sea Trial Date (STD)** — Date the ship undergoes full-speed and maneuvering trials at sea.
- **Delivery Date (DD)** — Date the vessel is officially handed over to the owner.
- **Standard Man-Hours (SMH)** — Predicted labor hours per ton of steel.
- **Gross Tonnage (GT)** — Volume-based measure of ship's capacity (unitless, but expressed numerically).
- **Compensated Gross Tonnage (CGT)** — Output metric used in shipbuilding productivity (1 GT ≈ 1 CGT for tankers, less for simple vessels).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 STAGE 0 — PRE-PRODUCTION STAGE (Detailed Breakdown)

The pre-production stage is the most planning-intensive phase. Errors here propagate exponentially into production delays. It is sub-divided into the following sub-stages:

#### 2.1.1 Contract Phase
- **Negotiation & Contract Signing:** Terms of build (price, payment schedule, delivery date, penalties for delay, performance guarantees).
- **Contract Design Review:** Examination of the Owner's specification, classification society rules (e.g., IRS, Lloyd's Register, ABS, DNV, BV), and statutory regulations (SOLAS, MARPOL).
- **Production Information Sheet (PIS):** Internal document that defines scantlings, main dimensions, and contractual clauses.

#### 2.1.2 Design & Engineering Phase
- **Basic Design (Conceptual):** Hull form, general arrangement, hydrostatics, stability, powering.
- **Contract Design (Functional):** Detailed to the level required to fix price.
- **Production Design (Working Drawings):** The most critical for the shipyard — every plate, stiffener, pipe, and cable is detailed in 2D drawings or 3D CAD models.
- **Interference Checking:** Performed in 3D CAD (e.g., FORAN, Aveva Marine, Tribon) to detect clashes between systems *before* fabrication.

#### 2.1.3 Work Planning Phase
- **Master Schedule / Production Schedule:** A network (often a **Precedence Diagram** or Gantt chart) that maps the build strategy — e.g., Zone Outfitting vs. Unit Pre-Outfitting vs. Block Outfitting strategies.
- **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS):** Hierarchical decomposition of the entire ship into work packages (e.g., Hull → Blocks → Sub-blocks → Parts).
- **Critical Path Method (CPM) / PERT:** Used to identify the longest sequence of dependent tasks and compute float times.

#### 2.1.4 Materials Procurement Phase
- **Bill of Materials (BOM):** Generated from the production design.
- **Procurement Cycle:** Order placement → Mill rolling → Delivery → Stockyard storage.
- **Long Lead Time (LLT) Items:** Engines, propellers, main switchboards, stern tubes — ordered months in advance.

#### 2.1.5 Process & Production Engineering
- **Nest Plans / Cutting Plans:** Software-driven optimization of plate nesting to minimize steel waste.
- **Welding Procedure Specifications (WPS):** Defines electrode, current, preheat, and travel speed for each joint type.
- **Sequence of Construction Plan:** Determines whether a *sectional*, *block*, or *unit construction* method is used.

#### 2.1.6 Shop Floor Preparation
- Workshop layout, jig calibration, workforce allocation, training, and safety audits.

### 2.2 STAGE 1–3 — PRODUCTION STAGE (Detailed Breakdown)

This is where physical steel becomes a ship. Modern yards use a **block construction** approach, assembling the ship in modular units.

| Sub-Stage | Activity | Workshop/Berth |
|-----------|----------|----------------|
| **Steel Stock Cutting** | Shot blasting, cutting, marking | Stockyard / CNC cutting shop |
| **Sub-Assembly (Panel Line)** | Plates + stiffeners → flat panels | Panel shop |
| **Mini-Block Assembly** | Panels + brackets → 3D mini-blocks | Sub-assembly shop |
| **Block Assembly** | Mini-blocks → full blocks (up to 200–500 t) | Block assembly shop |
| **Grand Block / Unit Assembly** | Multiple blocks → units (with pre-outfitting) | Outfitting berth / dry dock |
| **Erection on Berth** | Units joined on the building berth | Building berth / dry dock |
| **Launching** | Ship enters water | Slipway / floating dock |
| **Berth Outfitting** | Interior fit-out while on berth | Building berth (continued) |
| **Quay Outfitting** | Fit-out after launching alongside quay | Fitting-out quay |

#### 2.2.1 Outfitting Strategies
- **Pre-Outfitting (PEO):** Pipes, cables, equipment installed *before* the block is erected. Maximizes productivity.
- **Block Outfitting (BO):** Done after block assembly but before erection.
- **Unit Outfitting (UEO):** Done at grand-block level.
- **Zone Outfitting (ZEO):** Completed after erection, based on functional zones (e.g., engine room zone, accommodation zone).
- **Pier Outfitting / Quay Outfitting (QEO):** After launching.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern KTU High-Yield Concept — Modern Build Strategy:**
> The trend in modern shipyards is to **shift work as far upstream (forward) as possible** to maximize pre-outfitting, reducing expensive work at height, on the berth, or after launch. This is called the **"PEO Push-Up"** strategy.

### 2.3 STAGE 4–6 — POST-PRODUCTION STAGE (Detailed Breakdown)

Post-production begins effectively the moment the ship touches water, even though some yard work continues.

#### 2.3.1 Quay Outfitting & Docking
- Final hull piping, painting, insulation, and machinery installation.
- **Docking** (sometimes called "dry-docking for fit-out") — Hull cleaning, painting, propeller fitting, sea valve installation.

#### 2.3.2 Trials
- **Dock Trial (Harbor Trial):** With ship in water but at berth — tests main engines, auxiliaries, steering gear, generators, fire pumps.
- **Mooring Trial:** Alongside quay, testing mooring winches, anchors, and hose connections.
- **Sea Trial:** Full-speed trials, maneuvering (turning circle, crash stop, zig-zag), endurance, and noise/vibration measurements. Conducted over 2–5 days.
- **Speed and Powering Trial:** Confirms the vessel meets contractual service speed and fuel consumption.

#### 2.3.3 Snag List Rectification
- A **Snag List (Punch List)** is generated containing all defects, outstanding work, and minor non-conformities.
- Rectification continues parallel to trials to avoid delivery delay.

#### 2.3.4 Classification & Statutory Surveys
- **Classification Survey:** Class surveyor (e.g., IRS, LR) inspects the vessel to confirm compliance with rules — issuance of **Class Certificate**.
- **Flag State Surveys:** Compliance with SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Line, etc.
- **Owner's Acceptance:** Final walkthrough with owner/owner's superintendent.

#### 2.3.5 Delivery & Handover
- **Delivery Ceremony:** Symbolic handover with exchange of documents.
- **Documentation Package:** As-built drawings, operating manuals, certificates, spares list, test reports.
- **Warranty Period:** Typically 12–24 months, during which the yard rectifies defects under guarantee.

#### 2.3.6 Post-Delivery Support
- Guarantee claim handling, technical assistance, supply of spares, and follow-up service for retrofits or upgrades.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|---------------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Compensated Gross Tonnage | $\text{CGT} = \text{GT} \times C_f$ | unitless | $C_f$ is a complexity factor depending on vessel type |
| 2 | Standard Man-Hours | $\text{SMH} = \dfrac{\text{Total Man-Hours}}{\text{Steel Weight (t)}}$ | hours / ton | Typical frigate: 80–100; tanker: 15–25 |
| 3 | Schedule Slip | $\text{Slip} = \dfrac{T_{\text{actual}} - T_{\text{planned}}}{T_{\text{planned}}} \times 100\%$ | % | Negative = ahead of schedule |
| 4 | Productivity (tons/man-month) | $P = \dfrac{\text{Output (CGT)}}{\text{Man-months}}$ | CGT / man-month | Yard KPI |
| 5 | Critical Path Duration (CPM) | $T_{cp} = \sum t_{i,\;i+1}^{\text{critical}}$ | days | Sum of longest path activities |
| 6 | Float / Slack | $\text{Float} = LS - ES = LF - EF$ | days | LS=Late Start, ES=Early Start |
| 7 | Steel Yield | $Y = \dfrac{W_{\text{final hull}}}{W_{\text{plate purchased}}} \times 100\%$ | % | 75–85% is good |
| 8 | Pre-Outfitting Ratio | $R_{PEO} = \dfrac{\text{Outfitting man-hours in blocks}}{\text{Total outfitting man-hours}} \times 100\%$ | % | Higher = better |
| 9 | Welding Deposition Rate | $R_w = \dfrac{W_{\text{weld metal deposited}}}{t_{\text{arc on}}}$ | kg/h or mm/min | Function of current & wire feed |
| 10 | Net Section Modulus (hull girder) | $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{\max}}$ | cm³ or m³ | $I$ = 2nd moment of area |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Project Managers** in shipyards use this three-stage model as the spine of their **Project Execution Plan (PEP)**.
- **Production Engineers** use WBS and CPM outputs to balance labor across workshops and avoid bottlenecks.
- **Class Surveyors & Auditors** use the stage boundaries (SCD, KLD, LD, STD, DD) as audit checkpoints.
- **Commercial Teams** use **stage payments** linked to milestones (e.g., 5% on SCD, 5% on KLD, 55% on launch, 25% on sea trials, 10% on delivery — a common split).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

Since this topic is largely **process-management-oriented**, this section presents the analytical derivations of key scheduling and productivity formulas used in ship production management.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Critical Path Method (CPM) for Build Schedule

The **Critical Path** in a shipbuild network is defined as the longest path through the activity-on-node (AoN) or activity-on-arrow (AoA) network, because any delay along this path directly delays project completion.

**Step 1 — List all activities and their durations $t_i$.**
For a simplified build, suppose we have 5 activities A → B → C → D with parallel path X → Y feeding into D.

**Step 2 — Forward Pass (Earliest Event Times).**
For each node $j$:

$$ES_j = \max_{i \in \text{predecessors}(j)} \left( ES_i + t_{i \to j} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Backward Pass (Latest Event Times).**
For each node $i$ (starting from the last node with $LF = T_{cp}$):

$$LF_i = \min_{j \in \text{successors}(i)} \left( LS_j - t_{i \to j} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Compute Float for each activity.**

$$F_{i \to j} = LS_j - ES_i - t_{i \to j}$$

**Step 5 — Critical Path.**
All activities with $F = 0$ lie on the **Critical Path**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example Walk-Through:**
> Let activities: A(5) → B(3) → D(4); and A(5) → C(2) → D(4). Days in brackets = duration.
>
> Forward pass:
> ES(A) = 0; ES(B) = 0 + 5 = 5; ES(C) = 0 + 5 = 5; ES(D) = max(5 + 3, 5 + 2) = max(8, 7) = 8.
>
> Backward pass (assume D is end): LF(D) = 8.
> LF(B) = 8 - 3 = 5; LF(C) = 8 - 2 = 6; LF(A) = min(5 - 5, 6 - 5) = min(0, 1) = 0.
>
> Float for A→B: LS(B) - ES(A) - t_AB = 5 - 0 - 5 = 0. **Critical.**
> Float for A→C: LS(C) - ES(A) - t_AC = 6 - 0 - 5 = 1. **Not critical (slack = 1 day).**
>
> **Critical Path: A → B → D, Duration = 8 days.**

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Schedule Slip Percentage

The yard's performance against a baseline plan is quantified by **Schedule Slip**:

$$\text{Slip (\%)} = \frac{T_{\text{actual}} - T_{\text{planned}}}{T_{\text{planned}}} \times 100\%$$

**Worked Example:**
A ship was planned for launch at Day 250 from steel cutting, but actually launched on Day 268.

$$\text{Slip} = \frac{268 - 250}{250} \times 100\% = \frac{18}{250} \times 100\% = 7.2\%$$

This 7.2% slip will trigger **liquidated damages (LD)** clauses in the contract, typically charged at a daily rate (e.g., 0.1% of contract price per day, capped at 5–10%).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pre-Outfitting Ratio (PEO %)

To quantify how much outfitting is shifted upstream:

$$R_{PEO} = \frac{\text{Outfitting Man-Hours in Blocks}}{\text{Total Outfitting Man-Hours}} \times 100\%$$

**Worked Example:**
A frigate project has total outfitting = 800,000 man-hours. Of this, 560,000 man-hours are spent on outfitting inside blocks before erection (PEO + BO + UEO combined).

$$R_{PEO} = \frac{560{,}000}{800{,}000} \times 100\% = 70\%$$

A value of 70% is considered **excellent** for a warship; commercial yards aim for 40–60%.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Standard Man-Hours (SMH) per Ton

$$\text{SMH} = \frac{H_{\text{total}}}{W_{\text{steel}}}$$

**Worked Example (Tanker):**
A 30,000 DWT tanker uses 7,200 t of steel. Total man-hours consumed in hull fabrication = 144,000.

$$\text{SMH} = \frac{144{,}000}{7{,}200} = 20 \text{ hours/ton}$$

This is typical for a modern double-hull tanker (15–25 SMH range).

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Productivity in CGT/Man-Month

$$P = \frac{\text{Output (CGT)}}{\text{Man-months}}$$

**Worked Example:**
A yard delivered 150,000 CGT in one year, employing an average of 1,200 workers.

$$P = \frac{150{,}000}{1{,}200 \times 12} = \frac{150{,}000}{14{,}400} \approx 10.4 \text{ CGT/man-month}$$

A value above 5 CGT/man-month indicates a competitive shipyard (South Korean top yards exceed 15–20).

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Stage Interface Checklist

| Interface | From → To | Key Documents | Risk if Not Done |
|-----------|-----------|---------------|------------------|
| Contract → Design | Pre → Pre | Owner's Spec, Class Rules | Redesign cost |
| Design → Planning | Pre → Pre | Production Drawings | Schedule re-work |
| Planning → Procurement | Pre → Pre | BOM | Material shortage |
| Procurement → Cutting | Pre → Production | Material in stock | Steel shortage → SCD slip |
| Cutting → Block Assembly | Production | Cut parts | Line stoppage |
| Block → Erection | Production | Block ready for lifting | Berth congestion |
| Erection → Launching | Production | Hull complete | Launching delay |
| Launching → Trials | Production → Post | Quay outfitting done | Trial delays |
| Trials → Delivery | Post → Post | All certificates in hand | Delivery delay → LD |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Flow Diagram — Three-Stage Production Management

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete ship production management cycle from contract to post-delivery support:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Contract Signed] --> B[Pre-Production Stage]
    B --> B1[Contract Design Review]
    B1 --> B2[Production Design 3D CAD]
    B2 --> B3[Work Breakdown Structure WBS]
    B3 --> B4[Master Schedule CPM]
    B4 --> B5[Material Procurement BOM]
    B5 --> B6[Process Engineering WPS Nesting]
    B6 --> B7[Shop Floor Preparation]
    B7 --> C[Production Stage]
    C --> C1[Steel Stock Cutting]
    C1 --> C2[Sub-Assembly Panel Line]
    C2 --> C3[Block Assembly]
    C3 --> C4[Pre-Outfitting in Blocks]
    C4 --> C5[Grand Block Erection on Berth]
    C5 --> C6[Launching]
    C6 --> C7[Quay Outfitting]
    C7 --> D[Post-Production Stage]
    D --> D1[Dock Trial]
    D1 --> D2[Mooring Trial]
    D2 --> D3[Sea Trial]
    D3 --> D4[Snag List Rectification]
    D4 --> D5[Class and Flag Surveys]
    D5 --> D6[Delivery to Owner]
    D6 --> D7[Warranty Period Support]
    D7 --> E[Post Delivery Service Retrofits Spares]
```

### 4.2 Pre-Production Sub-Phase Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Contract Sign] --> P1[Design Kickoff]
    P1 --> P2[Basic Design]
    P2 --> P3[Contract Design]
    P3 --> P4[Production Design]
    P4 --> P5[3D CAD Modelling]
    P5 --> P6[Interference Check]
    P6 --> P7[BOM Generation]
    P7 --> P8[Procurement]
    P8 --> P9[Nesting and Cutting Plans]
    P9 --> P10[WPS Preparation]
    P10 --> P11[Master Schedule Approved]
    P11 --> P12[Steel Cutting Date Milestone]
```

### 4.3 Production Stage — Block Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Steel Plates and Profiles] --> S1[Shot Blasting]
    S1 --> S2[CNC Plasma or Oxy Cutting]
    S2 --> S3[Edge Preparation Beveling]
    S3 --> S4[Panel Line Sub-Assembly]
    S4 --> S5[Mini-Block Assembly]
    S5 --> S6[Block Assembly with Pre-Outfitting]
    S6 --> S7[Block Painting]
    S7 --> S8[Erection on Berth Grand Block]
    S8 --> S9[Hull Joining and Welding]
    S9 --> S10[Launching Float Out]
    S10 --> S11[Berth Outfitting Continued]
    S11 --> S12[Quay Outfitting Alongside]
```

### 4.4 Post-Production Stage — Acceptance Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q0[Quay Outfitting Completed] --> Q1[Docking for Hull Work]
    Q1 --> Q2[Dock Trial at Berth]
    Q2 --> Q3[Mooring Trial]
    Q3 --> Q4[Sea Trial Full Speed and Maneuvering]
    Q4 --> Q5[Snag List Generation]
    Q5 --> Q6[Snag Rectification]
    Q6 --> Q7[Class Survey]
    Q7 --> Q8[Flag State Survey]
    Q8 --> Q9[Owners Acceptance]
    Q9 --> Q10[Documentation Handover]
    Q10 --> Q11[Delivery Ceremony]
    Q11 --> Q12[Warranty Period 12 to 24 months]
    Q12 --> Q13[Post Delivery Spares and Retrofits]
```

### 4.5 Stage Payment Milestone Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M0[Contract Signing 10 percent] --> M1[Steel Cutting 10 percent]
    M1 --> M2[Keel Laying 10 percent]
    M2 --> M3[Launching 20 percent]
    M3 --> M4[Sea Trial 25 percent]
    M4 --> M5[Delivery 25 percent]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The exact percentage split is **contractually negotiated** and varies between commercial (LNG carriers, bulk carriers) and naval (warship) builds. The above is a typical illustrative split for a commercial vessel.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define the term "Steel Cutting Date" (SCD) and explain its significance as a milestone in ship production management.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Steel Cutting Date (SCD)** is the contractual milestone marking the first cut on the first steel plate of the vessel using a numerically controlled or oxy-cutting machine. Its significance:
1. **(1 mark)** It is the **interface milestone** between the **Pre-Production** and **Production** stages — formally indicating that all design, planning, procurement, and process engineering activities are complete and approved.
2. **(1 mark)** It triggers the **first stage payment** from the owner (typically 5–10% of contract price) and starts the **build clock** for delivery-date calculation.
3. **(1 mark)** It activates the **productivity clock** — from SCD, the yard is measured in **Standard Man-Hours per ton (SMH)**, **CGT/man-month**, and schedule slip.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*List any three differences between "Pre-Outfitting" and "Quay Outfitting" in ship production.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Pre-Outfitting (PEO) | Quay Outfitting (QEO) |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------------|
| **Stage** (1 mark) | During block fabrication (upstream) | After launching (downstream) |
| **Working Conditions** (1 mark) | At ground level, better access, safer | At height, on board, more difficult & costly |
| **Productivity** (1 mark) | High productivity, parallel with other trades | Lower productivity, sequential interference |
| **Cost Impact** | Lower unit cost | 2–3× higher cost per man-hour |

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Model)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample Model]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Pre-Production Stage** of ship production management. List at least six sub-activities and describe any two in depth. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Production Stage** of shipbuilding, including the various outfitting strategies adopted by modern shipyards. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Pre-Production Stage (7 marks):**

The **Pre-Production Stage** encompasses all activities that occur *before* the first steel is cut. It transforms a contract into a fully planned, resourced, and ready-to-build project. Sub-activities include:

1. **(½ mark)** Contract design review and Owner's specification analysis.
2. **(½ mark)** Production design and 3D CAD modelling with interference checking.
3. **(½ mark)** Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) generation.
4. **(½ mark)** Master schedule preparation (CPM/PERT network).
5. **(½ mark)** Bill of Materials (BOM) generation and procurement.
6. **(½ mark)** Welding Procedure Specifications (WPS) and process planning.
7. **(½ mark)** Shop floor preparation and workforce mobilization.

**Depth on Activity (i) — Production Design (2 marks):**
The **Production Design** converts the contract design into working drawings for every plate, stiffener, pipe, and cable. It is done in 3D CAD (FORAN, Aveva Marine, Tribon, CATIA) and includes **interference checking** to detect clashes between hull, machinery, piping, ventilation, and electrical systems. A 1% error here can cause 10% rework at the building berth, so the design is typically frozen progressively (block-by-block) using a **design freeze schedule**.

**Depth on Activity (ii) — Master Schedule (2 marks):**
The **Master Schedule** is a Precedence Diagram (AoN) showing the logical sequence of all major activities from SCD to DD. It is built using the **Critical Path Method (CPM)**, identifying the longest dependency chain that determines the **minimum build duration**. The yard then computes **float/slack** for non-critical paths, which becomes the buffer for absorbing delays. The schedule is also linked to **stage payments** and resource leveling (welders, fitters, electricians).

**Part (b) — Production Stage and Outfitting Strategies (7 marks):**

The **Production Stage** is the physical realization of the ship. Modern yards use a **block construction** philosophy where the ship is divided into 30–200 blocks, each fabricated off the berth and lifted into position.

**Main Sub-Stages (3 marks):**
- **Steel Stock Preparation:** Shot blasting, cutting, edge preparation.
- **Sub-Assembly:** Plates + stiffeners → flat panels.
- **Block Assembly:** Panels → 3D blocks (with pre-outfitting).
- **Grand Block / Unit Assembly:** Multiple blocks → units.
- **Erection on Berth:** Units joined on the building berth.
- **Launching:** Ship slides into water.
- **Quay Outfitting:** Fit-out work alongside the quay.

**Outfitting Strategies (4 marks):**

| Strategy | Where Done | Advantage |
|----------|------------|-----------|
| **Pre-Outfitting (PEO)** | At panel/mini-block stage | Maximum productivity, ground-level work |
| **Block Outfitting (BO)** | After block assembly | More access, parallel with painting |
| **Unit Outfitting (UEO)** | At grand-block level | Largest scope pre-erection |
| **Zone Outfitting (ZEO)** | On berth, by functional zone | Logical system testing |
| **Quay Outfitting (QEO)** | After launching | Final fit-out, system commissioning |

**Modern Yard Principle:** **"Push outfitting as far upstream as possible"** to maximize work done at ground level and minimize expensive work at height and after launch. A well-run modern yard achieves 50–70% of outfitting man-hours in the pre-erection stages.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** What is **post-production** in shipbuilding? Explain the various trials conducted before delivery of a vessel to the owner. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)** and the **Critical Path Method (CPM)** as applied to ship production management, with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Post-Production and Trials (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The **Post-Production Stage** encompasses all activities from the ship's launching (or final outfitting) to its formal delivery to the owner, including trials, surveys, snag rectification, documentation, and warranty support.

**Types of Trials (6 marks):**

1. **Dock Trial (Harbor Trial) (1½ marks):**
   Conducted with the ship moored at the fitting-out quay immediately after launch. Tests main engines (turning only, no propulsion), generators, pumps, steering gear, fire-fighting systems, and emergency shutdowns. Detects installation defects before going to sea.

2. **Mooring Trial (1 mark):**
   Tests mooring winches, anchor windlasses, capstans, and cargo/ballast transfer systems while still alongside. Verifies the ship's ability to handle lines and anchors.

3. **Sea Trial (3 marks):**
   The most critical test — conducted in open sea over 2–5 days. It includes:
   - **Builder's Sea Trial:** Initial performance verification.
   - **Speed and Powering Trial:** Measures service speed and fuel consumption against contract guarantees.
   - **Maneuvering Trials:** Turning circle, crash stop, zig-zag (Dieudonné), overshoot, and course-keeping.
   - **Endurance Trial:** Continuous running for 4–6 hours to check thermal stability of machinery.
   - **Noise and Vibration Survey:** Compliance with SOLAS and crew comfort specs.
   - **Owner's Sea Trial:** Final acceptance by the owner's superintendent.

**Survey and Delivery (½ mark):** Class surveyor and flag-state inspectors attend trials; on satisfactory completion, **Class Certificate** and **Statutory Certificates** are issued, enabling **Delivery**.

**Part (b) — WBS and CPM with Example (7 marks):**

**Work Breakdown Structure (3 marks):**
A **WBS** is a hierarchical decomposition of the entire ship into progressively smaller, manageable work packages. Example:

```
Ship
├── Hull
│   ├── Forward Block
│   │   ├── Plate Panel
│   │   ├── Stiffener Sub-assembly
│   │   └── Mini-block
│   ├── Amidship Block
│   └── Aft Block
├── Machinery
│   ├── Main Engine
│   ├── Generators
│   └── Pumps
├── Outfitting
│   ├── Piping
│   ├── Electrical
│   └── HVAC
└── Painting and Insulation
```

Each leaf node has a **man-hour estimate, material list, schedule, and responsible trade**. The WBS forms the basis for **cost control, scheduling, and progress measurement**.

**Critical Path Method (4 marks):**
CPM identifies the longest sequence of dependent activities that determines the minimum project duration. The **critical path** is the chain where any delay causes overall project delay.

**Numerical Example (4 marks):**
Consider a simplified erection sequence with the following activities:

| Activity | Predecessor | Duration (days) |
|----------|-------------|-----------------|
| A (Stockyard prep) | — | 5 |
| B (Cutting) | A | 3 |
| C (Sub-assembly) | B | 4 |
| D (Block assembly) | C | 6 |
| E (Erection) | D | 8 |
| F (Launching prep) | E | 2 |

All activities are in series, so the critical path is **A → B → C → D → E → F** with duration:

$$T_{cp} = 5 + 3 + 4 + 6 + 8 + 2 = 28 \text{ days}$$

Since all activities are critical, **float = 0**, meaning any single-day delay in any activity delays the launch by one day.

**In a real network**, parallel paths exist (e.g., outfitting parallel to hull), and float is computed for non-critical branches. CPM helps the project manager decide where to apply acceleration (e.g., overtime, additional shifts) for maximum schedule recovery.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make in This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "Pre-Production" with "Pre-Outfitting"** — Pre-Production is a *stage* (planning, design, procurement); Pre-Outfitting is a *strategy* (fitting pipes in blocks). Examiners deduct 1 mark for this mix-up.
> 2. **Omitting the interface milestones** — Always mention SCD, KLD, LD, STD, DD as *boundaries* between stages when answering long questions.
> 3. **Skipping the role of CPM/WBS** — Even a 14-mark answer without WBS or CPM examples is considered incomplete.
> 4. **Confusing "Sea Trial" with "Dock Trial"** — Sea trial is *at sea*; dock trial is *at berth*. Examiners frequently test this distinction.
> 5. **Forgetting warranty/post-delivery** — Post-Production does *not* end at delivery; warranty support (12–24 months) is part of the lifecycle.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Production Management (Module 2)**

- ✅ Ship production management is divided into **3 stages**: Pre-Production, Production, and Post-Production.
- ✅ **Pre-Production** = Everything *before* **Steel Cutting Date (SCD)**. Activities: design, planning, procurement, process engineering, shop-floor prep.
- ✅ **Production Stage** uses **block construction** philosophy. Modern yards aim for **70%+ pre-outfitting** to maximize ground-level work.
- ✅ **Outfitting strategies**: PEO → BO → UEO → ZEO → QEO (in increasing order of cost per man-hour).
- ✅ **Launching Date (LD)** is the interface between Production and Post-Production.
- ✅ **Post-Production** includes **Dock Trial → Mooring Trial → Sea Trial** (in that order).
- ✅ **Sea Trial** checks: speed, fuel consumption, maneuvering (turning circle, crash stop, zig-zag), endurance, noise & vibration.
- ✅ **Delivery Date (DD)** is the terminal milestone, after which a **12–24 month warranty** begins.
- ✅ **Key tools**: WBS (decomposition), CPM (longest path = critical path), PERT (probabilistic durations).
- ✅ **Key metrics**: SMH (man-hours/ton), CGT (complexity-weighted output), Schedule Slip %, PEO Ratio.
- ✅ **Stage Payment Milestones** are typically linked to: Contract → SCD → KLD → Launch → Sea Trial → Delivery.
- ✅ **Class survey** (e.g., IRS, LR) issues the **Class Certificate**; **Flag State** issues **Statutory Certificates** (SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Line).
- ✅ The modern shipyard mantra is: **"Push work upstream, build in blocks, test at every level, deliver on time."**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Planning, design, production, material management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST631 - SHIP PRODUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Planning, design, production, material management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Ship Production Planning

**Formal KTU Definition:**
Ship production planning is the systematic process of defining the work scope, sequencing activities, allocating resources (manpower, materials, machinery, and time), and scheduling operations required to convert a ship design into a fully built, commissioned, and delivered vessel within the contracted cost, quality, and time envelope.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition):**
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "The Wedding Planner"** — Imagine organizing a massive Indian wedding with 500 guests, 4 venues, 200 dishes, and strict rituals tied to an auspicious hour. The wedding planner (Production Planner) does NOT cook the food or sing the songs. Instead, they make a master list (Work Breakdown Structure), fix a date-by-date schedule (Gantt chart), identify the critical activities that cannot be delayed (CPM critical path), and ensure vendors, decorators, and cooks arrive *exactly* when needed. **Ship production planning is the "wedding planner" of shipbuilding** — coordinating thousands of trades, thousands of tonnes of steel, and a delivery deadline that simply cannot slip.

**Core Sub-Functions of Ship Production Planning:**

| Sub-Function | KTU 2024 Terminology | Real-World Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **WBS** (Work Breakdown Structure) | Hierarchical decomposition of the vessel | Breaking the whole ship into "manageable bites" |
| **Scheduling** | Time-phased execution plan | "When does each bite get eaten?" |
| **Capacity Planning** | Berth, gantry, panel line balancing | Can our factory physically handle the workload? |
| **Progress Monitoring** | Earned Value Management (EVM) | Are we ahead or behind the plan? |

## 1.2 Ship Design (Production-Oriented Design)

**Formal Definition:**
In the KTU 2024 context, ship design is the multi-stage engineering process — ranging from concept/feasibility design to contract, functional, and finally **production design** — that translates an owner's operational requirement into buildable drawings, specifications, and 3D models suitable for direct shop-floor execution.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: "The Tailor's Pattern"** — A tailor's first sketch is the *concept design* ("make a black suit"). The measurement sheet is the *contract design*. The cardboard pattern pieces are the *production design* — the tailor cannot cut fabric without the pattern. **In shipbuilding, a beautiful hull form on a screen means nothing until a production design exists that the welder on the floor can read and execute.** Production design = the "pattern pieces" for the welders, fitters, and outfitters.

**The Four Canonical Stages of Ship Design (Mandatory KTU 2024 Framework):**

1. **Concept Design (Feasibility Study)** — Overall dimensions, principal particulars, general arrangement.
2. **Contract Design** — Lines plan, capacity, powering, sea-keeping, manning. Becomes part of the shipbuilding contract.
3. **Functional Design** — Hull structure, machinery, electrical, automation. Defines *what* each system does.
4. **Production Design** — Detailed workshop drawings, nesting plans, weld maps, pipe spool drawings, cable schedules. Defines *how* it is built.

## 1.3 Ship Production (Construction Strategy)

**Formal Definition:**
Ship production is the *physical execution phase* of shipbuilding — encompassing steel preparation, fabrication of blocks, sub-assemblies, grand assembly on the berth/slipway, launching, outfitting afloat, and trials — using a chosen construction strategy such as **block construction**, **modular construction**, or **zonal outfitting**.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "Building a Lego Castle in a Stadium"** — You do NOT carry individual Lego bricks to the stadium. You build sections (modules) in a workshop, transport them, and snap them together at the venue. **Modern shipbuilding uses the exact same Lego logic**: build blocks of 200–800 tonnes in workshops, move them by giant self-propelled transporters (SPMTs) to the dry dock, and join them like Lego pieces.

## 1.4 Material Management in Shipyards

**Formal Definition:**
Material management in a shipyard is the integrated logistics function encompassing **procurement, receipt, inspection, storage, issue, and traceability** of steels, pipes, cables, outfit items, and consumables — timed precisely to match the production schedule to minimize working capital lock-up while avoiding stock-outs on the critical path.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: "The Shipyard Pantry"** — A restaurant does not buy 10 tonnes of rice "just in case." It buys exactly what the next 3 days' menu needs (Just-In-Time). However, it DOES keep a small emergency buffer of salt, oil, and spices (safety stock). **Shipyard material management is the "pantry manager"** — but on a 200-acre scale, where a single steel plate costs ₹5 lakh and a missing 50 mm pipe can stall a ₹500 crore vessel for 3 weeks.

**Key Standard Metrics in Bold (KTU Examinable):**
- **EOQ (Economic Order Quantity)** — optimal reorder quantity
- **Safety Stock (SS)** — buffer against demand/supply variability
- **Reorder Level (ROL)** — inventory point that triggers a new purchase order
