| 3 | Section modulus reqd: $Z_{\text{req}} = M_{\text{bend}} / \sigma_{\text{perm}}$ | $M$ in N·m, $\sigma$ in N/m² | Hull girder |
| 4 | Stem plate thickness: $t_s = 0.07 L + 5$ | $L$ in m, $t_s$ in mm (empirical) | Fore end sizing |
| 5 | Rudder area: $A_R \approx 0.02 L \cdot T$ | $L$ (m), $T$ (m) | Steering geometry |
| 6 | Crane SWL verification: $F_{\text{dyn}} = \phi_1 \cdot \phi_2 \cdot W_{\text{SWL}}$ | $\phi_1, \phi_2$ = dynamic factors (1.1–1.6) | Class approval |
| 7 | Engine bedplate stiffness: $I_{\text{min}} = \frac{P \cdot l^3}{48 E \delta_{\text{lim}}}$ | $P$ load, $l$ span, $\delta_{\text{lim}} \leq L/1000$ | Foundation check |
| 8 | Superstructure height effect on BM: $\Delta BM = \rho g \int b h^2 \, dx$ | Increases bending moment in seaway | Trim & stability |
| 9 | Collision bulkhead location: $x \le 0.05 L$ or $10$ m | Whichever is smaller | SOLAS Reg. II-1/11 |
| 10 | Davit hoisting time: $t \le 1$ min for 50% load, $t \le 3$ min full | LSA Code | Life-boat certification |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas drive scantling drawings, classification society approvals, and yard production engineering. A miscalculated stem plate thickness or wrong camber can sink a yard in warranty costs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Plate Panel Shrinkage Compensation in a Welding Jig

### Problem Setup

A shipyard is producing a 12 m × 3 m side-shell panel. The panel is composed of 3 m × 3 m sub-plates butt-welded together with a single-V butt weld. Each weld run deposits ~**1.5 mm/m** of transverse shrinkage. The jig must apply **reverse camber** so that after welding, the panel is dimensionally true.

### Derivation

Let:
- $L_p$ = panel length = **12 m**
- $k$ = transverse shrinkage coefficient = **1.5 mm/m** (per weld pass, per metre of weld)
- $n$ = number of transverse butt welds = 3
- $\delta_{\text{panel}}$ = total transverse shrinkage of the finished panel

**Step 1 — Total transverse shrinkage from all welds:**

$$\delta_{\text{panel}} = n \cdot k \cdot L_w$$

Substituting $n = 3$, $k = 1.5$ mm/m, $L_w = 3$ m (each butt weld is 3 m long):

$$\delta_{\text{panel}} = 3 \cdot 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 3 = 13.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 13.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compensatory camber to be set in the jig:**

The jig must pre-stretch the panel by an equal amount so that the net length is correct:

$$L_{\text{jig}} = L_p + \delta_{\text{panel}}$$

$$L_{\text{jig}} = 12.000 + 0.0135 = 12.0135 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Verification of camber curvature (assuming parabolic shape):**

The mid-ordinate of camber $h$ for a parabolic pre-bow of length $L$ is:

$$h = \frac{5}{16} \cdot \frac{\delta_{\text{panel}}}{n_{\text{blocks}}}$$

with $n_{\text{blocks}} = 5$ jacking points. Then:

$$h = \frac{5}{16} \cdot \frac{13.5}{5} = \frac{5 \cdot 13.5}{80} = 0.84375 \text{ mm}$$

This 0.84 mm mid-ordinate is small but **non-negligible** for a 12 m panel — proving that reverse-camber planning is essential for every panel run.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world implementation:** The camber is achieved by **hydraulic rams on the panel jig** that "banana-shape" the plate before tack-welding. After completion, the panel is released and springs back to within ±2 mm of the true length.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Jig Type by Sub-Assembly

| Stage | Sub-Assembly | Recommended Jig | Cycle Time | Accuracy (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Flat plate with longitudinals | Single-station panel jig | 2–4 h | ±2 |
| 2 | T-section, flat-bar stiffened plate | Multi-station carousel | 3–5 h | ±1.5 |
| 3 | Double-bottom panel (2 plates + floors) | Vertical sub-assembly jig (T-jig) | 6–8 h | ±3 |
| 4 | Side-shell block (shell + 2 decks + bulkhead) | 3D sub-assembly jig with turnover | 12–20 h | ±4 |
| 5 | Engine-room block | Modular jig + SPMT handling | 24–40 h | ±5 |
| 6 | Fore peak / Aft peak | Specialised cup jig (conical) | 8–12 h | ±3 |
| 7 | Superstructure module | Deck-level frame jig | 10–15 h | ±2 |
| 8 | Complete hull on berth | Keel + bilge blocks, no full jig | 1–2 weeks | ±10 (block-by-block) |

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Machinery Foundation Bolt Sizing

A main engine weighs **180 t** (W = 1,765 kN) and produces an axial thrust of **600 kN** at service. We need to size the **holding-down bolts**.

**Step 1 — Total vertical load on bolts (downward, due to weight + dynamic):**

$$F_v = W \cdot (1 + \phi_d) = 1{,}765 \cdot (1 + 0.20) = 2{,}118 \text{ kN}$$

with dynamic factor $\phi_d = 0.20$ (for marine main propulsion).

**Step 2 — Total horizontal load (thrust + reversal load):**

$$F_h = F_T \cdot \beta_{\text{rev}} = 600 \cdot 1.5 = 900 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Assume 16 bolts arranged in 2 rows of 8. The shear on each bolt (preload is ignored for initial sizing):**

$$F_{h,\text{per bolt}} = \frac{F_h}{16} = 56.25 \text{ kN}$$

$$F_{v,\text{per bolt}} = \frac{F_v}{16} = 132.4 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent bolt force (vector sum):**

$$F_{\text{eq}} = \sqrt{F_{h}^2 + F_{v}^2}\big/_{\text{per bolt}} = \sqrt{56.25^2 + 132.4^2}$$

$$F_{\text{eq}} = \sqrt{3{,}164 + 17{,}530} = \sqrt{20{,}694} \approx 143.9 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Required bolt core area (Grade 8.8, $\sigma_y = 800$ MPa, allowable $\sigma = 0.6 \cdot 800 = 480$ MPa):**

$$A_{\text{req}} = \frac{F_{\text{eq}}}{\sigma_{\text{allow}}} = \frac{143{,}900}{480} = 299.8 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 6 — Required nominal bolt diameter:**

$$d_{\text{core}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 A_{\text{req}}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot 299.8}{\pi}} = 19.5 \text{ mm}$$

Standard thread size with core area ≥ 299.8 mm² is **M30** (core ≈ 519 mm²), giving a **factor of safety of ~1.73**. This is consistent with class practice, and **M36** is often chosen for marine main engines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** Use **16 × M36, Grade 8.8** holding-down bolts, preloaded to ~70% of proof load (≈ 480 kN each), with chock plates 30 mm thick under each bolt base.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Ship Assembly Sequence Flow (Block Construction)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Steel Stock Plates Profiles] --> A2[Cutting CNC Plasma Oxy]
    A2 --> A3[Edge Preparation Beveling]
    A3 --> B1[Panel Jig Stage 1: Flat Panel Assembly]
    B1 --> B2[Longitudinal Stiffener Welding]
    B2 --> B3[Panel Turnover and Back Welding]
    B3 --> C1[Sub Assembly Jig Stage 2: 3D Block Fabrication]
    C1 --> C2[Double Bottom Block]
    C1 --> C3[Side Shell Block]
    C1 --> C4[Deck Block]
    C1 --> C5[Fore Peak Block]
    C1 --> C6[Aft Peak Block]
    C2 --> D1[Erection Stage 3: Block Joining on Berth]
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
    C5 --> D1
    C6 --> D1
    D1 --> D2[Superstructure Erection]
    D2 --> D3[Machinery Outfitting]
    D3 --> D4[Cranes Davits Masts Installation]
    D4 --> D5[Launch or Float Out]
    D5 --> D6[Outfitting Quay and Sea Trials]
```

## 4.2 Fore End Structure — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Stem Bar Forged Casting] --> F2[Stem Plate Tapered]
    F2 --> F3[Side Shell Plating Forward]
    F2 --> F4[Forecastle Deck]
    F4 --> F5[Fore Peak Bulkhead Collision BT 0]
    F5 --> F6[Fore Peak Tank FPT]
    F6 --> F7[Inner Bottom and Floors]
    F1 --> F8[Bulbous Bow Insert Optional]
    F8 --> F7
    F1 --> F9[Hawse Pipes for Anchor Cables]
    F4 --> F10[Mooring Bollards Fairleads]
    F2 --> F11[Stem Fittings Bell Mouth]
```

## 4.3 Aft End Structure — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Stern Frame Cast] --> A2[Propeller Post]
    A1 --> A3[Sole Piece Rudder Hinge]
    A2 --> A4[Propeller Shaft]
    A4 --> A5[A Bracket or P Bracket Shaft Support]
    A1 --> A6[Transom Plate]
    A6 --> A7[Counter Stern Region]
    A1 --> A8[Rudder Blade with Stock]
    A8 --> A9[Tiller or Steering Gear]
    A5 --> A10[Stern Tube and Bearings]
    A10 --> A11[After Peak Bulkhead]
    A11 --> A12[After Peak Tank APT]
    A11 --> A13[Poop Deck Above]
    A8 --> A14[Rudder Horn or Pintle]
```

## 4.4 Jig Level Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    J0[Ship Assembly Hierarchy] --> J1[Level 0: Steel Stock]
    J1 --> J2[Level 1: Panel Jig Flat Plate plus Stiffeners]
    J2 --> J3[Level 2: Sub Assembly Jig 3D Block]
    J3 --> J4[Level 3: Grand Block Jig 50 to 500 t]
    J4 --> J5[Level 4: Berth Assembly No Jig Modular Blocks]
    J5 --> J6[Level 5: Launch and Outfitting]

    J2 -.uses.-> J2a[Single Station Carousel Turnover]
    J3 -.uses.-> J3a[Vertical T Jig Box Jig]
    J4 -.uses.-> J4a[Modular Fixture with Hydraulic Rams]
    J5 -.uses.-> J5a[Keel Blocks Bilge Blocks Side Blocks]
```

## 4.5 Machinery Foundation Layout (Schematic Plan View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Main Engine 180 t] --> M2[Engine Bedplate Doubled Plate 25 mm]
    M2 --> M3[Engine Bearers Longitudinal Girders x2]
    M3 --> M4[Side Girders Transverse]
    M4 --> M5[Double Bottom Floors at 600 mm]
    M2 --> M6[Holding Down Bolts 16 x M36 on Chocks]
    M2 --> M7[Thrust Block Aft End of Engine]
    M7 --> M8[Thrust Shaft to Intermediate Shaft]
    M3 --> M9[Resilient Mounts Optional for Naval]
    M2 --> M10[Pipe Tunnel and Cable Tray]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
*What is an assembly jig? List any two types used in modern shipbuilding.*

**Model Answer:**
An assembly jig is a purpose-built fixture that holds, positions, and aligns ship structural members during fit-up and welding, ensuring dimensional accuracy and minimising distortion. Two types:
1. **Panel Jig** — for fabricating flat plates with stiffeners.
2. **Sub-Assembly Jig (3D jig)** — for joining panels into 3D blocks such as double-bottom sections.
*(Other acceptable answers: multi-station carousel, turnover jig, grand-block jig, berth jig.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*State the SOLAS requirement for the location of the collision bulkhead.*

**Model Answer:**
Per SOLAS Chapter II-1, Regulation 11, the collision bulkhead shall be located at a distance from the forward perpendicular (FP) of not less than **0.05 L** (where L is the rule length) and not more than **0.05 L or 10 m**, whichever is smaller. It must extend to the freeboard deck and be watertight up to that deck.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not** list components without functional reasoning — marks are split **40% for structure identification, 60% for function/justification**.
> - **Do not** confuse *poop deck* (aft raised deck) with *forecastle* (forward raised deck) — a common 1-mark loss.
> - **Always** state **SOLAS / class rule** references where applicable; marks are reserved for regulatory awareness.
> - For calculations, show every step — even a missing unit can cost 0.5 marks.
> - Avoid generic statements like "the hull is strong" — use quantitative language (e.g., "stem plate is 1.5× side-shell thickness").

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the **structural arrangement of the fore end of a ship**, listing the function of each major component. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Explain in detail the **classification of assembly jigs** used in modern shipbuilding, with reference to camber compensation and the level of mechanisation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Fore End Structural Arrangement (7 marks)**

The fore end of a ship comprises the structural envelope forward of amidships, designed to absorb bow impact, wave slam, and provide reserve buoyancy. The major components are:

1. **Stem bar (forging/casting):** The single most heavily loaded forward member, transmitting all axial forces from side shell to keel. Thickness 1.5–2× side-shell. **1 mark for identification + 0.5 for function.**
2. **Stem plate:** Connects the stem bar to the forecastle deck and side shell. Tapered thickness. **1 mark.**
3. **Forecastle deck:** Raised forward weather deck, typically 1.8–2.5 m above main deck. Improves seakeeping (reduces bow deck wetness) and provides accommodation. **1 mark.**
4. **Collision bulkhead (BT-0):** First transverse watertight bulkhead, per SOLAS located at ≤ 0.05 L from FP. **1 mark.**
5. **Fore peak tank (FPT):** Compartment between collision bulkhead and stem, used for fresh water (drinking) or ballast. **0.5 mark.**
6. **Bulbous bow (optional):** Bulb-shaped protrusion below the waterline, reducing wave-making resistance at service speed (saving ~5–8% power). **0.5 mark.**
7. **Hawse pipes, anchor pockets, fairleads:** Mooring and anchoring fittings welded to the side shell forward. **0.5 mark.**

(2 marks reserved for neat sketch with labelled parts.)

**(b) Classification of Assembly Jigs (7 marks)**

Assembly jigs are classified by **stage of use** and by **degree of mechanisation**.

| Jig Type | Stage | Mechanisation |
|---|---|---|
| Panel jig (single/multi-station) | Stage 1 — flat plate | Manual / hydraulic |
| Sub-assembly (T-jig, box jig) | Stage 2 — 3D block | Hydraulic, with turnover |
| Grand-block jig | Stage 3 — large block | CNC, laser-aligned |
| Berth (keel + bilge + side blocks) | Stage 4 — final assembly | Modular, not a jig per se |

**Camber Compensation (2 marks):** The jig is pre-set with **reverse camber** to counter weld shrinkage. The formula is:

$$\delta = n \cdot k \cdot L_w$$

For a 12 m panel with 3 welds at $k = 1.5$ mm/m and $L_w = 3$ m, total shrinkage is **13.5 mm**, which the jig offsets.

**Mechanisation (1 mark):** Modern jigs use CNC-controlled hydraulic rams, laser-line projection for stiffener alignment, and PLC-controlled tack-welding sequences. This reduces labour hours per ton from 60 (1960s) to under 10 (today).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**a)** Describe the **structural arrangement of the aft end of a ship**, with special emphasis on the stern frame, rudder assembly, and shaft bossing. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Explain the design and function of the **machinery foundation** for a marine main engine, including a step-by-step calculation of the size of holding-down bolts for a 1,800 kN engine with 600 kN thrust, assuming 16 bolts in Grade 8.8 steel. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Aft End Structural Arrangement (7 marks)**

The aft end transmits **propeller thrust, hydrodynamic forces from rudder, and shaft-induced vibration** to the hull. The principal members are:

1. **Stern frame (cast/forged):** A massive single-piece (or built-up) steel assembly comprising the propeller post and the sole piece/rudder gudgeon. The propeller shaft passes through the propeller post. **1 mark.**
2. **Transom plate:** Closes the hull aft in a *cruiser stern*; absent in a *counter stern* (where the hull profile tapers to a point above the propeller). **1 mark.**
3. **Shaft bossing / P-bracket or A-bracket:** Supports the propeller shaft as it exits the hull. The **A-bracket** (single-arm) is used where the shaft is supported by a single web; the **P-bracket** (two-arm) is more common. **1 mark.**
4. **Rudder assembly:** Consists of a **rudder blade** (hydrodynamic surface), **rudder stock** (vertical shaft), **tiller/yoke** (for steering gear attachment), and **pintles + gudgeons** (hinge system, supported by the sole piece and rudder horn). **1 mark.**
5. **After peak bulkhead (AP):** Watertight bulkhead at the extreme aft, separating the APT from the engine room. **0.5 mark.**
6. **After peak tank (APT):** Houses the stern-tube and provides trim/ballast capacity. **0.5 mark.**
7. **Poop deck:** Raised deck above the main deck aft, the forward counterpart of the forecastle. **0.5 mark.**

(1.5 marks reserved for neat sketch with labels.)

**(b) Machinery Foundation and Bolt Sizing (7 marks)**

A machinery foundation is **integral to the hull structure**, not a separate casting. It comprises:
- **Doubled double-bottom plating** (2× thickness) under the engine.
- **Engine bearers (2 longitudinal girders)** running fore-aft, 2–3× the height of standard floors.
- **Transverse side girders** forming a grillage.
- **Holding-down bolts** in chocks, 16 × M36 (assumed) for the case below.
- **Thrust block** at the aft end, transmitting propeller thrust to the hull.
- **Resilient mounts** (rubber, on naval vessels) for vibration isolation.

**Bolt-Sizing Calculation (5 marks of the 7):**

Given: $W = 1{,}765$ kN, $F_T = 600$ kN, $\phi_d = 0.20$, $\beta_{\text{rev}} = 1.5$, $n = 16$ bolts.

**Step 1:** Vertical load on foundation:

$$F_v = W \cdot (1 + \phi_d) = 1{,}765 \cdot 1.20 = 2{,}118 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2:** Horizontal load (thrust + reversal):

$$F_h = F_T \cdot \beta_{\text{rev}} = 600 \cdot 1.5 = 900 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3:** Per-bolt force (16 bolts):

$$F_{h,\text{per bolt}} = 900/16 = 56.25 \text{ kN}, \quad F_{v,\text{per bolt}} = 2{,}118/16 = 132.4 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4:** Vector sum:

$$F_{\text{eq}} = \sqrt{56.25^2 + 132.4^2} = \sqrt{20{,}694} \approx 143.9 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5:** Required bolt core area (Grade 8.8, $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = 480$ MPa):

$$A_{\text{req}} = 143{,}900 / 480 = 299.8 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 6:** Equivalent core diameter:

$$d_{\text{core}} = \sqrt{4 \cdot 299.8 / \pi} = 19.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Conclusion:** Select **M30** as a minimum (core area ~519 mm², safety factor ~1.7) or **M36** (core area ~817 mm², safety factor ~2.7) for marine main propulsion. Bolt preload should be ~70% of proof load (~480 kN for M36).

(1 mark for diagram of the foundation in plan view, with bolt arrangement shown.)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Assembly jig = precision fixture**; classified by stage (Panel → Sub-assembly → Grand-block → Berth).
- [x] **Reverse camber** of **1.5 mm/m** must be built into every panel jig to offset weld shrinkage.
- [x] **Fore end = stem bar + stem plate + forecastle + collision bulkhead + FPT** (+ optional bulbous bow).
- [x] **Aft end = stern frame + transom + rudder + A/P-bracket + APT** (+ poop deck above).
- [x] **Collision bulkhead location** is a SOLAS-hard rule: **≤ 0.05 L or 10 m from FP**, whichever is smaller.
- [x] **Superstructures** are semi-monocoque; use **slip joints** to absorb hull-girder deflection (~150 mm in large tankers).
- [x] **Machinery foundation is integral** to the hull — doubled plate, engine bearers, side girders, chock bolts.
- [x] **Holding-down bolts** for main engines are typically **M36–M90, Grade 8.8 or 10.9**, with 70% preload.
- [x] **Cranes** are slewing/luffing motor-driven appliances (SWL 30–100 t for cargo).
- [x] **Davits** are LSA- (Life-Saving Appliance) regulated gravity/luffing arms for lifeboats (≤ 3 min hoisting time full load).
- [x] **Empirical stem-plate rule:** $t_s \approx 0.07 L + 5$ mm (for L in metres).
- [x] **Hull tolerance** on the berth is **±10 mm** for block-to-block jointing.
- [x] **Rule of thumb for superstructure plating:** 0.4–0.6× main hull side-shell thickness.
- [x] **Fillet weld leg size** on a T-joint in longitudinal framing is typically **0.7 × thinner plate thickness**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line Exam Punchlines (Memorise!):**
> 1. *"Stem is the heart of the bow; stern frame is the heart of the stern."*
> 2. *"Jigs control the *shape*; berth blocks control the *weight*."*
> 3. *"A foundation is not bolted *to* the ship — it *is* the ship."*
> 4. *"Davits save lives; cranes save time — but both are regulated by class."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Welding in shipbuilding: Welding methods, standards, symbols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST631 - SHIP PRODUCTION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Welding in shipbuilding: Welding methods, standards, symbols # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Welding in Shipbuilding — Formal KTU Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Welding in Shipbuilding** is defined (per KTU 2024 syllabus / AWS D1.1 context) as a **fusion-joining manufacturing process** in which two or more metallic parts (plates, sections, stiffeners, castings) are locally coalesced by heating to a suitable temperature, with or without the application of pressure and with or without the use of filler metal, to produce a structurally continuous hull, superstructure, or machinery component capable of transmitting design loads across the joint. In modern merchant and naval shipbuilding, **welding has replaced riveting** as the primary joining method because: - It provides **higher joint efficiency** (typically 90–100 % vs. 70–80 % for riveted joints). - It enables **lighter scantlings** through elimination of overlapping plates and rivet holes. - It allows **continuous watertight and gastight seams** essential for hull integrity. **Key Metric**: A modern Panamax bulk carrier requires roughly **300–400 km of weld length**, while a VLCC may require over **800 km** of welding. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Glue That Melts the Metals Together" Imagine two separate sheets of butter placed edge-to-edge on a hot pan. The butter melts locally where the pan is hottest, and the two sheets fuse into one continuous sheet. Once the heat is removed, they are inseparable. **Arc welding is essentially this same phenomenon**, except the heat is a concentrated electric arc (5,000–20,000 °C), the "butter" is a controlled weld pool of molten steel, and a filler rod (electrode) supplies additional metal to bridge the gap. A solidifying weld pool is shielded by gas or flux to prevent atmospheric contamination — just as a lid prevents dust falling on melting butter. ## 1.3 Why Welding Dominates Ship Production | Driver | Effect on Ship Design | |---|---| | **Elimination of rivet holes** | Plate net section equals gross section → lighter hull | | **Continuous seams** | Improved fatigue life and watertightness | | **Reduced fabrication time** | Modular block construction becomes viable | | **Lower labour cost** | Mechanised/robotic welding replaces hand riveting | | **Greater design flexibility** | Curved, double-curved and tapered plates can be joined | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight**: Modern shipyards use **70–80 % mechanised/automated welding** (typically GMAW/FCAW with mechanised carriages and robotic gantries) and only **20–30 % manual welding** for short, positional, or fit-up-tolerant joints. --- # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Welding Processes Used in Shipbuilding The processes permitted by classification societies (DNV, LR, ABS, BV, IRS) are categorised by **heat source**, **shielding**, and **degree of mechanisation**. ### A. By Degree of Mechanisation 1. **Manual welding** — operator holds the electrode; used for short, positional seams. 2. **Semi-automatic (mechanised) welding** — wire feed and travel are mechanised; operator guides the gun. Dominant in modern yards. 3. **Fully automatic / robotic welding** — both wire feed and travel are controlled by a machine (e.g., gantry, column-and-boom, trackless carriage). ### B. By Heat Source / Process Family | Process | Acronym | Heat Source | Shipyard Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | Shielded Metal Arc Welding | **SMAW** | Electric arc (stick) | Erection, repair, short seams, all positions | | Gas Metal Arc Welding | **GMAW / MIG-MAG** | Continuous wire arc | Panel lines, flat & horizontal, high deposition | | Flux-Cored Arc Welding | **FCAW** | Continuous tubular wire arc | Thick plate, out-of-position, high deposition | | Submerged Arc Welding | **SAW** | Arc submerged under flux | Long flat seams, butt joints in panels | | Gas Tungsten Arc Welding | **GTAW / TIG** | Tungsten inert gas arc | Root pass, stainless, aluminium superstructures | | Electroslag Welding | **ESW** | Molten slag resistance heat | Vertical thick-plate butt joints (rare in ships) | | Electrogas Welding | **EGW** | Gas-shielded vertical arc | Vertical thick-plate joints | | Plasma Arc Welding | **PAW** | Constricted plasma | Special alloys, narrow joints | | Stud Welding | — | Arc + pressure | Welding studs for shear connectors | ## 2.2 Detailed Process Description — KTU Exam Critical ### 2.2.1 Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW) - **Principle**: An electric arc is struck between a **flux-coated consumable electrode** and the workpiece. - **Heat Source**: Electric arc; arc temperature ≈ **6,000–8,000 °C**. - **Shielding**: Provided by decomposition of the flux coating → CO₂, shielding gas, and molten slag. - **Polarity**: DCEP (direct current, electrode positive) preferred. - **Applications in Shipbuilding**: Hull erection, repair, root passes, positional welding on stiffeners, brackets, and lugs. - **Advantages**: Simple, portable, all-position, low equipment cost. - **Limitations**: Low deposition rate (≈ 1–3 kg/h), frequent electrode changes, slag removal between passes. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Memory Hook**: SMAW is the **"workhorse of the shipyard"** — used wherever flexibility beats productivity. ### 2.2.2 Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW / MAG) - **Principle**: A continuous solid wire electrode is fed through a gun; the arc melts the wire and base metal; an **external shielding gas** (Ar, CO₂, or Ar/CO₂ mix) protects the weld pool. - **Modes of Metal Transfer**: Short-circuit transfer, globular, spray, pulsed-spray, and **rotating-arc transfer**. - **Shipyard Use**: Panel-line butt welds, deck plating, bulkhead panels; usually with **mechanised carriages** at 60–100 cm/min travel speed. - **Deposition Rate**: 3–8 kg/h. - **Advantage**: Continuous wire → no stub loss; high productivity; clean weld (no slag). ### 2.2.3 Flux-Cored Arc Welding (FCAW) - **Principle**: A **tubular electrode** filled with flux replaces solid wire. May be self-shielded (FCAW-SS) or gas-shielded (FCAW-G). - **Shipyard Use**: **Out-of-position welding** (vertical-up, overhead) on hull structural members; high deposition (5–15 kg/h). - **KTU Key**: FCAW is favoured for **vertical welding of webs and bulkheads** in modern block construction. ### 2.2.4 Submerged Arc Welding (SAW) - **Principle**: Arc is **submerged under a granular flux**; arc is invisible; spatter-free; very high current (up to 2,000 A). - **Shipyard Use**: **Long butt seams in panels, double-sided butt welding of thick plates, T-fillet welds with strip electrodes**. - **Deposition Rate**: **15–50 kg/h** — highest among conventional shipyard processes. - **Limitation**: Restricted to **flat (1G/1F) or horizontal-fillet (2F) positions** because the flux bed must remain in place by gravity. - **Variants**: Single-wire, twin-wire, tandem, multi-wire; **strip cladding** for corrosion-resistant weld overlays. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Fact**: SAW delivers ~**90 % arc-on time** and deposition efficiencies > 95 %, making it the process of choice for **panel-line welding in shipyards**. ### 2.2.5 Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW / TIG) - **Principle**: A **non-consumable tungsten electrode** produces the arc; filler is added manually if required; inert gas (Ar) shields. - **Shipyard Use**: **Root pass** of butt joints (especially when one side is inaccessible), stainless-steel pipework, aluminium superstructure joints, repair of castings. - **Limitation**: Slow, requires high operator skill — hence used only where weld quality is critical. ## 2.3 Welding Standards in Shipbuilding > [!IMPORTANT] > Ship welds are governed by a **hierarchy of standards**: International (ISO) → Regional/Society (IACS) → Classification Society Rules → Company Procedures. ### 2.3.1 Major International Standards | Standard | Issuing Body | Scope | |---|---|---| | **AWS D1.1** | American Welding Society | Structural welding — steel (used worldwide in shipbuilding) | | **AWS D1.4** | AWS | Reinforcing steel | | **AWS D1.5M/D1.5** | AWS | Bridge welding | | **AWS D3.6M** | AWS | Underwater welding | | **AWS A2.4** | AWS | Standard symbols for welding, brazing, and nondestructive examination | | **ISO 3834** | ISO | Quality requirements for fusion welding of metallic materials | | **ISO 9606** | ISO | Qualification testing of welders | | **ISO 15614** | ISO | Procedure qualification test (WPS/PQR) | | **ISO 2553** | ISO | Symbolic representation of welded joints | | **ISO 6520** | ISO | Classification of imperfections | | **ASME Section IX** | ASME | Welding procedure and performance qualification (boiler & pressure) | | **EN 1011** | CEN | Recommendations for welding metallic materials | ### 2.3.2 Classification Society Rules (IACS Members) - **DNV GL** (now DNV) — DNVGL-CG-0178 / Rules for Ships Pt.2 Ch.4 - **Lloyd's Register (LR)** — Pt.2 Ch.4 Welding - **American Bureau of Shipping (ABS)** — Rules for Materials and Welding - **Bureau Veritas (BV)** — Pt. B Ch. 4 - **Indian Register of Shipping (IRS)** — Rules & Regulations Pt.2 Ch.4 - **ClassNK, KR, CCS, RINA** — corresponding chapters ### 2.3.3 Standard Welding Procedure Specification (WPS) Chain $$\text{WPS (Procedure)} \;\longleftarrow\; \text{PQR (Qualification Record)} \;\longleftarrow\; \text{WPQ (Welder Performance Qualification)}$$ A **WPS** is qualified by a **PQR** (test coupon, mechanical & NDT tests) and each **welder** must hold a valid **WPQ** for the WPS scope. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Heat input** | $HI = \dfrac{60 \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000}$ $[\text{kJ/mm}]$ | Controls HAZ width & distortion | | 2 | **Deposition rate** | $DR = k \cdot I \cdot \dfrac{60}{1000}$ $[\text{kg/h}]$ | Process selection, productivity | | 3 | **Weld metal dilution** | $D\,\% = \dfrac{A_{\text{base}}}{A_{\text{base}}+A_{\text{filler}}}\times 100$ | Composition control in dissimilar welds | | 4 | **Leg size → Throat** (45° fillet) | $t = 0.707 \cdot z$ | Strength calculation | | 5 | **Throat area (fillet)** | $A = 0.707 \cdot z \cdot L$ | Shear strength of fillet | | 6 | **Heat input limit (class)** | $HI \le 3.5$ to $5.0$ kJ/mm | For AH36/DH36/EH36 to avoid HAZ softening | | 7 | **Interpass temperature limit** | $T_{ip} \le 200\text{–}250\,°\text{C}$ | Typical for C-Mn shipbuilding steels | | 8 | **WPS qualification range** (thickness) | $t_{\text{qualified}} = 2t$ or per ISO 15614 | Procedure coverage | | 9 | **Leg size for full-strength fillet** | $z = 0.707 \cdot t_{\text{plate}}$ | Match base-metal strength | | 10 | **Weld shrinkage allowance** | $\Delta L = 0.001\,\text{–}\,0.002 \times L$ per pass | Plate nesting allowance | Where, in formula 1: - $V$ = arc voltage (V), $I$ = welding current (A), $v$ = travel speed (mm/min). ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Significance - **Heat input** directly governs the **heat-affected zone (HAZ) microstructure**. Exceeding 5 kJ/mm on TMCP/AH36 plates reduces toughness — a leading cause of brittle fractures (e.g., Liberty Ships, 1940s). Modern rules cap HI strictly. - **Deposition rate** determines yard throughput: replacing SMAW with mechanised FCAW doubles productivity per welder. - **Interpass temperature control** prevents excessive HAZ softening and hydrogen cracking in thick-plate welds. --- # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat Input (HI) From First Principles Heat input is defined as the **arc energy supplied per unit length of weld**. The electrical energy supplied over time $t$ is: $$ E_{\text{elec}} = V \cdot I \cdot t \quad \text{[Joules]} $$ The **time** the arc spends on a unit length of weld is the inverse of travel speed: $$ t = \dfrac{L}{v} \quad \text{where } L = 1 \text{ mm}, \quad v \text{ in mm/s} $$ A more practical form uses **travel speed in mm/min**. For a length of $1$ mm: $$ t = \dfrac{1\,\text{mm}}{v\,[\text{mm/min}]} = \dfrac{60}{v}\;\text{s} $$ So the energy supplied per mm of weld: $$ E_{\text{per mm}} = V \cdot I \cdot \dfrac{60}{v}\;\text{[J/mm]} $$ Convert to kilojoules per millimetre and multiply by the conventional **arc efficiency factor** $\eta$: $$ \boxed{\,HI = \eta \cdot \dfrac{60 \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000}\;\text{[kJ/mm]}\,} $$ **Arc-efficiency values (typical):** $$ \eta_{\text{SMAW}} \approx 0.80,\quad \eta_{\text{GMAW}} \approx 0.80,\quad \eta_{\text{FCAW-G}} \approx 0.85,\quad \eta_{\text{SAW}} \approx 0.95 \text{–}1.0,\quad \eta_{\text{GTAW}} \approx 0.60 $$ ### Worked Example A **SAW** weld is made with $V = 32$ V, $I = 750$ A, travel speed $v = 480$ mm/min, and $\eta = 1.0$. Determine the heat input. $$ \begin{aligned} HI &= \eta \cdot \dfrac{60 \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000} \\[4pt] &= 1.0 \times \dfrac{60 \times 32 \times 750}{480 \times 1000} \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{1\,440\,000}{480\,000} \\[4pt] &= 3.0\;\text{kJ/mm} \end{aligned} $$ Since most class rules cap HI at **3.5 kJ/mm** for AH36 plates ≥ 25 mm, this weld is acceptable. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Throat Thickness of a 45° Fillet Weld For a fillet weld with leg size $z$ on each side, the **theoretical throat** $t$ is the perpendicular distance from the root to the hypotenuse. By trigonometry on the isoceles right triangle formed by the two legs and the weld face: $$ \begin{aligned} t^2 + t^2 &= z^2 \\ 2t^2 &= z^2 \\ t &= \dfrac{z}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{z}{1.4142} \\ \boxed{\,t &= 0.707 \cdot z\,} \end{aligned} $$ ### Worked Example A 10 mm leg-size fillet is welded in DH36 plate. Compute (a) throat thickness and (b) allowable shear load if weld metal allowable shear is $\tau_{allow} = 180$ MPa and weld length $L = 500$ mm. **(a)** $$ \begin{aligned} t &= 0.707 \times 10 = 7.07\;\text{mm} \end{aligned} $$ **(b)** $$ \begin{aligned} A &= t \cdot L = 7.07 \times 500 = 3535\;\text{mm}^2 \\[4pt] P &= \tau_{allow} \cdot A = 180 \times 3535 = 6.36 \times 10^{5}\;\text{N} \\[4pt] \boxed{\,P &\approx 636\;\text{kN}\,} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Fillet Weld Leg Size for Full Strength Match The fillet must develop at least the strength of the weaker plate joined. Equating throat shear capacity to plate tension capacity: $$ 0.707 \cdot z \cdot L \cdot \tau_w = t_p \cdot L \cdot \sigma_y $$ Solving for $z$: $$ \boxed{\,z = \dfrac{t_p \cdot \sigma_y}{0.707 \cdot \tau_w}\,} $$ Taking $\tau_w \approx 0.577 \sigma_y$ (von Mises) for ductile weld metal: $$ \begin{aligned} z &= \dfrac{t_p \cdot \sigma_y}{0.707 \times 0.577 \cdot \sigma_y} \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{t_p}{0.408} \\[4pt] \boxed{\,z &\approx 2.45 \cdot t_p \;\text{(upper bound)}\,} \end{aligned} $$ In practice, shipbuilding rules specify the fillet leg by **plate thickness bands**, not by this derivation alone. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Selection by Joint Type | Joint / Location | Position | Recommended Process | Reason | |---|---|---|---| | Panel-line butt (long) | Flat (1G) | **SAW** (twin-arc) | High deposition, deep penetration | | Panel-line butt (short) | Flat | **GMAW** | Flexible, fast set-up | | Hull erection butt | All-position | **FCAW-G** | High deposition out-of-position | | Stiffener-to-plate fillet | Horizontal (2F) | **FCAW-G** / GMAW | Productivity + positional | | Bulkhead-to-deck web | Vertical (3F) | **FCAW-G** | Out-of-position capability | | Root pass (one-side) | All | **GTAW** | Clean, defect-free root | | Repair / on-board | All | **SMAW** | Portability, simplicity | | Cladding (corrosion-resistant) | Flat | **SAW strip cladding** | High dilution control | --- # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Welder-to-Weld Qualification Chain ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Base Material Selection
AH36 DH36 EH36 per Class Rules] --> B[Define WPS
Process Preheating HI Interpass] B --> C[Fabricate PQR Test Coupon
per ISO 15614 or ASME IX] C --> D{Mech and NDT Tests} D -->|Pass| E[PQR Qualified] D -->|Fail| C E --> F[Approve WPS for Production] F --> G[Welder Qualification WPQ
per ISO 9606] G --> H{Welder Test} H -->|Pass| I[Welder Authorised
valid for WPS scope] H -->|Fail| G I --> J[Production Welding] J --> K[NDT 100 percent Visual
plus MT UT RT as required] K --> L{NDT Result} L -->|Accept| M[Welded Joint Approved] L -->|Defects| N[Repair per WPS] N --> J ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Function of Welding Symbols (AWS A2.4 / ISO 2553) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SYMBOL[AWS A2.4 Welding Symbol Structure] A1[Reference Line] --> A2[Arrow Side] A1 --> A3[Other Side] A2 --> B1[Weld Type Symbol
Fillet V Butt U etc] A3 --> B1 B1 --> C1[Size and Length] C1 --> D1[Contour Symbol] D1 --> E1[Supplementary Symbols
Backing Spacer etc] E1 --> F1[Tail Information
WPS Number Process] end ``` ## 4.3 Schematic Description — Anatomy of an AWS Fillet Weld Symbol > [!NOTE] > Because Mermaid cannot draw the exact geometric symbol, the structure is described analytically for the student. An AWS A2.4 fillet-weld symbol consists of: 1. **Reference line** — horizontal straight line. 2. **Arrow** — points to the joint, with **arrow side** (below the line) and **other side** (above the line). 3. **Fillet symbol** — an isoceles right triangle placed on the **side of the line where the weld is to be made**. 4. **Leg size** (in mm or inches) — placed to the **left of the symbol** on the same side. 5. **Length of weld** (in mm) — placed to the **right of the symbol**. 6. **Pitch (centre-to-centre distance)** — for intermittent welds, placed to the **right of length**, separated by a hyphen. 7. **Contour symbol** — flush (straight line) or convex (curve) symbol above the fillet. 8. **Field-weld flag** — small flag on the arrow-tail end → "weld to be made in field". 9. **Tail** — gives WPS number, process, or reference. ### Example Symbol Reading > A symbol showing a fillet triangle **below the reference line**, leg `8`, length `100-300` means: > - **Fillet weld of 8 mm leg size** > - **100 mm long welds, with 300 mm pitch (intermittent)** - **Weld located on the arrow side**. ## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Modern Shipyard Welding Cell ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph YARD[Modern Mechanised Shipyard Welding Cell] P1[Power Source
Inverter 600 to 1500 A] --> W1[Wire Feeder] W1 --> G1[Welding Gun or Torch] G1 --> ARC[Arc and Weld Pool] G2[Gas Cylinder or Flux Hopper] --> G1 M1[Mechanised Travel Carriage
or Robot Gantry] --> G1 ARC --> J1[Workpiece Plate] J1 --> CLAMP[Fit up and Clamping Jig] M1 --> CTRL[PLC or Robot Controller] CTRL --> P1 CTRL --> M1 CLAMP --> J1 end ``` --- # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions ### Q1. Define "heat input" in arc welding and state its typical class-rule limit for AH36 shipbuilding steel. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 model]` **Model Answer:** Heat input is the arc energy delivered per unit length of weld, expressed as $$ HI = \eta \cdot \dfrac{60 \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000}\;\text{kJ/mm} $$ where $V$ is voltage (V), $I$ is current (A), $v$ is travel speed (mm/min), and $\eta$ is arc efficiency. For **AH36/DH36/EH36** plates with thickness ≥ 25 mm, classification societies (DNV, LR, ABS) typically limit **HI ≤ 3.5 kJ/mm** to maintain HAZ toughness and avoid under-bead cracking. Higher values cause excessive grain coarsening in the HAZ and reduce impact toughness at service temperature. --- ### Q2. List any four welding processes commonly used in modern shipbuilding and give one typical application for each. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 model]` **Model Answer:** | # | Process | Typical Shipbuilding Application | |---|---|---| | 1 | **SAW** (Submerged Arc Welding) | Long flat butt welds in panel-line production | | 2 | **FCAW-G** (Gas-shielded Flux-Cored Arc Welding) | Vertical-up welding of bulkhead webs | | 3 | **GMAW** (Gas Metal Arc Welding) | Mechanised panel-line butt welds and fillet welds | | 4 | **SMAW** (Shielded Metal Arc Welding) | Hull erection, on-board repair and short positional welds | | 5 | **GTAW** (Gas Tungsten Arc Welding) | Root pass of one-sided butt welds and stainless piping | --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions ### Question A (14 Marks) — Welding Processes and Heat Input **(a)** Compare **SMAW**, **GMAW**, **FCAW** and **SAW** on the basis of: (i) electrode type, (ii) shielding medium, (iii) deposition rate, (iv) typical shipbuilding application, and (v) positional capability. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A single-pass **SAW** weld is made on a 20 mm AH36 plate butt joint using $V = 30$ V, $I = 600$ A, $v = 450$ mm/min. Take arc efficiency $\eta = 1.0$. Compute the heat input and state whether it satisfies the class limit of 3.5 kJ/mm. **[7 Marks]** --- #### Model Solution **(a) Comparison Table [7 Marks]** | Parameter | SMAW | GMAW | FCAW | SAW | |---|---|---|---|---| | Electrode | Flux-coated stick | Continuous solid wire | Continuous tubular wire | Continuous solid wire/strip | | Shielding | Flux decomposition (gas + slag) | External inert/active gas (Ar/CO₂) | Flux core ± external gas | Granular flux blanket | | Deposition Rate | 1–3 kg/h | 3–8 kg/h | 5–15 kg/h | 15–50 kg/h | | Shipyard Application | Erection, repair, short welds | Panel-line, mechanised butt welds | Vertical webs, positional fillets | Long flat butts, panel-line, cladding | | Positional Capability | Excellent (all positions) | Good (with pulsed transfer) | Excellent (with appropriate wire) | Limited to flat / horizontal-fillet (1G / 1F / 2F) | Award **1 mark** per correctly filled row plus **1 mark** for overall comparative summary sentence. **(b) Heat Input Calculation [7 Marks]** Step 1 — Write the heat-input formula [2 marks]: $$ HI = \eta \cdot \dfrac{60 \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000} $$ Step 2 — Substitute values [2 marks]: $$ HI = 1.0 \cdot \dfrac{60 \times 30 \times 600}{450 \times 1000} $$ Step 3 — Evaluate [2 marks]: $$ HI = \dfrac{1\,080\,000}{450\,000} = 2.4\;\text{kJ/mm} $$ Step 4 — Compare with class limit [1 mark]: $$ 2.4\;\text{kJ/mm} \;<\; 3.5\;\text{kJ/mm} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Acceptable per class rules.} $$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Welding Standards, Symbols and WPS **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the components of an **AWS A2.4 welding symbol** for a fillet weld. Indicate how the symbol distinguishes between (i) weld side, (ii) weld length and pitch, and (iii) field vs shop weld. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Discuss the role of **WPS**, **PQR** and **WPQ** in shipbuilding quality assurance as per **ISO 3834** / **ISO 15614** / **ISO 9606**. Why is this hierarchy essential for classification society certification? **[7 Marks]** --- #### Model Solution **(a) AWS A2.4 Fillet Welding Symbol [7 Marks]** A welding symbol is read from the **arrow** to the **tail**. Key elements: 1. **Reference Line** — horizontal axis of the symbol. [1 mark] 2. **Arrow** — connects the symbol to the joint location on the drawing. [1 mark] 3. **Arrow side vs other side** — weld placed **below** the reference line is on the **arrow side**; weld placed **above** is on the **other side**. [1 mark] 4. **Fillet triangle** — isoceles right triangle placed on the side where the weld is to be deposited. [1 mark] 5. **Leg size** — written to the **left** of the fillet symbol on the same side. [1 mark] 6. **Length and pitch** — written to the **right** of the fillet symbol as e.g. `100–300` meaning 100 mm long welds at 300 mm centre-to-centre pitch. [1 mark] 7. **Field-weld flag** — a small flag at the junction of the reference line and the arrow indicates "weld to be made in the field (during erection on board)". [1 mark] > **Marking Tip**: Sketch must show reference line, arrow, fillet triangle, leg, length, and field flag. A text-only answer without clear reference to the geometry loses 2 marks. **(b) WPS / PQR / WPQ Hierarchy [7 Marks]** 1. **WPS (Welding Procedure Specification)** — A qualified document specifying all welding variables: process, joint type, base metal, thickness range, filler, shielding, preheat, interpass, heat input, position, electrical parameters. **2 marks** 2. **PQR (Procedure Qualification Record)** — The mechanical-test and NDT evidence (tensile, bend, impact, macro, hardness) recorded on a test coupon that **proves the WPS can produce acceptable welds**. ISO 15614 prescribes the test matrix. **2 marks** 3. **WPQ (Welder Performance Qualification)** — A test per ISO 9606 that proves an **individual welder** can produce welds to the WPS; valid for the WPS scope (material, thickness, position). **1 mark** 4. **Hierarchy of authority** — Classification societies (DNV, LR, ABS, IRS) require **WPS → PQR → WPQ → production weld log**. This ensures: traceability, repeatability, weld quality independent of operator, and ability to assign defect liability. **1 mark** 5. **Link to ISO 3834** — ISO 3834 sets the **Quality Management System** around these documents; classification societies accept ISO 3834-2 (full quality) as equivalent to their own approval. **1 mark** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Mixing up arc efficiency** — Students often write $HI = \dfrac{60VI}{v}$ without $\eta$, losing **1 mark**. For SMAW/FCAW, $\eta < 1$. > 2. **Wrong symbol side** — Placing the fillet triangle on the wrong side of the reference line reverses the meaning; examiners award **0** for the symbol interpretation sub-question. > 3. **Confusing WPS and PQR** — WPS is the *procedure document*; PQR is the *test record* that qualifies it. Confusing the two costs **2 marks**. > 4. **Forgetting units in heat input** — Answer must include **kJ/mm**; numerical value alone is incomplete. > 5. **Position-limitation oversight** — Many students mark SAW as "all-position"; it is restricted to flat (1G/1F) and horizontal-fillet (2F). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist - **Welding** = fusion joining with/without filler and pressure; dominant joining method in shipbuilding (>90 % of joints). - **Heat input** $HI = \eta \cdot 60VI / (v \cdot 1000)$ in kJ/mm; limit 3.5 kJ/mm for AH36/DH36. - **SMAW** — manual, all-position, low deposition; workhorse for ship erection. - **GMAW** — continuous wire + external gas; mechanised panel-line welds. - **FCAW-G** — tubular wire + gas; out-of-position high-deposition process. - **SAW** — flux-submerged; highest deposition (15–50 kg/h); flat/horizontal only. - **GTAW** — non-consumable tungsten + inert gas; root pass and stainless/aluminium. - **ESW / EGW** — vertical thick-plate joints. - **Standards**: AWS D1.1 (structural), ISO 3834 (QMS), ISO 15614 (PQR), ISO 9606 (WPQ), ISO 2553 & AWS A2.4 (symbols). - **Classification Society Rules** — DNV, LR, ABS, BV, IRS — Pt.2 Ch.4 in most. - **WPS / PQR / WPQ** — procedure → qualification record → welder qualification. - **Welding symbol** — reference line, arrow, fillet symbol, leg, length, pitch, field flag. - **Interpass temperature** ≤ 200–250 °C for C-Mn shipbuilding steels. - **Deposition rate** $DR = k \cdot I \cdot 60/1000$ kg/h; ~90 % arc-on time for SAW. - **Throat of fillet** $t = 0.707 z$. - **Heat input** controls HAZ microstructure, distortion and residual stress. --- > **END OF NOTES — Module 3 / Welding in Shipbuilding: Methods, Standards, Symbols**

topics.title: Erection of ship's hull: Handling of pre-assembled units in the erection area, different methods of hull assembly, block erection methods (floor, ring and pyramid), single/multi point erection methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST631 - SHIP PRODUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Erection of ship's hull: Handling of pre-assembled units in the erection area, different methods of hull assembly, block erection methods (floor, ring and pyramid), single/multi point erection methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Hull Erection** is defined as the *final stage of ship hull construction* in which pre-assembled structural units (blocks, sections, or sub-assemblies) fabricated in workshops, panel lines, and pre-assembly shops are transported, positioned, lifted, and welded together in the **erection berth, building dock, or slipway** to produce a complete, integrated ship's hull structure.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the process is broadly classified under three work domains:

| Domain | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Pre-assembly Area** | Joining small sub-units (panels, stiffeners, brackets) into larger **assembled units** |
| **Erection Area** | Joining assembled units into **blocks** and **grand blocks** |
| **Berth / Dock** | Final joining of blocks into the **complete hull** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition Box:**
> *Hull erection in modular shipbuilding refers to the systematic lifting, translation, alignment, and joining (welding) of pre-fabricated hull blocks to form the complete ship's structure. The chosen erection sequence directly determines the **welding distortion control**, **dimensional accuracy**, and **overall build duration**.*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Building a Ship Like a Lego Castle"

Imagine you are constructing a massive 3D Lego castle. Instead of attaching every tiny brick directly on the table:

- You **first assemble large Lego wall sections** (panels) on a side table — this is the **panel fabrication shop**.
- You **join several wall sections together** to form a complete room module — this is the **pre-assembly area** producing **blocks**.
- Finally, you **lift each room module with a crane** and place it on the foundation — this is the **erection area/berth**.

The ship is the "castle," the blocks are the "room modules," and the cranes are the giant hands placing each piece in position. The *order* in which you stack them (from the ground up, from the middle outward, or in rings) defines the **erection method**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants, Standard Metrics & Key Parameters

The following engineering constants and standard values govern hull erection planning:

- **Acceleration factor during lifting:** $\alpha = 1.10 \text{ to } 1.25$ (10–25 % dynamic allowance over static weight)
- **Standard sling safety factor (SWL):** **5 : 1 to 6 : 1** for steel wire ropes
- **Block weight range (modern shipyards):** **50 tonnes – 1500 tonnes** per block
- **Maximum gantry crane lift capacity (modern yards):** **up to 1000–2000 tonnes** (single lift)
- **Typical block dimensional envelope:** Length **20–40 m**, Height **12–28 m**, Width **15–30 m**
- **Erection cycle time per block:** **2 to 8 hours** depending on block size
- **Welding shrinkage allowance per joint:** **1–2 mm** (per metre of weld)
- **Minimum clearance between blocks during fit-up:** **20–50 mm** for root gap

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these numbers matter in KTU exams:** Examiners frequently test whether you know the *dynamic amplification factor* applied to static block weight when computing crane capacity. Always remember: **Required SWL ≥ (Static Load) × (Dynamic Factor) × (Safety Factor)**.

## 1.4 Conceptual Map of the Hull Erection Workflow

The complete hull erection workflow follows a strict sequence:

$$
\text{Sub-assemblies} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Panels} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Assembled Units} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Blocks} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Grand Blocks} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Hull}
$$

Each arrow represents a **transport + lifting + alignment + welding** cycle. KTU 2024 Scheme examiners emphasise this *flow logic* because it underpins every question on production planning.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Logical Pillars of Hull Erection

Hull erection, irrespective of method, rests on five operational pillars. Every KTU question maps to at least one pillar.

### Pillar 1 — Unit Identification & Logistics
Each block is assigned a unique **block number** (e.g., A-12, B-07) and a **block weight tag**. The erection sequence is pre-planned using **Network Planning (CPM/PERT)** to optimise crane utilisation.

### Pillar 2 — Transportation to Erection Site
- **Self-propelled modular transporters (SPMTs)** — for heavy blocks (>200 t).
- **Rail-bound trolleys** — for medium blocks in covered halls.
- **Crawler-mounted transporters** — for outdoor berths.
- **Floating transfer (pontoon method)** — for dry-dock construction.

### Pillar 3 — Lifting & Handling
Two principal strategies exist: **single-point lifting** and **multi-point lifting**. The choice depends on block geometry, rigidity, and crane availability.

### Pillar 4 — Positioning & Alignment
- **Sighting marks** on blocks align with the **theoretical reference line (datum line)**.
- **Hydraulic jacks, chain blocks, and wedges** fine-tune position before tack welding.
- Tolerances: longitudinal **±2 mm/m**, transverse **±3 mm/m** (typical).

### Pillar 5 — Joining (Welding)
- **Tack welding** → **CO₂ arc welding / SAW / FCAW** for permanent joints.
- **Welding sequence** is symmetric to control distortion.
- **NDT** (Ultrasonic, Radiographic) is performed on critical joints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Focus:** When asked to "explain the steps involved in hull erection," always present your answer using these five pillars. Examiners reward *structured, sequential logic*.

## 2.2 Methods of Hull Assembly — Comprehensive Classification

Modern shipyards use one (or a hybrid) of the following **four primary methods**:

| S.No | Method | Description | Block Size |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Piece-Small Method (Conventional)** | Every plate and stiffener fitted directly on berth. Outdated. | Sub-assemblies |
| 2 | **Section Method** | Medium transverse/longitudinal sections built on the berth. | 50–200 t |
| 3 | **Block Method (Modular)** | Complete 3D blocks built off-berth, then erected. Industry standard. | 200–1500 t |
| 4 | **Grand Block / Unit Method** | Entire bow, stern, or midship built as a single mega-block. | 1000–10000 t |

### Comparative Analysis (KTU-Favourite Table)

| Parameter | Piece-Small | Section | Block | Grand Block |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Welding on berth (%)** | 90–100 | 60–80 | 25–40 | 10–20 |
| **Build duration** | Very long | Long | Medium | Short |
| **Labour on berth** | Maximum | High | Low | Minimum |
| **Accuracy** | Low | Medium | High | Very High |
| **Initial investment** | Low | Medium | High | Very High |
| **Modern yard use** | None | Rare | Standard | Growing |

## 2.3 Block Erection Methods — The Three Classical Sequences

This is the **core KTU Module 3 topic**. There are three classical sequences:

### 2.3.1 Floor (Bottom-Up) Erection Method

**Logic:** Erect blocks starting from the **keel and bottom shell**, then progressively upwards to side shells, decks, and superstructure.

**Sequence of erection:**

$$
\text{Keel block} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Bottom shell blocks} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Side shell blocks} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Deck blocks} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Superstructure}
$$

**Why and How:**

- **Why chosen:** Provides a stable, gravity-centred base. Welding shrinkage is uniformly distributed downward.
- **How it works:** The keel is laid on the berth with maximum precision (datum). Each subsequent block rests *on* the previous one — naturally self-supporting during fit-up.
- **Best suited for:** Tankers, bulk carriers, and any vessel with a strong flat bottom.

### 2.3.2 Ring Erection Method (Transverse Ring Method)

**Logic:** Complete **transverse ring sections** (one frame spacing wide, full depth — bottom + two sides + deck) are pre-assembled off-berth, then erected like a stack of rings from midship towards bow and stern.

**Sequence of erection:**

$$
\text{Midship ring (Ring 0)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Adjacent rings forward} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Adjacent rings aft} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Bow block} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Stern block}
$$

**Why and How:**

- **Why chosen:** Maximises pre-fabrication work; the hull becomes self-supporting at the earliest stage.
- **How it works:** Each ring is built complete in the pre-assembly shop, lifted as a unit, and joined to the previous ring at the **ring-to-ring boundary joints**.
- **Best suited for:** Submarines, smaller naval vessels, and modular yards with limited crane reach.

### 2.3.3 Pyramid (Inverted Pyramid / Tower) Erection Method

**Logic:** A **central tower block** (typically amidships, full breadth and depth) is erected first. Successive blocks are then added **outward and upward** in a pyramid shape.

**Sequence of erection:**

$$
\text{Central tower block} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Adjacent blocks laterally} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Upper blocks above} \;\rightarrow\; \text{End blocks (bow/stern)}
$$

**Why and How:**

- **Why chosen:** Provides maximum stability early; the central tower acts as a structural backbone supporting all subsequent blocks.
- **How it works:** The tower block is exceptionally heavy and rigid. Lifting gear can work symmetrically, halving crane load swings.
- **Best suited for:** VLCCs, capesize bulk carriers, and large container ships where amidships strength is critical.

### 2.3.4 Comparative Matrix — Floor vs. Ring vs. Pyramid

| Parameter | Floor Method | Ring Method | Pyramid Method |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **First block erected** | Keel / Bottom | Midship ring | Central tower |
| **Direction of growth** | Upward | Forward + Aft | Outward + Upward |
| **Berth stability (early stage)** | Excellent | Good | Excellent |
| **Welding access** | Moderate | Excellent | Difficult (crowded centre) |
| **Suitability for inclined berth** | Best | Poor | Good |
| **Suitability for level berth/dock** | Good | Excellent | Good |
| **Crane requirement** | Medium-high | Medium | Very high (central tower weight) |
| **Distortion control** | Easiest | Moderate | Difficult |
| **Most common in modern yards** | Yes | Rare | Yes (hybrid) |

## 2.4 Single-Point vs. Multi-Point Erection Methods

### 2.4.1 Single-Point Erection (Single-Lift Method)

A **single crane + single lifting point** lifts the block using a **lifting beam (spreader bar)** with slings attached to one central pad-eye on the block.

**Governing equation for sling tension:**

$$
T = \frac{W \cdot \alpha}{n \cdot \cos\theta}
$$

Where:
- $T$ = tension in each sling leg (N)
- $W$ = static block weight (N)
- $\alpha$ = dynamic amplification factor (**1.10 to 1.25**)
- $n$ = number of sling legs
- $\theta$ = sling angle from vertical (degrees)

**Advantages:** Fast, simple rigging, lower crane complexity.
**Disadvantages:** Block tends to swing; cannot lift long/flat blocks safely.

### 2.4.2 Multi-Point Erection (Multi-Lift / Tandem Method)

Either **two cranes in tandem** or **one crane with a multi-point spreader beam** lifts the block via **four or more pad-eyes**.

**Equilibrium condition (4-point lift):**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{4} T_i = W \cdot \alpha \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{4} T_i \cdot x_i = W \cdot \alpha \cdot \bar{x}
$$

Where $x_i$ is the horizontal distance of each pad-eye from the block's centre of gravity and $\bar{x}$ is the CG offset.

**Advantages:** Stable, controls swing, lifts long/flat blocks (e.g., engine-room casing blocks).
**Disadvantages:** Complex rigging; requires precise CG calculation.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| S.No | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Sling tension | $T = \dfrac{W \cdot \alpha}{n \cdot \cos\theta}$ | N or kN | Single-point lift design |
| 2 | Dynamic amplification | $\alpha = 1.10 \text{ to } 1.25$ | dimensionless | Crane SWL selection |
| 3 | Required crane SWL | $\text{SWL} \geq W \cdot \alpha \cdot f_s$ | tonnes | Crane sizing |
| 4 | Wire-rope safety factor | $f_s = 5 \text{ to } 6$ | dimensionless | Sling specification |
| 5 | Hook approach factor | $\text{Hook load} = W \cdot \alpha \cdot f_s$ | tonnes | Hook capacity |
| 6 | Block CG offset check | $\vert \bar{x} \vert \leq \dfrac{L}{20}$ | m | Stability check |
| 7 | Welding shrinkage (linear) | $\Delta L = k \cdot L$ | mm | Distortion control |
| 8 | Erection cycle time | $t_c = t_{lift} + t_{align} + t_{weld}$ | hours | Planning |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall Alert:** Never confuse *dynamic amplification factor* ($\alpha$) with *safety factor* ($f_s$). They are **multiplied**, not added. A common student error in KTU exams is to write SWL = $W + \alpha + f_s$, which is wrong.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Welding distortion control:** Choosing *floor erection* for distortion-sensitive hulls (LNG carriers) reduces angular distortion by ~30 %.
- **Project scheduling:** The *ring erection* method can reduce total build time by up to **15–20 %** for submarines.
- **Cost economics:** The *pyramid method* allows parallel work on 8–12 blocks simultaneously, optimising yard labour utilisation.
- **Safety engineering:** Multi-point lifting reduces **swing-induced accidents** by ~70 % compared to single-point for long blocks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Single-Point Sling Tension Formula

We begin with a rigid block of weight $W$ suspended by $n$ identical sling legs, each making angle $\theta$ with the vertical. The block is in static equilibrium (we later multiply by $\alpha$ for dynamics).

### Step 1: Force Equilibrium (Vertical Direction)

The vertical component of each sling's tension must support the block's weight:

$$
n \cdot T \cdot \cos\theta = W
$$

Rearranging to isolate $T$:

$$
T = \frac{W}{n \cdot \cos\theta}
$$

### Step 2: Introduce the Dynamic Amplification Factor

During lifting, the block experiences jerking, swinging, and acceleration. KTU design codes prescribe a multiplier $\alpha$:

$$
T = \frac{W \cdot \alpha}{n \cdot \cos\theta}
$$

### Step 3: Apply the Safety Factor $f_s$

For procurement specification, we must include the wire-rope safety factor:

$$
T_{\text{design}} = \frac{W \cdot \alpha \cdot f_s}{n \cdot \cos\theta}
$$

### Step 4: Numerical Illustration (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A block weighing $W = 200$ tonnes is to be lifted using **4 sling legs**, each inclined at $\theta = 30^{\circ}$ from vertical. Take $\alpha = 1.20$ and $f_s = 5$. Compute (a) tension in each sling, (b) required SWL of each sling, and (c) total hook load.

**Solution:**

Convert weight to kiloNewtons: $W = 200 \times 9.81 = 1962$ kN.

Part (a):

$$
T = \frac{W \cdot \alpha}{n \cdot \cos\theta}
$$

$$
T = \frac{1962 \times 1.20}{4 \times \cos 30^{\circ}}
$$

$$
\cos 30^{\circ} = 0.866
$$

$$
T = \frac{2354.4}{4 \times 0.866} = \frac{2354.4}{3.464}
$$

$$
T \approx 679.7 \text{ kN per leg}
$$

Part (b):

$$
T_{\text{SWL}} = T \times f_s = 679.7 \times 5 = 3398.5 \text{ kN}
$$

Part (c):

$$
\text{Hook Load} = n \cdot T = 4 \times 679.7 = 2718.8 \text{ kN} \approx 277 \text{ tonnes}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-check with crane requirement:** Total hook load = $W \cdot \alpha = 200 \times 1.20 = 240$ tonnes. Our calculated 277 t includes the additional dynamic amplification from sling geometry — engineers must use the **larger of the two** values for safe crane selection.

## 3.2 Derivation of Multi-Point Lift Equilibrium (4-Point Case)

For a rectangular block lifted at four corners, each pad-eye carries a different tension if the CG is not perfectly centred.

### Step 1: Define Geometry

Let the block be rectangular with corners at pad-eyes 1, 2, 3, 4. The CG is at $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$ measured from one corner.

### Step 2: Vertical Equilibrium

$$
T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + T_4 = W \cdot \alpha
$$

### Step 3: Moment Equilibrium about X-axis

$$
T_1 \cdot y_1 + T_2 \cdot y_2 + T_3 \cdot y_3 + T_4 \cdot y_4 = W \cdot \alpha \cdot \bar{y}
$$

### Step 4: Moment Equilibrium about Y-axis

$$
T_1 \cdot x_1 + T_2 \cdot x_2 + T_3 \cdot x_3 + T_4 \cdot x_4 = W \cdot \alpha \cdot \bar{x}
$$

### Step 5: Solve the Three Simultaneous Equations

Using standard matrix form:

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\
y_1 & y_2 & y_3 & y_4 \\
x_1 & x_2 & x_3 & x_4
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} T_1 \\ T_2 \\ T_3 \\ T_4 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} W \cdot \alpha \\ W \cdot \alpha \cdot \bar{y} \\ W \cdot \alpha \cdot \bar{x} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Solving yields individual sling tensions. **KTU 2024 exam tip:** You will not be asked to solve a 4×4 matrix in 14 marks; instead, expect a *qualitative* explanation of how CG offset shifts tension distribution.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting the Erection Method

The following decision matrix is widely used in KTU Module 3 examination answers:

| Selection Criterion | Floor Method | Ring Method | Pyramid Method |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Vessel type: Tanker | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ |
| Vessel type: Container | ★★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★★ |
| Vessel type: Submarine | ★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ |
| Vessel type: Naval Frigate | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ |
| Berth type: Inclined (Slipway) | ★★★★★ | ★ | ★★★ |
| Berth type: Level (Building dock) | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ |
| Crane availability: Single large | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★ |
| Crane availability: Multiple gantry | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| Distortion control priority | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ |
| Build duration priority | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ |

> [!TIP]
> **How to use this matrix in a KTU 14-mark answer:** Always state *three selection criteria*, *justify with at least one engineering reason each*, and *conclude with the recommended method*. Examiners reward structured justification, not just naming the method.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison — Single-Point vs. Multi-Point Lifting

| Engineering Aspect | Single-Point Lift | Multi-Point Lift |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Rigging time | Minimal (~15 min) | Significant (~45–90 min) |
| Crane requirement | 1 crane | 1 or 2 cranes |
| Block swing risk | High | Low |
| CG sensitivity | Critical | Less critical (geometry spreads load) |
| Block rigidity requirement | Low | High (must resist sling tension differential) |
| Maximum practical block weight | Up to ~800 t (single crane) | Up to 10,000+ t (tandem gantries) |
| Typical application | Engine-room casing blocks, small panels | Bow/stern blocks, double-bottom grand blocks |

## 3.5 Detailed Step-by-Step Process of Floor Erection (14-Mark Answer Skeleton)

**Step 1 — Keel Block Placement:** The keel block (or flat bottom panel) is positioned on the berth with reference to the **base line (datum line)**. Alignment checked using theodolite/theodolite + laser.

**Step 2 — Bottom Shell Block Erection:** Bottom shell blocks are lifted using gantry crane, positioned adjacent to the keel block, and tack-welded. Symmetric erection on port and starboard sides.

**Step 3 — Side Shell Block Erection:** Side shell blocks erected using multi-point lift (due to large planar dimensions). Tack welded to bottom shell. Internal stiffeners pre-installed.

**Step 4 — Deck Block Erection:** Main deck blocks (some with deckhouse base) lifted into position. Multi-point lift essential for stability.

**Step 5 — Superstructure Block Erection:** Upper decks, bridge wings, and funnels erected. Single-point or multi-point depending on size.

**Step 6 — Alignment and Final Welding:** All blocks aligned to within tolerance; full-penetration welds performed using SAW/FCAW.

**Step 7 — Inspection and NDT:** Visual, ultrasonic, and radiographic inspection of all critical joints.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hull Erection Workflow (Adaptive Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Panel Fabrication Shop] --> B[Pre-Assembly Area]
    B --> C[Assembled Units]
    C --> D[Block Assembly Shop]
    D --> E[Completed Blocks]
    E --> F[SPMT or Rail Transport]
    F --> G[Erection Berth or Dock]
    G --> H{Single Point or Multi Point Lift}
    H --> I[Block Positioning and Alignment]
    I --> J[Tack Welding]
    J --> K[Full Penetration Welding]
    K --> L[NDT Inspection]
    L --> M[Complete Hull Structure]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Floor Erection Sequence (Bottom-Up)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Lay Keel Block on Berth] --> S2[Step 2: Erect Bottom Shell Blocks Port and Starboard]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Erect Side Shell Blocks]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Erect Main Deck Blocks]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Erect Superstructure Blocks]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Alignment and Final Welding]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Hull Complete]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Ring Erection Sequence (Midship Outward)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Step 1: Pre-assemble Transverse Ring in Shop] --> R2[Step 2: Erect Midship Ring Ring Zero]
    R2 --> R3[Step 3: Erect Ring Forward of Midship]
    R2 --> R4[Step 4: Erect Ring Aft of Midship]
    R3 --> R5[Step 5: Continue Rings Toward Bow]
    R4 --> R6[Step 6: Continue Rings Toward Stern]
    R5 --> R7[Step 7: Erect Bow Block]
    R6 --> R8[Step 8: Erect Stern Block]
    R7 --> R9[Step 9: Hull Joined Complete]
    R8 --> R9
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Pyramid Erection Sequence (Central Tower Outward)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Erect Central Tower Block Amidships] --> P2[Step 2: Erect Adjacent Blocks Laterally]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Erect Upper Blocks Above Central Tower]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Erect End Blocks Bow and Stern]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Complete Pyramid Structure Forms Hull]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Single-Point vs. Multi-Point Lifting Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Block Ready for Lifting] --> L2{Block Weight and Dimensions}
    L2 -->|Weight less 200t and Compact| L3[Single Point Lift via Spreader Beam]
    L2 -->|Weight greater 200t or Long Flat Block| L4[Multi Point Lift via Four Point Spreader]
    L3 --> L5{Block CG Centred}
    L5 -->|Yes| L6[Proceed with Single Crane Lift]
    L5 -->|No| L7[Recalculate CG or Use Two Crane Tandem]
    L4 --> L8{Number of Cranes Available}
    L8 -->|Two Gantries| L9[Tandem Lift Operation]
    L8 -->|Single Gantry with Spreader| L10[Multi Point Spreader Beam Lift]
```

## 4.6 Mermaid Block Diagram — Handling of Pre-Assembled Units in the Erection Area

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U1[Sub Assemblies from Workshops] --> U2[Pre Assembly Bay]
    U2 --> U3[Assembled Units Panels with Stiffeners]
    U3 --> U4[Block Assembly Area]
    U4 --> U5[Completed Blocks]
    U5 --> U6[Storage Yard or Direct to Berth]
    U6 --> U7[Erection Area Berth or Dock]
    U7 --> U8[Block Lifting by Gantry or Tower Crane]
    U8 --> U9[Block Alignment using Theodolite or Laser]
    U9 --> U10[Tack Welding and Full Welding]
    U10 --> U11[Complete Hull]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip (for KTU exams):** When asked to "draw a flowchart of the erection process," always include at least the following nodes: *Fabrication → Pre-assembly → Block → Transport → Lifting → Alignment → Welding → Hull*. Examiners give full marks only when all these nodes are explicitly shown.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define hull erection in modular shipbuilding. Mention any four pre-assembly activities carried out in the erection area before final block lifting.**

**Model Answer:**

Hull erection is the final phase of hull construction in which pre-fabricated blocks are lifted, positioned, aligned, and welded together in the erection berth or building dock to form the complete ship's hull.

Four pre-assembly activities in the erection area:

1. **Panel sub-assembly** — joining small plates and stiffeners into panels.
2. **Unit assembly** — joining panels into 3D sub-units (e.g., double-bottom unit).
3. **Block assembly** — joining units into complete blocks (200–1500 t).
4. **Grand block assembly** — joining blocks into mega-blocks representing sections of the ship.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between single-point lifting and multi-point lifting of hull blocks. State two applications of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Single-Point Lifting | Multi-Point Lifting |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Number of lifting points | One central pad-eye | Four or more pad-eyes |
| Rigging complexity | Low | High |
| Block swing risk | High | Low |
| Block weight limit | Up to ~800 t | Up to 10,000+ t |

**Single-point applications:** (1) Small engine-room casing blocks, (2) Compact deckhouse modules.
**Multi-point applications:** (1) Long flat bottom blocks, (2) Grand bow/stern blocks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**a)** Explain in detail the **Floor (Bottom-Up) erection method** of hull assembly. Discuss its advantages, limitations, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **Ring Erection Method** of hull assembly. Compare it with the Floor method. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Definition:** Floor erection, also called the bottom-up method, is a hull erection sequence in which the keel and bottom shell blocks are laid first, followed by the progressive upward erection of side shells, decks, and superstructure.

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**

1. **Keel Block Placement:** The keel block (or flat bottom panel) is positioned on the building berth with reference to the **base line (datum line)**. Accuracy is paramount as it sets the alignment for all subsequent blocks.
2. **Bottom Shell Erection:** Bottom shell blocks (port and starboard) are erected symmetrically, tack-welded to the keel block, and then full-welded.
3. **Side Shell Erection:** Side shell blocks (which may include inner skin and stiffeners pre-installed) are lifted using multi-point spreaders due to their large planar dimensions.
4. **Deck Block Erection:** Main deck blocks are positioned. For tankers, the deck may include pre-installed piping and outfitting.
5. **Superstructure Erection:** Deckhouse, bridge wings, and funnel are erected last.
6. **Final Welding and Inspection:** Symmetric welding sequence is followed to control distortion. NDT performed.

**Advantages:**

- Excellent **gravity stability** at every stage of erection.
- Welding shrinkage is naturally directed downward, **minimising distortion**.
- **Easiest alignment** since each new block rests on a previously erected one.
- Suitable for **inclined berths (slipways)**.

**Limitations:**

- **Berth occupation time is long** — earlier blocks must be complete before upper work starts.
- **Limited parallel work** — vertical build sequence restricts concurrency.
- **Outfitting work is delayed** until upper blocks are welded.

**Applications:** Tankers, bulk carriers, VLCCs.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Ring Erection Method — Description:**

In the ring erection method, complete **transverse ring sections** (each typically one frame spacing wide and full hull depth) are pre-assembled in the pre-assembly shop, transported to the erection berth, and erected one by one starting from amidships and progressing forward and aft.

**Sketch Description (text-based, since graphical drawing is the student's task):**

A horizontal hull is shown with vertical lines representing frame stations. The midship ring (Ring 0) is at the centre. Rings -1, -2, ... extend toward the bow on one side; Rings +1, +2, ... extend toward the stern on the other. Each ring is a complete circular (or U-shaped) section including bottom, two sides, and deck.

**Comparison with Floor Method:**

| Aspect | Floor Method | Ring Method |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Direction of growth | Upward | Forward + Aft |
| Pre-fabrication level | Medium | Very high |
| Welding on berth | High | Low |
| Distortion control | Easy | Moderate |
| Best for inclined berths | Yes | No |
| Build duration | Longer | Shorter |
| Block weight range | 50–500 t typical | 100–800 t typical |

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**a)** With suitable diagrams, explain the **Pyramid Erection Method** of hull assembly. State its advantages and disadvantages. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A hull block weighs **350 tonnes**. It is to be lifted using **4 wire-rope slings** each inclined at **45°** to the vertical. The dynamic amplification factor is **1.20** and the rope safety factor is **5**. Compute the **(i)** tension in each sling, **(ii)** design tension per sling, and **(iii)** total hook load on the crane. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Pyramid Erection Method:**

The pyramid erection method is a hull erection sequence in which a **central tower block** (typically amidships, extending the full depth and breadth of the hull) is erected first. Subsequent blocks are added **outward and upward** in a pyramid configuration.

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**

1. **Central Tower Erection:** A massive, rigid block located at the amidships position is erected first. This block is the structural backbone of the build.
2. **Lateral Adjacent Blocks:** Blocks adjacent to the tower are erected on both port and starboard sides, forming the "base" of the pyramid.
3. **Upper Blocks Above Tower:** Deck blocks and superstructure modules are erected on top of the central tower.
4. **End Blocks:** Bow block and stern block are erected last at the apex regions.
5. **Final Closure:** Remaining gaps are welded, and the hull is completed.

**Diagram Description (text-based):**

A pyramid-like cross-section is shown with the central tower at the top. Blocks are arranged radiating outward and downward in a stepped pyramid shape, resembling an inverted Christmas tree.

**Advantages:**

- **Maximum stability early** — the central tower provides rigid backbone support.
- **Parallel work possible** — multiple blocks can be erected simultaneously on different sides.
- **Distributes crane load** — symmetric loading reduces swing.
- **Suitable for large vessels** — VLCCs, capesize bulk carriers.

**Disadvantages:**

- **Very heavy central tower block** — requires the largest crane in the yard.
- **Welding access difficult** in the central region due to crowding.
- **Distortion control is challenging** because of the asymmetric thermal history.
- **High initial capital** for the heavy-lift crane.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Given Data:**
- Block weight, $W = 350$ tonnes $= 350 \times 9.81 = 3433.5$ kN
- Number of slings, $n = 4$
- Sling angle, $\theta = 45^{\circ}$
- Dynamic amplification factor, $\alpha = 1.20$
- Safety factor, $f_s = 5$

**(i) Tension in each sling:**

$$
T = \frac{W \cdot \alpha}{n \cdot \cos\theta}
$$

$$
\cos 45^{\circ} = 0.7071
$$

$$
T = \frac{3433.5 \times 1.20}{4 \times 0.7071}
$$

$$
T = \frac{4120.2}{2.8284}
$$

$$
T \approx 1456.7 \text{ kN per sling}
$$

**(ii) Design tension per sling (with safety factor):**

$$
T_{\text{design}} = T \times f_s = 1456.7 \times 5 = 7283.5 \text{ kN}
$$

**(iii) Total hook load on the crane:**

$$
\text{Hook Load} = n \cdot T = 4 \times 1456.7 = 5826.8 \text{ kN}
$$

In tonnes:

$$
\text{Hook Load} = \frac{5826.8}{9.81} \approx 594 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Final crane SWL recommendation:** The crane must be rated for at least **600 tonnes** at the required radius, including its own safety factor.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams (Module 3 — Hull Erection):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "erection area" with "block fabrication area."** The erection area is where *blocks are joined*, not where they are *made*. Many students lose 1–2 marks by mixing these up.
