> 2. **Omitting the dynamic amplification factor.** A 350-tonne block cannot be lifted by a 350-tonne crane. Always multiply by $\alpha = 1.10 \text{ to } 1.25$.
> 3. **Not stating the block weight unit.** Use **tonnes or kiloNewtons consistently**. Mixing units mid-calculation is a frequent error.
> 4. **Describing only one erection method.** In 14-mark answers, *comparing* two methods (e.g., floor vs. ring) is essential. A single-method answer gets maximum 8–9 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention welding sequence for distortion control.** Examiners specifically test this because it links Module 3 to Module 4 (welding technology).
> 6. **Treating single-point and multi-point lifting as identical.** Always discuss CG offset, sling angles, and swing dynamics.
> 7. **Writing formulae without substituting values.** KTU valuation rewards *fully worked numerical solutions*. An unsubstituted formula scores zero marks for the calculation step.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Hull erection** is the final assembly of pre-fabricated blocks into the complete ship hull.
- The **erection area workflow** is: *Pre-assembly → Block assembly → Transport → Lift → Align → Weld*.
- The **four methods of hull assembly** are: piece-small, section, block (modular), and grand block.
- The **three block erection methods** are:
  - **Floor (bottom-up)** — best for stability and inclined berths.
  - **Ring (transverse)** — best for submarines and parallel work.
  - **Pyramid (central tower)** — best for large vessels and parallel work.
- **Single-point lifting** uses one crane and one sling assembly; **multi-point lifting** uses four or more sling points or tandem cranes.
- **Sling tension formula:** $T = \dfrac{W \cdot \alpha}{n \cdot \cos\theta}$.
- **Dynamic amplification factor:** $\alpha = 1.10 \text{ to } 1.25$.
- **Wire-rope safety factor:** $f_s = 5 \text{ to } 6$.
- **Block weight range:** **50 t to 1500 t** typical; grand blocks can exceed 10,000 t.
- **Erection cycle time:** **2–8 hours** per block.
- **Welding shrinkage allowance:** **1–2 mm per metre** of weld.
- **Welding sequence is symmetric** to control distortion.
- **Crane types:** gantry, tower, portal jib, crawler, and floating cranes.
- **SPMTs (Self-Propelled Modular Transporters)** are used for heavy block (>200 t) movement.
- **Always state selection criteria** when recommending an erection method (vessel type, berth type, crane availability, distortion control, build duration).
- **Always apply both dynamic factor AND safety factor** when computing crane SWL.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Technological processes in the installation of main machinery, shafting and propeller
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST631 - SHIP PRODUCTION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Technological processes in the installation of main machinery, shafting and propeller

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Main Machinery Installation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** *Main machinery installation* is the technological process of accurately positioning, aligning, and securing the prime mover (main engine, reduction gear, thrust bearing, and auxiliary units) on the prepared foundation within the ship's engine room, ensuring concentric alignment, controlled clearance, and structural load transfer to the hull.

In simpler terms, it is the **"surgical operation" of placing the heart of the ship into its body**. The main engine is the heart that pumps life (rotational power) into the ship, and installing it correctly is what makes the difference between a vessel that runs smoothly for 30 years and one that breaks down at sea.

### Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Spinal Column:** Imagine fitting the spine into a human body. The main engine is the brain, the shaft is the spinal cord, and the propeller is the foot. If the spine is even slightly bent (misalignment), the foot will limp (vibration), the nerves will hurt (bearing wear), and the body will tire prematurely. Ship installation is exactly this — we are inserting a "spinal column" of rotating machinery into a steel hull.

## 1.2 Shafting Installation

**Shafting** is the system of shafts, bearings, and couplings that transmits rotational power from the main engine output flange to the propeller hub. The *technological process* involves:

- **Intermediate shaft** segments
- **Line shaft bearings** (white-metal or rubber)
- **Thrust bearing** (absorbs propeller thrust)
- **Plummer blocks** and **bearing seats**
- **Stern tube** and **A-bracket** (P-bracket)
- **Tailshaft** (propeller shaft) with **cone** and **keyway**

## 1.3 Propeller Installation

**Propeller installation** is the final technological process in the power transmission chain, wherein the propeller is mounted on the tapered cone of the tailshaft and secured using a key and nut (or hydraulically pushed on), followed by **dynamic balancing** and **underwater fitting checks**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Module 3 expects students to understand the **sequential technological workflow** (not just individual components). Marks are awarded for describing *process steps*, *alignment methods*, *tolerances*, and *quality checks*.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standards

> [!NOTE]
> - **Lloyd's Register / IACS alignment tolerance:** Shaft centerline deviation ≤ **0.10 mm/m** (typically 0.05 mm/m for naval vessels).
> - **Standard deflection of intermediate shaft (sag):** δ = **0.10 to 0.15 mm** for merchant vessels.
> - **Propeller pull-up hydraulic pressure:** **80 to 150 MPa** depending on taper ratio.
> - **Taper ratio (standard):** **1:12, 1:15, or 1:16** on tailshaft cone.
> - **Key clearance (top):** **0.0005 to 0.0010 × shaft diameter (d)**.
> - **Class rule: Dmin of shaft** = k × √(HP/N), where k is a constant per classification society.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Logical Pillars of Installation

The entire Module-3 topic rests on **three sequential pillars**:

### Pillar A — Main Machinery Installation
1. **Foundation inspection** (flatness, hardness, holding-down bolt integrity)
2. **Engine arrival, skidding, and lifting** (using overhead cranes, gantries, or chain blocks)
3. **Preliminary positioning** (rough centering using wire/plumb-line method)
4. **Final alignment** (laser, optical, or dial gauge method)
5. **Holding-down bolt tightening** (controlled sequence: snug → full → final torque)
6. **Pipework, cabling, and commissioning**

### Pillar B — Shafting Installation
1. **Stern tube boring** (in-situ machining on vessel)
2. **Stern tube and A-bracket fitting** (with chock adjustment)
3. **Tailshaft, intermediate shaft erection** (shore-to-vessel transfer)
4. **Shaft alignment (sag and offset)**
5. **Bearing white-metal lining and scraping**
6. **Coupling fit and centering**

### Pillar C — Propeller Installation
1. **Tailshaft cone preparation** (polishing, taper verification)
2. **Key fitting and propeller slinging**
3. **Hydraulic nut pulling** (or heating method)
4. **Locking nut torque and locking pin**
5. **Propeller nut cover fitting and sealing**
6. **Dynamic balancing and sea trial verification**

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

> [!NOTE]
> **Why foundation inspection first?** Because any cast-iron or steel foundation that is not flat to **0.05 mm/m** will distort the engine bedplate, causing cyclic stress and eventual cracking at holding-down bolt feet. Foundations are checked with a **precision straightedge** and **feeler gauges**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why sequential bolt tightening?** If the bolts are tightened in a random order, the bedplate will warp. KTU's standard answer uses the **"star pattern from center outward"** method — this is the same logic used in cylinder head tightening of internal combustion engines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why shaft sag (deflection) is allowed?** Because a perfectly straight shaft line is impossible to achieve. By deliberately allowing the shaft to **sag downward by 0.10 to 0.15 mm** at the coupling, we compensate for the **upward thrust load** during operation. When the propeller pushes forward, the shaft line rises and becomes straight — this is **pre-loading the shaft line**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why hydraulic nut for propeller?** A propeller of mass **20 tonnes** cannot be driven onto a **1:15 taper** by mechanical force alone. Hydraulic oil at **120 MPa** in a built-in puller nut generates **gigapascal-level contact pressure**, creating an interference fit that grips by friction — this is the **Wedge Principle** of the cone.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for solving KTU 2024 numerical problems on this topic.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Standard |
|---|---------|-------------|------------------|
| 1 | $\delta = \dfrac{5\,w\,L^{4}}{384\,E\,I}$ | Deflection (sag) of intermediate shaft under own weight | mm; $w$ in N/mm |
| 2 | $I = \dfrac{\pi\,d^{4}}{64}$ | Second moment of area of solid circular shaft | $\text{mm}^{4}$ |
| 3 | $D_{\min} = k \cdot \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{\text{HP}}{N}}$ | Minimum shaft diameter (BS/Lloyd's rule) | mm; k ≈ 64–72 for marine |
| 4 | $T = 9550 \cdot \dfrac{\text{HP}}{N}$ | Torque transmitted by shaft | N·m (metric) |
| 5 | $P_{b} = \dfrac{W}{L \cdot D}$ | Bearing pressure | MPa or N/mm² |
| 6 | $P_{h} = \dfrac{2 \cdot F}{\pi \cdot d \cdot l \cdot \tan\alpha}$ | Hydraulic pressure for propeller pull-up (Wedge fit) | MPa |
| 7 | $\theta = \dfrac{180 \cdot \delta}{\pi \cdot R}$ | Slope at shaft end due to sag | degrees |
| 8 | $\sigma_{b} = \dfrac{32 \cdot M}{\pi \cdot d^{3}}$ | Bending stress in shaft | N/mm² |
| 9 | $\tau = \dfrac{16 \cdot T}{\pi \cdot d^{3}}$ | Shear stress in shaft | N/mm² |
| 10 | $\Delta = \delta_{T} - \delta_{C}$ | Net vertical offset (thrust load deflection − cold alignment deflection) | mm |
| 11 | $h = \dfrac{P \cdot L}{2 \cdot A \cdot E}$ | Bolt elongation under preload | mm |
| 12 | $F_{f} = \mu \cdot F_{n}$ | Friction grip force on propeller cone | N; $\mu$ ≈ 0.12–0.18 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used daily by ship superintendents and shipyard planning engineers. The shaft-diameter rule (Formula 3) is a **classification society** mandate. The hydraulic pressure formula (Formula 6) governs **propeller pull-up safety** — incorrect pressure can either slip the propeller at sea (catastrophic) or damage the tailshaft.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Working:** The following derivations are written out completely. No step is skipped — this is the KTU examiner's expected answer pattern.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sag (Deflection) of an Intermediate Shaft

**Problem Statement:** An intermediate shaft of length **L = 6.0 m**, diameter **d = 300 mm**, made of steel ($E = 2.1 \times 10^{5} \text{ N/mm}^{2}$), is supported at its ends. Find the deflection (sag) at the centre due to its own weight.

**Step 1 — Calculate weight per unit length, $w$.**

Density of steel $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Volume per metre} &= \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^{2} \cdot 1 \\
&= \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.300)^{2} \cdot 1 \\
&= 0.07069 \; \text{m}^{3}/\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass per metre} &= \rho \cdot V = 7850 \times 0.07069 \\
&= 554.9 \; \text{kg/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
w &= m \cdot g = 554.9 \times 9.81 \\
&= 5443 \; \text{N/m} \\
&= 5.443 \; \text{N/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the second moment of area, $I$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^{4}}{64} \\
&= \dfrac{\pi \cdot (300)^{4}}{64} \\
&= \dfrac{\pi \cdot 8.1 \times 10^{9}}{64} \\
&= 3.976 \times 10^{8} \; \text{mm}^{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the deflection formula for a simply-supported beam with UDL.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_{\text{sag}} &= \dfrac{5\,w\,L^{4}}{384\,E\,I} \\
&= \dfrac{5 \times 5.443 \times (6000)^{4}}{384 \times (2.1 \times 10^{5}) \times (3.976 \times 10^{8})} \\
&= \dfrac{5 \times 5.443 \times 1.296 \times 10^{15}}{384 \times 2.1 \times 10^{5} \times 3.976 \times 10^{8}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 5 \times 5.443 \times 1.296 \times 10^{15} \\
&= 3.527 \times 10^{16}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Denominator} &= 384 \times 2.1 \times 10^{5} \times 3.976 \times 10^{8} \\
&= 3.207 \times 10^{16}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_{\text{sag}} &= \dfrac{3.527 \times 10^{16}}{3.207 \times 10^{16}} \\
&= 1.100 \; \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The shaft sags by **1.10 mm** at midspan. For a merchant ship, the KTU/classification standard requires the **sag to be compensated upward by this exact value** during cold alignment. This ensures that under hot running and propeller thrust, the line becomes straight.

**Step 4 — Convert this to an alignment offset for the surveyor.**

The shaft is supported at two ends and the slope at the support (used to compute the offset) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \dfrac{w \cdot L^{3}}{24 \cdot E \cdot I} \\
&= \dfrac{5.443 \times (6000)^{3}}{24 \times 2.1 \times 10^{5} \times 3.976 \times 10^{8}} \\
&= \dfrac{5.443 \times 2.16 \times 10^{11}}{2.004 \times 10^{15}} \\
&= 5.87 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Note that the slope $\theta$ at the support is the **angular deviation** that the surveyor must "look through" when sighting with an optical scope. In practice, a forward alignment telescope is set to this angle before the final bearing readjustment.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bearing Pressure Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A plummer block bearing carries a shaft of diameter **D = 250 mm**, length **L = 400 mm**, under a steady radial load of **W = 50 kN**. Find the bearing pressure.

**Step 1 — Apply the bearing pressure formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{b} &= \dfrac{W}{L \cdot D} \\
&= \dfrac{50 \times 10^{3}}{400 \times 250} \\
&= \dfrac{50000}{100000} \\
&= 0.50 \; \text{N/mm}^{2} \; (\text{MPa})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Acceptable Limit:** For **white-metal (Babbitt) bearings** under marine service, the design bearing pressure is typically **0.5 to 1.5 MPa**. Our value of **0.5 MPa** is at the lower end — a safe, conservative design.

**Step 2 — Comment on projected area design.**

Sometimes, the projected area is taken as **L × D** (cylindrical projection) or **L × (D − clearance)**. For KTU 2024 board purposes, the simpler **W / (L × D)** form is sufficient and preferred.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Propeller Pull-Up Hydraulic Pressure

**Problem Statement:** A tailshaft cone has diameter **d = 500 mm**, axial length **l = 600 mm**, taper **1:15**. Find the hydraulic pressure required to push the propeller on, if the axial force available from the hydraulic nut is **F = 8 MN**, and the static friction coefficient is $\mu = 0.14$.

**Step 1 — Compute the taper half-angle $\alpha$.**

Taper 1:15 means:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan\alpha &= \dfrac{1}{15} \\
\alpha &= 3.814^{\circ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the wedge-fit pressure equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{h} &= \dfrac{2 \cdot F}{\pi \cdot d \cdot l \cdot \tan\alpha} \\
&= \dfrac{2 \times 8 \times 10^{6}}{\pi \times 500 \times 600 \times \frac{1}{15}} \\
&= \dfrac{1.6 \times 10^{7}}{\pi \times 500 \times 40} \\
&= \dfrac{1.6 \times 10^{7}}{62831.85} \\
&= 254.6 \; \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> This pressure seems high but is correct for hydraulic pull-up. Note that the actual **mean contact pressure** (across the cone surface) is $P_{m} = P_{h}/\tan\alpha \approx$ **3.82 GPa** — yielding an interface contact stress well above the yield of the steel cone. This is what produces the **cold weld / friction grip** that holds the propeller.

**Step 3 — Verify the axial holding force after pull-up.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{f} &= \mu \cdot F_{n} \\
&= \mu \cdot P_{h} \cdot \pi \cdot d \cdot l \cdot \tan\alpha \cdot \cos\alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

For a thin taper, $\cos\alpha \approx 1$ and substituting back:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{f} &\approx \mu \cdot 2F \\
&= 0.14 \times 2 \times 8 \times 10^{6} \\
&= 2.24 \; \text{MN}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **axial holding force** that prevents the propeller from slipping under full-ahead thrust.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Step | Quantity Calculated | Symbol | Typical Range (Marine) |
|------|---------------------|--------|------------------------|
| 1 | Sag deflection | $\delta$ | 0.10 – 1.50 mm |
| 2 | Slope at support | $\theta$ | 0.0001 – 0.001 rad |
| 3 | Bearing pressure | $P_{b}$ | 0.5 – 1.5 MPa |
| 4 | Propeller pull-up force | $F$ | 4 – 12 MN |
| 5 | Holding friction force | $F_{f}$ | 1 – 4 MN |
| 6 | Alignment tolerance | $\Delta$ | $\leq 0.10$ mm/m |

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Sequential Workflow of Installation

> [!NOTE]
> The following diagram shows the **complete technological process** as a workflow, mapping the dependencies between main machinery, shafting, and propeller installation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Ship on Building Berth] --> A2[Foundation Scrutiny]
    A2 --> A3[Engine Lifting via Crane]
    A3 --> A4[Rough Centering Wire Method]
    A4 --> A5[Final Alignment Laser Optical]
    A5 --> A6[Chock Grouting]
    A6 --> A7[Holding Down Bolt Torquing]
    A7 --> A8[Pipework and Cabling]
    A8 --> A9[Engine Commissioning]

    B1[Stern Tube Boring] --> B2[A Bracket Fitting]
    B2 --> B3[Thrust Block Alignment]
    B3 --> B4[Intermediate Shaft Erection]
    B4 --> B5[Shaft Cold Alignment Sag]
    B5 --> B6[Bearing White Metal Scraping]
    B6 --> B7[Coupling Bolt Up]

    C1[Tailshaft Cone Polishing] --> C2[Key and Keyway Check]
    C2 --> C3[Propeller Slinging]
    C3 --> C4[Hydraulic Pull Up]
    C4 --> C5[Locking Nut Torque]
    C5 --> C6[Nut Cover Sealing]
    C6 --> C7[Dynamic Balance]
    C7 --> C8[Sea Trial]

    A9 --> B1
    B7 --> C1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Notice the **dependency arrows** at the bottom — Engine commissioning **must precede** Stern tube boring (because the engine fixes the forward shaft center), and shaft alignment **must precede** propeller fitting. This sequencing is the most commonly tested concept in KTU 2024.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Block Architecture of Shafting System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ME[Main Engine] --> C1[Flexible Coupling]
    C1 --> G[Reduction Gear]
    G --> C2[High Speed Coupling]
    C2 --> TB[Thrust Bearing Block]
    TB --> IS1[Intermediate Shaft 1]
    IS1 --> IB1[Intermediate Bearing]
    IB1 --> IS2[Intermediate Shaft 2]
    IS2 --> IB2[Intermediate Bearing]
    IB2 --> TS[Tail Shaft]
    TS --> ST[Stern Tube Bush]
    ST --> AB[A Bracket Bearing]
    AB --> PROP[Propeller]
    TS --> CONE[Cone and Key]

    ME -. thrust load .-> TB
    TB -. absorbed to hull .-> HULL[Hull Foundation]
    PROP -. hydrodynamic thrust .-> HULL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Block Diagram:** Solid arrows show **power flow direction** (rotational torque). Dotted arrows show **force transfer to hull** (thrust absorption path). The reduction gear is optional — for direct-drive ships, it is omitted, and the main engine couples directly to the thrust bearing.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Propeller Mounting Detail (Block Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Hub Bore Cleaning] --> D2[Key Insertion into Keyway]
    D2 --> D3[Propeller Slinged to Shaft]
    D3 --> D4[Initial Push Hand Operated]
    D4 --> D5[Hydraulic Nut Engagement]
    D5 --> D6[Pressure Ramp Up to 100 MPa]
    D6 --> D7[Hold for 5 Minutes]
    D7 --> D8[Pressure Release and Check Movement]
    D8 --> D9{Recheck with Feeler Gauge}
    D9 -->|Not Seated| D5
    D9 -->|Fully Seated| D10[Locking Nut Spinning]
    D10 --> D11[Locking Pin Insertion]
    D11 --> D12[Nut Cover with Gasket]
    D12 --> D13[Grease Filling and Sealing]
    D13 --> D14[Final Mark and Documentation]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block-Level Adaptation:** A physical drawing of a propeller cone requires shaded cross-sections and tolerance callouts. As per KTU-PREMIER ENGINE rules, this **block-level functional architecture** is the safe, KTU-compliant alternative and is **fully acceptable** in board examinations.

---

## 4.4 Schematic Explanation: Why Sag and Offset are Compensated

> [!NOTE]
> **Cold Condition (Ship at berth, no thrust):** The shaft sags **downward** due to gravity. Bearings sit on the lower half of the shaft.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hot Running Condition (Ship at sea, full ahead):** The propeller thrust pushes the shaft **forward**, the shaft line **lifts upward** by the same magnitude as the cold sag. The shaft becomes **straight and concentric** with the engine centerline.

> [!NOTE]
> **Vertical Offset Diagram (textual representation):**

```
Engine Centerline (Reference)
   |
   |  ▲ 0.15 mm (sag)
   |  │
   |  ▼─── Intermediate shaft cold position
   |  │
   |  ▼─── Intermediate shaft hot position (straight)
   |
   ▼ Stern Tube
```
