# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "shaft sag" and explain its importance in shaft alignment. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Shaft sag is the **downward deflection of an intermediate shaft at its midspan** due to its own weight when supported at the two end bearings. Mathematically, for a simply-supported uniform shaft:

$$
\delta = \dfrac{5\,w\,L^{4}}{384\,E\,I}
$$

The typical allowable sag for a marine intermediate shaft is **0.10 to 0.15 mm**. Its importance lies in **cold alignment compensation**: during the alignment process at the shipyard, the shaft line is deliberately set to be **higher at the coupling** by an amount equal to the expected operational deflection. This way, when the propeller applies thrust at sea, the shaft line straightens out and runs concentrically with the engine, ensuring smooth, vibration-free operation.

---

### Question 2: List any six sequential steps in the installation of a marine propeller on the tailshaft. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Tailshaft cone preparation** — polishing the cone surface and verifying taper with a sine bar or taper gauge.
2. **Key fitting** — insertion and check-fit of the key in the keyway of both cone and propeller hub bore.
3. **Propeller slinging** — rigging the propeller with chain blocks and lifting to the shaft flange height.
4. **Initial manual push** — sliding the propeller onto the cone by hand until resistance is felt.
5. **Hydraulic nut pull-up** — applying controlled pressure (80 to 150 MPa) via a built-in hydraulic nut to force the propeller fully onto the taper.
6. **Locking nut and pin** — fitting the locking nut, applying the specified torque, and inserting the locking pin to prevent loosening.
7. *(Optional for full marks)* Nut cover, grease filling, dynamic balancing, sea trial.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Full Question on Main Machinery & Shafting*

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q A (a)** Explain in detail the **technological process of installing the main engine** in a ship's engine room. Discuss foundation preparation, alignment methods, and holding-down bolt tightening sequence. **(7 marks)**

**Q A (b)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **shafting system** of a merchant ship. Describe how **cold alignment** is carried out using a telescope and dial gauge, including the role of **sag and offset**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q A (a)

**Foundation Preparation (2 marks):**
- The engine foundation is a **cast steel or fabricated structure** welded to the ship's double bottom.
- Foundation flatness is checked using a precision straightedge of length 1.5 m.
- Maximum allowable deviation: **0.05 mm/m** (Lloyd's Register).
- Chock surfaces are roughened by chipping to ensure grout adhesion.

**Engine Lifting & Positioning (2 marks):**
- The main engine is lifted by an **overhead crane** of capacity 2× the engine mass.
- It is lowered onto **chocks** (thin steel shims) placed on the foundation.
- A safety margin of 50–100 mm is maintained above the final position.

**Alignment Methods (2 marks):**
- **Wire alignment method (initial)**: A piano wire is stretched between engine and gearbox centerline, and the engine position is adjusted using feeler gauges.
- **Laser/optical alignment (final)**: A laser beam is projected through a target on the gearbox coupling face, achieving **±0.02 mm accuracy**.

**Bolt Tightening Sequence (1 mark):**
- The **star pattern from the center outward** is used.
- Three stages: **snug-tight (50%) → full torque (75%) → final torque (100%)**.
- Hydraulic torque wrenches are used to ensure uniform preload.

---

#### Model Solution — Q A (b)

**Shafting System Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to **Section 4.2** Mermaid block diagram. The system consists of: Main engine → Coupling → Reduction gear → High-speed coupling → **Thrust bearing** → Intermediate shaft 1 → Intermediate bearing → Intermediate shaft 2 → Intermediate bearing → **Tailshaft** → Stern tube → A-bracket → Propeller.

**Cold Alignment Procedure (3 marks):**

1. The engine and reduction gear are already grouted and bolted.
2. A **forward alignment telescope** is set up at the reduction gear coupling face, sighted along the shaft centerline.
3. A **dial gauge** is mounted on each intermediate shaft coupling, reading the vertical (and horizontal) gap to the telescope cross-hair.
4. Bearings are shimmed up or down using **chocks** to bring the dial gauge readings to the **predicted cold offset** values (typically 0.10–0.15 mm upward).
5. After alignment, the **white-metal bearings** are scraped to final fit.

**Role of Sag and Offset (1 mark):**
- The shaft line is set **high** at the coupling by the calculated sag value.
- At full thrust, the shaft deflects down and the line becomes **straight**.
- This pre-loading avoids **edge-loading** of bearings and minimizes vibration.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice on Propeller & Numerical*

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q B (a)** Describe in detail the **technological process of fitting a marine propeller** on the tailshaft cone. Include methods of securing, locking, and post-installation checks. **(7 marks)**

**Q B (b)** An intermediate shaft of diameter **320 mm** and length **7.0 m** is supported at its ends. The shaft is made of mild steel ($E = 2.0 \times 10^{5} \text{ N/mm}^{2}$, $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$). Calculate: (i) the **sag (deflection)** at the centre, and (ii) the **slope at the support**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q B (a)

**Stage 1 — Cone Preparation (1.5 marks):**
- The cone is cleaned with solvent, polished with fine emery cloth (grit 600+).
- Taper is verified using a **sine bar** or **taper gauge**; allowable deviation is **±0.01 mm/m**.

**Stage 2 — Key Fitting (1 mark):**
- The key is inserted into the shaft keyway first, then the propeller hub is slid over.
- **Top clearance** in the keyway should be **0.0005 to 0.0010 × d**.
- Key is made of **forged steel** (typically 40–50 HRC).

**Stage 3 — Propeller Slinging and Initial Push (1 mark):**
- Propeller (mass 15–25 tonnes typical) is rigged with chain blocks and lifted to the shaft height.
- It is hand-pushed onto the cone until it contacts the keyway shoulder.

**Stage 4 — Hydraulic Pull-up (2 marks):**
- A **hydraulic nut** is screwed onto the rear threads of the shaft.
- Pressure is ramped in stages: 30 → 60 → 100 → 120 MPa, held for **5 minutes** at each stage.
- The **wedge effect** of the cone (taper 1:12 to 1:16) generates **gigapascal contact pressure** at the interface, creating a **friction grip**.

**Stage 5 — Locking and Post-checks (1.5 marks):**
- Locking nut is torqued (per maker's specification, e.g., 15,000 N·m for 500 mm shaft).
- **Locking pin** is inserted and welded to prevent rotation.
- Nut cover with **O-ring gasket** is fitted and grease-packed for corrosion protection.
- **Dynamic balancing** is done (residual imbalance ≤ 50 g·mm/kg per ISO 1940).
- **Sea trial** confirms no abnormal vibration at full RPM.

---

#### Model Solution — Q B (b)

**Given Data:**
- Diameter: $d = 320 \text{ mm}$
- Length: $L = 7.0 \text{ m} = 7000 \text{ mm}$
- $E = 2.0 \times 10^{5} \text{ N/mm}^{2}$
- $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^{2}$

**Step 1 — Calculate weight per unit length $w$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Area} &= \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^{2} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (320)^{2} = 80424.77 \; \text{mm}^{2} \\
\text{Mass per mm} &= 80424.77 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 7850 \times 10^{3} \cdot 10^{-3} \\
&= 0.6313 \; \text{kg/mm} \\
\text{Weight per mm} &= 0.6313 \times 9.81 = 6.193 \; \text{N/mm} \\
\therefore w &= 6.193 \; \text{N/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate second moment of area $I$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^{4}}{64} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (320)^{4}}{64} \\
&= \dfrac{\pi \cdot 1.0486 \times 10^{10}}{64} \\
&= 5.147 \times 10^{8} \; \text{mm}^{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (i) — Sag deflection at midspan.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta &= \dfrac{5 \cdot w \cdot L^{4}}{384 \cdot E \cdot I} \\
&= \dfrac{5 \times 6.193 \times (7000)^{4}}{384 \times 2.0 \times 10^{5} \times 5.147 \times 10^{8}} \\
&= \dfrac{5 \times 6.193 \times 2.401 \times 10^{15}}{3.953 \times 10^{16}} \\
&= \dfrac{7.435 \times 10^{16}}{3.953 \times 10^{16}} \\
&= 1.881 \; \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result: $\delta \approx 1.88$ mm**

**Step 4 (ii) — Slope at the support.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \dfrac{w \cdot L^{3}}{24 \cdot E \cdot I} \\
&= \dfrac{6.193 \times (7000)^{3}}{24 \times 2.0 \times 10^{5} \times 5.147 \times 10^{8}} \\
&= \dfrac{6.193 \times 3.43 \times 10^{11}}{2.471 \times 10^{15}} \\
&= \dfrac{2.124 \times 10^{12}}{2.471 \times 10^{15}} \\
&= 8.595 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to arcseconds for surveyor convenience:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= 8.595 \times 10^{-4} \times \dfrac{180}{\pi} \times 3600 \\
&\approx 177 \; \text{arcseconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result: $\theta \approx 8.60 \times 10^{-4}$ rad $\approx$ 177″**

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU Board:** The slope value of **177 arcseconds** corresponds to an angular deviation that the alignment telescope is pre-set to. In practice, this is added to the cold alignment offset to compensate for the shaft's natural tendency to sag.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Confusing the "sag" direction with the "thrust" direction:** Students often claim the shaft rises during operation. The correct answer is: **the shaft is set UPWARD by the sag value at the coupling** so that during forward thrust, the shaft straightens.
> 2. **Forgetting the star-pattern bolt tightening:** Marks are deducted if the bolt tightening is described in random or sequential order. **Always mention "star-pattern from the center outward"** to get full credit.
> 3. **Missing the role of grout:** Chocks alone do not transmit load — **grout (cementitious or epoxy resin)** fills the void and transmits the engine weight to the foundation. Many students skip this.
> 4. **Propeller pull-up pressure errors:** Using the formula $P = F/A$ (flat area) instead of the **wedge formula** $P_h = 2F/(\pi d l \tan\alpha)$ is a frequent mistake. The wedge geometry must be reflected.
> 5. **Skipping the "Why":** KTU 2024 follows OBE pattern. Always state **why** a step is performed (e.g., *why* hydraulic pressure is held for 5 minutes — to allow **stress equalization** and **material creep** to settle the propeller into the cone).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 3**

- **Three Pillars:** Main Machinery → Shafting → Propeller — *in strict sequence.*
- **Foundation flatness tolerance:** ≤ **0.05 mm/m**.
- **Bolt tightening pattern:** **Star from center outward**, in three stages.
- **Alignment methods:** Wire (rough) → Laser/Optical (final).
- **Sag formula:** $\delta = 5wL^{4}/(384EI)$ — applied during **cold alignment**.
- **Cold alignment offset:** **+0.10 to +0.15 mm upward** at the coupling to compensate for thrust deflection.
- **Bearing pressure:** $P_b = W/(L \cdot D)$, acceptable range **0.5 to 1.5 MPa** for white metal.
- **Propeller cone taper:** Typically **1:12, 1:15, or 1:16**; verified by **sine bar** or **taper gauge**.
- **Hydraulic pull-up pressure:** **80 to 150 MPa**; held for **5 minutes** to allow stress equalization.
- **Wedge-fit equation:** $P_h = 2F/(\pi d l \tan\alpha)$ — remember the **taper angle term**.
- **Friction grip:** $F_f = \mu \cdot F_n$ with $\mu \approx 0.12 - 0.18$ at the cone interface.
- **Locking mechanism:** **Locking nut + locking pin** welded in place; never rely on nut torque alone.
- **Dynamic balance grade:** **G 6.3** or better per ISO 1940 for marine propellers.
- **Post-installation checks:** Sea trial at **100% MCR**, vibration monitoring at **all bearings**, stern tube oil sample.
- **Class rules:** Shaft diameter by **Lloyd's / DNV** formula $D = k \cdot \sqrt[3]{HP/N}$; **never** deviate from class-approved values.
- **Key clearance:** Top: **0.0005–0.0010 d**; sides: **snug fit** (interference).
- **Chock material:** Steel or epoxy grout; thickness **25–50 mm**; never use wood or rubber in main machinery.
- **Stern tube boring:** Done **in-situ on the vessel**, not at the shop, to ensure alignment with ship's centerline.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weld defects, distortions and their preventive measures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST631 - SHIP PRODUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Weld defects, distortions and their preventive measures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Weld Defect:** A weld defect is any irregularity or imperfection in a weld that deviates from the specified design intent, fails to meet the requirements of applicable codes (e.g., **AWS D1.1**, **ISO 5817**, **ASME Sec. IX**), or compromises the mechanical/metallurgical integrity of the welded joint.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Weld Distortion:** Weld distortion is the undesirable change in shape, size, or geometric configuration of a welded structure caused by the non-uniform expansion and contraction of the base metal and weld metal during the localized thermal cycle of the welding process.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Cooking Roti on a Tawa" Analogy:** Imagine placing a small, cold lump of dough on a hot tawa (griddle) — only the underside heats, the top stays cool, water evaporates unevenly, and the roti warps, cracks, and shrinks asymmetrically. Welding is the metal-equivalent: a tiny, intensely heated weld pool (the "tawa spot") rapidly cools and tries to shrink, but is held back by the surrounding cool, rigid base metal. The tug-of-war between hot and cold zones **causes** both **defects** (cracks, pores — like the cracks on a dry roti) and **distortion** (warping, bending — like the curled edges of the roti). Preventive measures in welding are like pre-conditioning the dough (preheating), rotating the tawa (proper sequence), or using a frame (jigs/fixtures) to keep things flat.

### 1.3 Key Industrial Standards & Reference Metrics

> **[!WARNING] KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently award 1–2 marks for mentioning the correct **acceptance standard** linked to each defect. Memorize the standard codes in **bold** below.

| Standard | Governing Body | Acceptance Level |
|---|---|---|
| **AWS D1.1** | American Welding Society | Structural Welding Code – Steel |
| **ISO 5817** | International Org. for Standardization | Quality levels B, C, D for fusion welds |
| **ASME Sec. IX** | ASME | Boiler & Pressure Vessel welding qualification |
| **EN 1090-2** | CEN | Execution of steel structures – EXC1 to EXC4 |
| **ABS Rules** | American Bureau of Shipping | Shipbuilding-specific weld acceptance |

### 1.4 Primary Causes of Weld Imperfections (Root-Cause Family Tree)

Weld defects and distortions are governed by **four** root causes:
1. **Thermal** – uneven heating/cooling rates
2. **Metallurgical** – phase changes, hydrogen, carbon equivalent
3. **Mechanical** – restraint, residual stress
4. **Procedural** – poor parameter selection, improper technique

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Classification of Weld Defects (Hierarchical Breakdown)

```
Weld Defects
   ├── Surface Defects (visible without NDT)
   │     ├── Undercut
   │     ├── Underfill / Concavity
   │     ├── Overlap
   │     ├── Surface Cracks
   │     ├── Spatter
   │     └── Porosity (open)
   ├── Internal / Sub-surface Defects
   │     ├── Porosity (closed)
   │     ├── Slag Inclusion
   │     ├── Tungsten Inclusion
   │     ├── Lack of Fusion (LOF)
   │     ├── Lack of Penetration (LOP)
   │     ├── Internal Cracks
   │     └── Wormholes
   └── Dimensional / Geometric Defects
         ├── Excessive Reinforcement
         ├── Misalignment / Hi-lo
         ├── Root Concavity / Suck-back
         └── Incomplete root
```

### 2.2 Detailed Defect Analysis (Why & How)

#### 2.2.1 Cracks (Most Critical Defect)
- **Why they form:** Tensile stress exceeds local material strength during cooling.
- **Hot cracks (solidification cracks):** Form at high temperature; caused by low-melting eutectics (S, P) and high restraint.
- **Cold cracks (hydrogen-induced):** Form below 200 °C; caused by H₂, martensite, and restraint.
- **Reheat cracks:** Form in PWHT of Cr-Mo / Cr-V steels.
- **Lamellar tearing:** Stepwise cracks in through-thickness direction of rolled plates with high S content.

> [!NOTE] **Prevention:** Reduce restraint → use buttering, controlled H₂ electrodes (low-hydrogen: AWS E7018), preheat to **≥ 150 °C**, control carbon equivalent **CE ≤ 0.45 %**, post-weld heat treatment (PWHT) at **595–620 °C**.

#### 2.2.2 Porosity
- **Why:** Gas entrapment (H₂, N₂, CO) due to contaminated base metal, wet electrodes, insufficient shielding gas, or rapid solidification.
- **How:** Molten metal solidifies before gases can escape.
- **Prevention:** Clean base metal, dry electrodes (**350 °C for 1 hr** for low-H types), adequate shielding gas flow (**15–20 L/min** for GMAW), correct travel speed.

#### 2.2.3 Lack of Fusion (LOF) & Lack of Penetration (LOP)
- **LOF:** Failure of weld metal to fuse with parent metal or preceding bead.
- **LOP:** Weld root fails to reach the bottom of the joint preparation.
- **Why:** Low heat input, dirty surfaces, wrong torch angle, excessive travel speed.
- **Prevention:** Adequate **heat input (1.0–2.0 kJ/mm for mild steel)**, proper joint preparation (root face 1.5–2 mm, gap 2–3 mm), correct root pass technique.

#### 2.2.4 Slag Inclusion
- **Non-metallic solid** entrapped between weld beads or at the fusion line.
- **Why:** Insufficient cleaning between passes, high viscosity weld pool, incorrect electrode manipulation.
- **Prevention:** Stringer beads, thorough inter-pass cleaning, weave width ≤ **3× electrode diameter**.

#### 2.2.5 Undercut
- **Groove melted into the base metal at weld toe** and not filled by weld metal.
- **Why:** Excessive welding current, wrong electrode angle, fast travel speed.
- **Acceptance (ISO 5817 Level B):** Undercut depth ≤ **0.05t** (max **0.5 mm**).

#### 2.2.6 Overlap & Excessive Reinforcement
- **Overlap:** Weld metal flows over the surface without fusion.
- **Excess reinforcement:** Weld cap too convex, causing stress concentration at the toe.
- **Prevention:** Reduce current, use stringer beads, grind flush where required (e.g., butt welds in fatigue service).

### 2.3 Classification of Weld Distortions

| Type | Schematic Description | Direction of Shrinkage |
|---|---|---|
| **Transverse Shrinkage** | Plate edges move towards weld | Perpendicular to weld axis |
| **Longitudinal Shrinkage** | Plates shorten along weld axis | Parallel to weld axis |
| **Angular Distortion** | Plates rotate about weld axis (bevel distortion) | Tilting |
| **Longitudinal Bowing** | Long welded plates curve along weld length | Out-of-plane bending |
| **Transverse Bowing / Buckling** | Thin plates buckle in a wave pattern | Out-of-plane (wave) |
| **Rotational Distortion** | T-joints rotate out of plane | Twist |
| **Distortion in Closed Structures** | Overall shortening/dimensional loss | 3D contraction |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Examination Tip:** Use `\vert` instead of `|` to avoid table syntax breakage (per engine protocol).

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta L \;=\; \dfrac{\alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot L}{1}$ | Free linear expansion/contraction (α = thermal expansion coeff.) | mm |
| 2 | $Q \;=\; \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000}$ | Heat input per unit length (Q) | kJ/mm |
| 3 | $\sigma_{res} \;=\; E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Residual stress estimate (if fully restrained) | MPa |
| 4 | $\Delta_{trans} \;\approx\; 0.2 \cdot \dfrac{A_w}{t}$ | Transverse shrinkage (Voldrich formula, single-V butt) | mm |
| 5 | $\Delta_{long} \;\approx\; \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T_{eff}$ | Longitudinal shrinkage approximation | mm |
| 6 | $\alpha \cdot \Delta T_{eff} \;\approx\; 0.001 \;(0.1\%)$ | Effective shrinkage strain (mild steel, multi-pass) | – |
| 7 | $\beta \;\approx\; \dfrac{0.2 \cdot A_w}{t \cdot b}$ | Angular distortion (deg.) | degrees |
| 8 | $CE \;=\; C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$ | Carbon Equivalent (IIW formula) | % |
| 9 | $T_{preheat} \;\geq\; 150\;^{\circ}C$ | Minimum preheat for CE > 0.45 | °C |
| 10 | $P_{cr} \;=\; \dfrac{k \cdot \pi^2 \cdot E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{L}\right)^2$ | Critical buckling load of plate (for buckling distortion) | N |

Where:
- $\alpha$ = thermal expansion coefficient (**≈ 12 × 10⁻⁶ /°C for steel**)
- $E$ = Young's modulus (**≈ 210 GPa**)
- $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio (**≈ 0.30**)
- $A_w$ = cross-sectional area of weld
- $t$ = plate thickness
- $b$ = plate width
- $k$ = buckling coefficient (≈ **4.0** for simply supported long plate)
- $\eta$ = arc efficiency (GMAW 0.85, SMAW 0.80, GTAW 0.60, SAW 1.0)
- $V$ = arc voltage, $I$ = current, $v$ = travel speed

### 2.5 Engineering Utility in Shipbuilding

In ship production, distortion control is critical for:
- **Hull panel accuracy** (±2 mm tolerance per ISO 9001 shipyard standard)
- **Block alignment** (dihedral and joggle mismatch causes rework costs)
- **Propulsion shaft alignment** (cumulative distortion causes bearing wear)
- **Propeller clearance** (skew distortion affects clearance)
- **Class society approval** (ABS, DNV, LR — all require dimensional surveys)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Heat Input (Q)

Heat input governs both defect formation and distortion magnitude. KTU 2024 scheme expects students to derive and use it.

**Starting Energy Definition:**

$$Q_{arc} \;=\; \eta \cdot V \cdot I \quad \text{(Watts, J/s)}$$

**Divide by travel speed v (mm/s) to get energy per unit length:**

$$Q \;=\; \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000} \quad \text{(kJ/mm)}$$

*(The 1000 converts W·s/mm to kJ/mm.)*

**Example:** SMAW, η = 0.80, V = 25 V, I = 200 A, v = 5 mm/s

$$Q \;=\; \dfrac{0.80 \times 25 \times 200}{5 \times 1000} \;=\; 0.80 \;\text{kJ/mm}$$

> **Interpretation:** Heat input of **0.80 kJ/mm** is within the standard SMAW range (0.5–1.5 kJ/mm for mild steel ≤ 25 mm).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Transverse Shrinkage (Voldrich's Formula)

For a single-V butt weld joining two plates of thickness $t$ with weld cross-section $A_w$:

**Step 1 — Identify the contracting volume:**
The molten weld pool solidifies and contracts. The transverse contraction per unit length is proportional to the weld area $A_w$ and inversely proportional to the plate thickness $t$ (thicker plates resist less shrinkage per mm width).

$$\Delta_{trans} \;\approx\; k \cdot \dfrac{A_w}{t}$$

**Step 2 — Empirical constant** (from Voldrich's experiments on mild steel):

$$k \;\approx\; 0.2$$

**Step 3 — Final form:**

$$\Delta_{trans} \;\approx\; 0.2 \cdot \dfrac{A_w}{t} \quad \text{(mm, per unit length)}$$

**Step 4 — Worked example:** 
Two 10 mm plates, single-V butt, root gap 2 mm, included angle 60°.

$$A_w \;\approx\; \dfrac{(t + g)^2}{4 \tan(\theta/2)} \;=\; \dfrac{(10+2)^2}{4 \tan 30°} \;=\; \dfrac{144}{4 \times 0.5774} \;\approx\; 62.35\;\text{mm}^2$$

$$\Delta_{trans} \;\approx\; 0.2 \times \dfrac{62.35}{10} \;\approx\; 1.25\;\text{mm}$$

> **Note:** This matches observed shipyard data of ~1.2–1.5 mm for 10 mm butt welds.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Angular (Bevel) Distortion

For a single-V butt weld in plates of width $b$ and thickness $t$:

**Step 1 — Non-uniform shrinkage:** The weld metal on the bevel face shrinks more than the base metal at the root side because the weld metal is closer to the top surface and the thermal gradient is steeper there.

**Step 2 — Geometric model (rotation about weld root):**
The differential shrinkage $\delta$ across the thickness creates an angular tilt $\beta$:

$$\beta \;\approx\; \dfrac{\delta}{t}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute empirical shrinkage:**

$$\beta \;\approx\; \dfrac{0.2 \cdot A_w}{t \cdot b} \quad \text{(radians)}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to degrees:**

$$\beta^{\circ} \;\approx\; \dfrac{0.2 \cdot A_w}{t \cdot b} \times \dfrac{180}{\pi}$$

**Step 5 — Example:** $A_w = 62.35$ mm², $t = 10$ mm, $b = 1000$ mm

$$\beta \;\approx\; \dfrac{0.2 \times 62.35}{10 \times 1000} \;\approx\; 0.001247\;\text{rad} \;\approx\; 0.0714^{\circ}$$

> **Practical significance:** A 0.07° tilt across a 1 m panel is small — but **in a 200 m hull** with many welds, cumulative angular distortion can exceed 50 mm displacement.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Critical Buckling Condition (for Thin-Plate Distortion)

For thin panels where compressive residual stress exceeds plate buckling load, **buckling distortion** (wave) occurs.

**Step 1 — Residual compressive stress from restrained cooling:**

$$\sigma_{res} \;=\; E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T_{eff}$$

For mild steel: $\alpha \Delta T_{eff} \approx 0.001$ (0.1 % effective strain)

$$\sigma_{res} \;\approx\; 210{,}000 \times 0.001 \;=\; 210\;\text{MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Critical buckling stress (Euler plate buckling):**

$$\sigma_{cr} \;=\; \dfrac{k \cdot \pi^2 \cdot E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{L}\right)^2$$

**Step 3 — Buckling occurs when:**

$$\sigma_{res} \;\geq\; \sigma_{cr}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for critical plate slenderness:**

$$\left(\dfrac{L}{t}\right)_{cr} \;=\; \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{k \cdot E}{12(1-\nu^2)\sigma_{res}}}$$

**Step 5 — Plug in** (k = 4, ν = 0.30, σ_res = 210 MPa):

$$\left(\dfrac{L}{t}\right)_{cr} \;=\; \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 210{,}000}{12 \times 0.91 \times 210}} \;\approx\; \pi \sqrt{366.3} \;\approx\; 60.1$$

> **Conclusion:** When **L/t > 60**, thin panels will **buckle** under welding residual stress. Shipyard rule: panels thinner than **6 mm** with unsupported length > **360 mm** require **temporary stiffeners** to prevent buckling distortion.

---

### 3.5 Carbon Equivalent & Preheat Determination

**Step 1 — Compute CE (IIW):**

$$CE \;=\; C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$$

**Step 2 — For a typical ship steel (EH36):** C = 0.16 %, Mn = 1.5 %, Cr = 0.05 %, Ni = 0.30 %

$$CE \;=\; 0.16 + \dfrac{1.5}{6} + \dfrac{0.05}{5} + \dfrac{0.30}{15} \;=\; 0.16 + 0.25 + 0.01 + 0.02 \;=\; 0.44$$

**Step 3 — Decision (per AWS D1.1 Table 3.2):**

$$CE \leq 0.45 \Rightarrow \text{No mandatory preheat, but good practice } \geq 50^{\circ}\text{C}$$

$$CE > 0.45 \Rightarrow \text{Mandatory preheat } \geq 150^{\circ}\text{C}$$

$$CE > 0.60 \Rightarrow \text{PWHT mandatory at } 595\pm15^{\circ}\text{C}$$

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Defect → Cause → Prevention

| Defect | Primary Cause | Prevention Strategy | Acceptance (ISO 5817 B) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Solidification crack | High restraint + S, P | Lower heat input, buttering, lower S content | None permitted |
| HAZ hydrogen crack | H₂ + martensite + restraint | Preheat ≥ 150 °C, low-H electrode, PWHT | None permitted |
| Porosity | Gas entrapment | Clean metal, dry electrodes, correct gas flow | Max 2.5 % of bead area |
| LOF / LOP | Insufficient heat | Increase Q to 1.0–2.0 kJ/mm, clean joint | None permitted |
| Slag inclusion | Poor inter-pass cleaning | Grind & wire-brush between passes | Max 2 mm or 10 % t |
| Undercut | High current, wrong angle | Reduce I, correct travel angle | Max 0.5 mm deep |
| Overlap | Low travel speed | Increase speed, reduce current | None permitted |
| Lamellar tearing | Through-thickness strain | Butter layer, Z-grade plate | Disallowed in critical joints |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS (MERMAID BLOCKS)

### 4.1 Master Flowchart: Defect Origin → Detection → Prevention

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Welding Process Initiated] --> B{Four Root Causes Active?}
    B -- Thermal --> C[Uneven Heating/Cooling]
    B -- Metallurgical --> D[Phase Change, H2, CE]
    B -- Mechanical --> E[Restraint, Residual Stress]
    B -- Procedural --> F[Wrong Parameters, Poor Technique]
    C --> G[Distortion Group]
    D --> H[Cracks, Porosity]
    E --> G
    E --> H
    F --> G
    F --> H
    G --> I[Transverse Shrinkage]
    G --> J[Angular Distortion]
    G --> K[Buckling in Thin Plates]
    H --> L[Solidification Crack]
    H --> M[Hydrogen Crack]
    H --> N[Porosity]
    H --> O[Slag Inclusion]
    L --> P{NDT Method}
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P -- Surface --> Q[Visual + MPI]
    P -- Subsurface --> R[UT or RT]
    P -- Volumetric --> S[Radiographic Test]
    Q --> T{Acceptance}
    R --> T
    S --> T
    T -- Pass --> U[Approved per ISO 5817]
    T -- Fail --> V[Repair: Grind, Re-weld, PWHT]
    V --> A
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Distortion Mitigation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PREDICT[Predictive Stage]
    P1[Heat Input Calculation]
    P2[Shrinkage Estimation]
    P3[CE Evaluation]
    end
    subgraph PREVENT[Prevention Stage]
    Q1[Preheat Application]
    Q2[Fixtures and Jigs]
    Q3[Welding Sequence Plan]
    Q4[Backing Gas and Clamping]
    end
    subgraph EXECUTE[Execution Stage]
    R1[Stringer Beads]
    R2[Balanced Heat Input]
    R3[Symmetric Welding]
    end
    subgraph CORRECT[Correction Stage]
    S1[Flame Heating]
    S2[Mechanical Straightening]
    S3[PWHT Stress Relief]
    S4[Peening]
    end
    PREDICT --> PREVENT --> EXECUTE --> CORRECT
    CORRECT --> PREDICT
```

### 4.3 Weld Defect Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Weld Defects] --> B[Surface]
    A --> C[Internal]
    A --> D[Dimensional]
    B --> B1[Undercut]
    B --> B2[Overlap]
    B --> B3[Surface Crack]
    B --> B4[Spatter]
    C --> C1[Porosity]
    C --> C2[Slag Inclusion]
    C --> C3[LOF]
    C --> C4[LOP]
    C --> C5[Internal Crack]
    C --> C6[Tungsten Inclusion]
    D --> D1[Excess Reinforcement]
    D --> D2[Hi-lo Misalignment]
    D --> D3[Root Concavity]
    D --> D4[Incomplete Root]
```

### 4.4 Welding Sequence Strategy (Block-Level Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SEQ[Block Welding Sequence]
    direction TB
    T1[Step 1: Tack Weld Corners]
    T2[Step 2: Weld Center Then Outward]
    T3[Step 3: Skip Welding Alternately]
    T4[Step 4: Backstep Sequence]
    T5[Step 5: Final Cap with Low Heat Input]
    end
    SEQ --> OUT[Reduced Angular and Transverse Distortion]
```

### 4.5 Distortion Type vs Plate Geometry Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START{Plate Geometry} --> T1[Thick Plate, L/t less than 30]
    START --> T2[Thin Plate, L/t greater than 60]
    START --> T3[Moderate, 30 to 60]
    T1 --> D1[Angular Distortion Dominant]
    T2 --> D2[Buckling Wave Distortion]
    T3 --> D3[Transverse plus Longitudinal Shrinkage]
    D1 --> FIX1[Use Strong Backing, Symmetric Joint]
    D2 --> FIX2[Add Temporary Stiffeners, Reduce Heat Input]
    D3 --> FIX3[Use Proper Sequence, Clamp Edges]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** *List any **six** common weld defects and state the root cause for each.*
*(Module 4, KTU 2024 Scheme, Part A – 3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| # | Defect | Root Cause |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Porosity | Entrapment of gases (H₂, N₂, CO) due to contaminated base metal, wet electrodes, or insufficient shielding gas. |
| 2 | Solidification crack | Low-melting eutectics (S, P) combined with high restraint during solidification. |
| 3 | Hydrogen-induced (cold) crack | Diffusible H₂ + martensitic microstructure + tensile residual stress. |
| 4 | Lack of fusion (LOF) | Insufficient heat input or improper torch manipulation. |
| 5 | Lack of penetration (LOP) | Root face too thick, root gap too narrow, or low current. |
| 6 | Slag inclusion | Failure to clean slag between passes; high viscosity weld pool. |

*(Any 6 correct defects with causes = 3 marks; 0.5 per defect)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2.** *Define **weld distortion**. Name the **five** main types with one-line descriptions.*
*(Module 4, KTU 2024 Scheme, Part A – 3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> **Weld Distortion:** The unwanted change in shape or dimensions of a welded component caused by non-uniform thermal expansion and contraction during the welding cycle.

| # | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transverse shrinkage | Plate edges move toward the weld axis. |
| 2 | Longitudinal shrinkage | Plate shortens along the weld length. |
| 3 | Angular (bevel) distortion | Plate rotates about the weld axis due to uneven shrinkage through thickness. |
| 4 | Buckling distortion | Thin plates ripple out-of-plane in a wave pattern. |
| 5 | Rotational distortion | T-joints or lap joints twist out of plane. |

*(Definition 1 m, 5 types × 0.4 m = 2 m; total 3 m)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** *Explain the **classification of weld defects** with neat sketches of (i) undercut, (ii) lack of fusion, and (iii) porosity. For each, state the **cause** and the **prevention method**. **[7 marks]***

**(b)** *A **single-V butt weld** is made between two **12 mm thick mild steel plates** with root gap 2 mm and included angle 60°. Calculate the **(i) weld cross-sectional area**, **(ii) transverse shrinkage** using Voldrich's formula, and **(iii) angular distortion** in degrees for a 1 m long weld. Given: α = 12 × 10⁻⁶ /°C, η = 0.80, V = 26 V, I = 220 A, v = 5 mm/s. **[7 marks]***

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

> **Weld Defect Classification:**
>
> **1. Undercut** — a groove melted into the base metal at the weld toe, not refilled.
> - *Cause:* Excessive welding current, incorrect electrode/torch angle, fast travel speed.
> - *Prevention:* Reduce current by 10–15 %, correct torch angle to 15–20° drag, reduce travel speed, use weave technique.
>
> **2. Lack of Fusion (LOF)** — failure of weld metal to coalesce with parent metal or previous bead.
> - *Cause:* Low heat input, dirty/oxidized surface, wrong torch angle, large root face.
> - *Prevention:* Increase heat input to 1.0–2.0 kJ/mm, grind and clean joint faces, use proper 70–80° drag angle.
>
> **3. Porosity** — cavities formed by gas entrapment during solidification.
> - *Cause:* Wet electrodes, contaminated base metal, insufficient shielding gas, high solidification rate.
> - *Prevention:* Dry electrodes at 350 °C for 1 hr, clean with acetone, maintain shielding gas flow 15–20 L/min, use stringer beads.

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**(i) Weld cross-sectional area (single-V):**

$$A_w \;=\; \dfrac{(t + g)^2}{4 \tan(\theta/2)} \;=\; \dfrac{(12 + 2)^2}{4 \tan 30°}$$

$$A_w \;=\; \dfrac{196}{4 \times 0.5774} \;=\; \dfrac{196}{2.309} \;\approx\; 84.9\;\text{mm}^2$$

**(ii) Transverse shrinkage (Voldrich):**

$$\Delta_{trans} \;=\; 0.2 \cdot \dfrac{A_w}{t} \;=\; 0.2 \times \dfrac{84.9}{12} \;\approx\; 1.42\;\text{mm}$$

**(iii) Angular distortion:**

$$\beta \;=\; \dfrac{0.2 \cdot A_w}{t \cdot b} \;\times\; \dfrac{180}{\pi} \;=\; \dfrac{0.2 \times 84.9}{12 \times 1000} \times 57.296$$

$$\beta \;\approx\; 0.0810^{\circ} \;\;\text{(per meter, small but cumulative)}$$

**Verification — Heat input:**

$$Q \;=\; \dfrac{0.80 \times 26 \times 220}{5 \times 1000} \;=\; 0.915\;\text{kJ/mm} \quad \text{(Acceptable range)}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** *Discuss the **mechanisms of weld distortion**. With the aid of a sketch, explain **(i) transverse shrinkage, (ii) longitudinal shrinkage, (iii) angular distortion, and (iv) buckling distortion**. State the **governing formula** for each. **[7 marks]***

**(b)** *A shipyard welds a **6 mm thick, 1.5 m long panel** of mild steel using SAW with heat input 2.0 kJ/mm. Determine **(i) the effective residual stress** and **(ii) whether buckling distortion is likely** (use k = 4, ν = 0.30, α·ΔT_eff = 0.001). **[7 marks]***

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

> **Mechanism:** Welding creates a localized heat source that rapidly melts the metal. On cooling, the weld metal attempts to contract, but the surrounding cool, rigid base metal resists. This generates **inherent shrinkage strains** in the weld zone and **residual stresses** in the surrounding plate, leading to distortion.

> **(i) Transverse shrinkage** — perpendicular to weld axis. Plates move toward each other.
>
> $$\Delta_{trans} \;\approx\; 0.2 \cdot \dfrac{A_w}{t}$$

> **(ii) Longitudinal shrinkage** — parallel to weld axis. Plate shortens along the weld line.
>
> $$\Delta_{long} \;\approx\; \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T_{eff} \;\approx\; 0.001 \cdot L$$

> **(iii) Angular distortion** — non-uniform transverse shrinkage through thickness causes rotation about the weld line.
>
> $$\beta \;\approx\; \dfrac{0.2 \cdot A_w}{t \cdot b} \quad \text{(radians)}$$

> **(iv) Buckling distortion** — in thin panels, longitudinal compressive residual stress exceeds Euler buckling load, producing out-of-plane waves.
>
> $$\sigma_{cr} \;=\; \dfrac{k \cdot \pi^2 \cdot E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{L}\right)^2$$

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**(i) Effective residual stress:**

$$\sigma_{res} \;=\; E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T_{eff} \;=\; 210{,}000 \times 0.001 \;=\; 210\;\text{MPa}$$

**(ii) Check for buckling:**

$$L/t \;=\; 1500 / 6 \;=\; 250 \quad \gg \quad 60 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{HIGH buckling risk}$$

$$\sigma_{cr} \;=\; \dfrac{4 \times \pi^2 \times 210{,}000}{12 \times (1 - 0.30^2)} \times \left(\dfrac{6}{1500}\right)^2$$

$$\sigma_{cr} \;=\; \dfrac{4 \times 9.87 \times 210{,}000}{12 \times 0.91} \times (0.004)^2$$

$$\sigma_{cr} \;=\; \dfrac{8{,}290{,}800}{10.92} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-5} \;\approx\; 759{,}400 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-5} \;\approx\; 12.15\;\text{MPa}$$

Since $\sigma_{res} = 210$ MPa $\gg \sigma_{cr} = 12.15$ MPa,

> **Conclusion:** **Buckling distortion is virtually certain.** Mitigation: install **temporary stiffeners** at 200 mm spacing, or use **stitch welding** sequence, or reduce heat input to ~1.0 kJ/mm.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting units** on shrinkage values (must be mm). [-0.5]
> 2. **Mixing up** Voldrich's constant (0.2) with empirical factors for dissimilar joints. [-1]
> 3. **Not converting** radians to degrees in angular distortion. [-0.5]
> 4. **Omitting acceptance limits** (e.g., ISO 5817 Level B undercut ≤ 0.5 mm). [-1]
> 5. **Failing to name standards** when describing prevention — examiners expect AWS/ISO/ASME tags. [-0.5]
> 6. **Skipping the carbon equivalent calculation** when justifying preheat. [-1]
> 7. **Ignoring the L/t buckling check** for thin panels — this is a high-yield 2-mark sub-question. [-2]

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> **High-Density Summary — Memorize Before Exam**

- [ ] **Weld defects = internal + surface + dimensional** (Porosity, LOF, LOP, slag, cracks, undercut, overlap, lamellar tearing).
- [ ] **Hot cracks** form during solidification → control S, P, restraint.
- [ ] **Cold (hydrogen) cracks** form below 200 °C → control H₂, preheat ≥ 150 °C, use low-H electrodes (E7018).
- [ ] **Porosity** → dry electrodes, clean metal, 15–20 L/min shielding gas.
- [ ] **LOF/LOP** → heat input 1.0–2.0 kJ/mm, clean joint, correct root gap (2–3 mm).
- [ ] **Undercut** → reduce current, correct angle, slow travel; ISO 5817 B: ≤ 0.5 mm.
- [ ] **Slag inclusion** → inter-pass cleaning, stringer beads, weave ≤ 3× electrode Ø.
- [ ] **Distortions = transverse, longitudinal, angular, buckling, rotational**.
- [ ] **Voldrich formula**: $\Delta_{trans} = 0.2 \cdot A_w / t$.
- [ ] **Angular distortion**: $\beta = 0.2 \cdot A_w / (t \cdot b)$ rad.
- [ ] **Buckling limit**: $L/t \leq 60$ to avoid wave distortion in steel panels.
- [ ] **Heat input formula**: $Q = \eta \cdot V \cdot I / (v \cdot 1000)$ kJ/mm.
- [ ] **Carbon Equivalent (IIW)**: $CE = C + Mn/6 + (Cr+Mo+V)/5 + (Ni+Cu)/15$.
- [ ] **CE > 0.45** → preheat; **CE > 0.60** → PWHT at 595 °C.
- [ ] **Prevention methods**: preheat, low-H process, proper sequence (backstep/skip), jigs, stiffeners, balanced heat input, post-weld straightening (flame, mechanical, peening).
- [ ] **Standards to cite**: AWS D1.1, ISO 5817, ASME Sec. IX, EN 1090-2, ABS Rules.
- [ ] **Standard constants**: α = 12 × 10⁻⁶ /°C, E = 210 GPa, ν = 0.30, η_SMAW = 0.80.
- [ ] **Shipbuilding applications**: hull panels (≤ 2 mm tolerance), block alignment, shaft alignment, propeller clearance, class survey approval.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing: Destructive/non-destructive
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST631 - SHIP PRODUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Testing: Destructive/non-destructive

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Testing in Ship Production

Testing is a **mandatory quality assurance activity** in shipbuilding, ensuring that the steel, welds, castings, and fabricated assemblies meet the design specifications of classification societies such as **IRS (Indian Register of Shipping)**, **Lloyd's Register**, **ABS**, **DNV**, and **Bureau Veritas**. Testing in ship production is broadly bifurcated into two fundamental categories:

- **Destructive Testing (DT)** — Tests that permanently damage or consume the test specimen.
- **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)** — Tests that evaluate integrity **without impairing** the future usefulness of the part.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive (Module 4):** Students must understand the *principle, procedure, application, advantages, and limitations* of each major DT and NDT method used in shipyards. Classification society acceptance criteria are implied learning outcomes.

---

## 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU Board-Standard Wording)

> [!NOTE]
> **Destructive Testing (DT):** A category of mechanical/metallurgical tests in which the test specimen is subjected to controlled loading or environmental conditions **until failure occurs**, in order to determine its mechanical, physical, or chemical properties.

> [!NOTE]
> **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)** — also called **Non-Destructive Evaluation (NDE)** or **Non-Destructive Inspection (NDI):** A group of inspection techniques used to evaluate the integrity, surface or sub-surface discontinuities, and mechanical properties of a material or component **without destroying its serviceability**.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

Imagine you bake a large cake (your ship hull) and you want to confirm the recipe is perfect.

- **Destructive Testing** is like slicing the cake, tasting a piece, dropping it from a height to see if it crumbles, or breaking it apart to check the inside. After this, the cake is **no longer whole** — but you have learned *exactly* how strong it is.
- **Non-Destructive Testing** is like tapping the cake gently, listening for hollow sounds, shining a torch through a thin edge, or holding it up to a magnifying glass. The cake remains **intact and edible**, but you still learn a lot about its quality.

In a shipyard, before launching a vessel, the classification surveyor demands **DT certificates** (from the steel mill) and **NDT records** (from in-house inspection) to ensure every joint will survive the rough ocean environment.

---

## 1.4 Primary Classification Overview

The two major families are sub-classified as follows (this will be expanded in Section 2):

### A. Destructive Testing Methods
| # | Method | Property Evaluated |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tensile Test | Ultimate Tensile Strength, Yield Strength, % Elongation |
| 2 | Impact Test (Charpy / Izod) | Toughness / Notch Sensitivity |
| 3 | Hardness Test (Brinell, Rockwell, Vickers) | Surface Hardness |
| 4 | Bend Test (Guided / Free) | Ductility & Weld Integrity |
| 5 | Fatigue Test | Endurance Limit |
| 6 | Creep Test | High-Temperature Performance |
| 7 | Macro / Micro Examination | Grain Structure, Weld Defects |

### B. Non-Destructive Testing Methods
| # | Method | Defect Type Detected |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Visual Testing (VT) | Surface defects visible to naked eye |
| 2 | Liquid Penetrant Testing (LPT / DPI) | Surface-breaking cracks, porosity |
| 3 | Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT) | Surface & near-surface defects in ferromagnetic steel |
| 4 | Eddy Current Testing (ECT) | Surface & sub-surface defects in conductive materials |
| 5 | Ultrasonic Testing (UT) | Sub-surface & volumetric defects, thickness measurement |
| 6 | Radiographic Testing (RT) | Internal volumetric defects (slag, porosity, lack of fusion) |
| 7 | Acoustic Emission Testing (AET) | Real-time crack growth monitoring |

---

## 1.5 Comparison Table — DT vs NDT (Board Favorite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This comparison appears almost every KTU exam. Memorize it verbatim.**

| Parameter | Destructive Testing | Non-Destructive Testing |
|---|---|---|
| Specimen | Sacrificial / disposable | Production component (in-service) |
| Damage | Yes, irreversible | No damage |
| Quantitative Result | Highly accurate (mechanical values) | Mostly qualitative (Yes/No type) |
| Cost per test | Moderate (but destroys a part) | Higher per test, but no material waste |
| Speed | Slower (machining + testing) | Faster, can be automated |
| Operator skill | Moderate | High (certified per ASNT / ISNT Level II) |
| Application stage | Material qualification / type approval | In-process & final inspection |
| Output | Mechanical properties (MPa, J, HRC) | Defect location, size, orientation |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Destructive Testing — Principle, Procedure & Engineering Utility

### 2.1.1 Tensile Test (Uniaxial Tension Test)

**Principle:** A standardized specimen is pulled axially at a slow, uniform rate (per **ASTM E8 / IS 1608**) until fracture. The load-elongation curve is recorded to derive strength and ductility parameters.

**Procedure (Step-wise Logic):**
1. Prepare a *standard round or rectangular specimen* with gauge length **L₀** and cross-sectional area **A₀**.
2. Mount in the **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)** — hydraulic or screw-driven.
3. Apply axial tensile load at constant crosshead speed (typically **1–10 mm/min** for mild steel).
4. Record continuous **Load (P)** vs **Elongation (ΔL)** data.
5. Compute engineering stress and strain from the raw data.
6. Identify **Yield Point**, **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS)**, and **% Elongation** from the curve.

**Key Engineering Output (Why shipbuilders care):**
- Yield strength (σ_y) determines the **allowable scantlings** of the hull girder.
- UTS sets the **ultimate load capacity** against rogue waves.
- % Elongation ensures the steel has **ductility** for plastic redistribution during collisions or grounding.

---

### 2.1.2 Impact Test (Charpy V-Notch)

**Principle:** A *notched* specimen is struck by a heavy pendulum hammer, and the energy absorbed in fracturing the specimen is measured. **Lower temperature ⇒ Lower absorbed energy ⇒ More brittle behavior.**

**Procedure:**
1. Cut **10 × 10 × 55 mm** specimen with **2 mm deep V-notch** (Charpy) or **5 mm deep U-notch** (Izod, less common in ship class rules).
2. Sub-cool (or heat) specimen to test temperature — common ship service temperatures are **0 °C**, **−20 °C**, and **−40 °C** for Arctic / Polar Class vessels.
3. Strike with pendulum and read absorbed energy (Joules).

**Engineering Utility:** Ships operating in cold seas (Baltic, North Atlantic, Polar routes per **IACS Polar Class PC 7**) demand **Charpy V-notch impact energy ≥ 27 J at −40 °C** for primary hull steel. This is a strict classification rule.

---

### 2.1.3 Hardness Tests

Three principal scales used in shipyards:

| Scale | Indenter | Load Range | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Brinell (HB)** | Tungsten carbide ball (10 mm) | 500–3000 kgf | Forged parts, rough castings |
| **Rockwell (HRC / HRB)** | Diamond cone (C) or ball (B) | 60–150 kgf | Heat-treated shafts, gears |
| **Vickers (HV)** | Square pyramid diamond | 1–100 kgf | Welds, thin plates, case-hardened |

**Why shipbuilders care:** Brinell hardness correlates directly to **ultimate tensile strength** through the empirical relationship (critical for quick checks in the steel stockyard):

> The classical empirical correlation between Brinell Hardness Number (HB) and Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS) for carbon and alloy steels, originally proposed by engineers and widely cited in shipbuilding metallurgy references, is approximately:

This relationship allows inspectors to estimate UTS using a portable Brinell test on a finished plate within seconds — vital for material identification during ship repair.

---

### 2.1.4 Bend Test (Guided Bend)

**Principle:** A weld joint specimen is bent around a mandrel until a specified angle (**180°** for most ship welds) is reached. The convex surface is examined for cracks.

**Procedure:** Specimen transverse to the weld → placed on a die → plunger presses until **180° bend** → check for cracks > 3 mm on the tension surface.

**Engineering Utility:** Confirms **weld metal ductility** and **fusion zone soundness** before a hull panel is welded into the vessel. Mandatory under **AWS D1.1 / IACS UR W11 / W14** for procedure qualification.

---

### 2.1.5 Fatigue Test

A specimen is subjected to cyclic loading at a specified **stress ratio (R = σ_min / σ_max)** until failure or 10⁷ cycles (endurance limit).

**Application in shipbuilding:** Shafts, propeller brackets, bilge keels, and rudder horns are fatigue-sensitive components. Ship classification societies demand S-N curves (Wöhler curves) for these parts.

---

### 2.1.6 Macro & Micro Examination

- **Macro examination:** Polished & etched cross-section of weld; viewed at 10× for fusion boundary, lack of penetration, slag entrapment.
- **Micro examination:** Polished, etched, and viewed at 50×–1000× under microscope for grain size, inclusions, HAZ structure.

---

## 2.2 Non-Destructive Testing — Principle, Procedure & Engineering Utility

### 2.2.1 Visual Testing (VT)

**Principle:** The oldest NDT — *direct human eye inspection*, often aided by **magnifying glasses (5×–10×)**, **borescopes**, and **dye-marking**.

**Standard:** **ASTM E165 / IS 11802**. It is the *first* inspection in any NDT sequence. Roughly **80% of all defects are first detected visually** before deeper NDT is engaged.

**KTU Angle:** Always mention that VT is the **baseline** before any other NDT method. Many students lose marks by skipping VT in the procedure.

---

### 2.2.2 Liquid Penetrant Testing (LPT / DPI)

**Principle:** A low-surface-tension penetrant liquid is drawn into surface-breaking discontinuities by **capillary action**. After cleaning the surface, a developer draws the trapped penetrant back out, producing a visible indication.

**Procedure Steps (Memorize!):**
1. **Pre-clean** — remove oil, paint, scale.
2. **Apply penetrant** — dwell time 10–30 minutes.
3. **Remove excess penetrant** — water-washable, solvent-removable, or post-emulsifiable.
4. **Apply developer** — white powder suspended in solvent.
5. **Inspect** — under UV-A light (for fluorescent) or white light (for visible dye).
6. **Post-clean & record.**

**Materials limitation:** Works **only on non-porous surfaces** (steel, aluminium, austenitic SS). Not for cast iron, weld root faces.

---

### 2.2.3 Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT)

**Principle:** When a ferromagnetic material is magnetized, **magnetic flux leakage** occurs at surface/near-surface discontinuities, attracting fine iron particles (dry powder or wet fluorescent suspension) to form a visible indication.

**Two magnetization techniques:**
- **Continuous method** — current flows while particles are applied (most sensitive).
- **Residual method** — particles applied *after* magnetization (suitable for parts with low retentivity).

**Limitations:**
- Only for **ferromagnetic** materials (carbon steel, low-alloy steel). Cannot inspect austenitic SS, aluminium, brass.

---

### 2.2.4 Ultrasonic Testing (UT)

**Principle:** High-frequency sound waves (**1–25 MHz**) are transmitted into the material via a piezoelectric transducer. **Echoes** returning from flaws or the back wall are analyzed for **time-of-flight** and **amplitude**.

**Two primary display modes:**
- **A-scan:** Time-base display of echo amplitude.
- **B-scan:** Cross-sectional image (depth vs probe position).
- **C-scan:** Plan-view map (X-Y position vs defect size).

**Engineering Utility in Shipbuilding:**
- **Lamination detection** in incoming steel plates.
- **Weld inspection** (in lieu of or alongside RT).
- **Thickness measurement** (corrosion survey in service ships) — critical for class renewal surveys.

---

### 2.2.5 Radiographic Testing (RT)

**Principle:** Penetrating **X-rays** or **Gamma rays** (Ir-192, Co-60) pass through the test object. Differential absorption by defects creates a latent image on a **film** (or digital detector array in modern DR systems).

**Key Image Quality Indicator:** The **Image Quality Indicator (IQI)** — also called **penetrameter** — is placed on the source side to verify sensitivity. Wire-type IQI (per **ASTM E747**) is most common in shipyards.

**Radiation Safety:** X-ray and gamma sources demand strict **ALARA** (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) protocols. Shipyard RT rooms are typically in isolated zones with 2 m thick concrete walls or equivalent shielding. Operators wear **TLD badges** for personal dosimetry.

---

### 2.2.6 Eddy Current Testing (ECT)

**Principle:** A high-frequency AC coil induces **eddy currents** in a conductive material. Flaws or material property changes disturb the eddy current field, detected as changes in coil impedance.

**Application:** Surface inspection of austenitic SS welds, propeller blade crack detection, conductivity sorting of non-ferrous alloys. Particularly useful for detecting cracks in **non-magnetic** materials where MPT is useless.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All numerical problems in KTU Module 4 are based on these formulas. Master every single one.**

| # | Test / Concept | Formula / Relationship | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tensile Stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A_0}$ | P = axial load (N), A₀ = original area (mm²) | Output in N/mm² = MPa |
| 2 | Tensile Strain | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | ΔL = elongation (mm), L₀ = gauge length (mm) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | % Elongation | $\%\text{El} = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | L_f = final gauge length (mm) | Key ductility indicator |
| 4 | % Reduction in Area | $\%\text{RA} = \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$ | A_f = fractured area (mm²) | Better ductility indicator than %El |
| 5 | UTS to HB Correlation | $\text{UTS (MPa)} \approx 3.45 \times \text{HB}$ | HB = Brinell Hardness Number | Empirical, valid for carbon steel |
| 6 | Impact Toughness | $\text{Energy absorbed} = E_{\text{initial}} - E_{\text{final}}$ | Joules (J) | Read directly from Charpy machine |
| 7 | Sound Velocity in Steel | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho}} \approx 5920 \text{ m/s}$ | E = Young's modulus, ρ = density | Used in UT time-of-flight |
| 8 | UT Flaw Depth | $d = \dfrac{v \times t}{2}$ | t = time between transmit & flaw echo (s) | Divide by 2 (round trip) |
| 9 | Plate Thickness (UT) | $T = \dfrac{v \times \Delta t}{2}$ | Δt = back-wall echo time | For corrosion survey |
| 10 | RT Exposure (Inverse Square Law) | $I \propto \dfrac{1}{D^2}$ | D = source-to-film distance | Doubling distance quarters intensity |
| 11 | Fatigue Endurance Limit (steel) | $\sigma_e \approx 0.5 \times \sigma_{\text{UTS}}$ | σ_UTS in MPa | Empirical (Marine steels) |
| 12 | Stress Intensity Factor (LEFM) | $K = \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | σ = applied stress, a = half-crack length (m) | Used in fracture mechanics & fatigue |
| 13 | Hardness (Brinell) | $\text{HB} = \dfrac{2P}{\pi D \left(D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}\right)}$ | P = load (kgf), D = ball dia (mm), d = indent dia (mm) | Standard test |
| 14 | Hardness (Vickers) | $\text{HV} = \dfrac{1.854 \times P}{d^2}$ | P = load (kgf), d = diagonal (mm) | Standard test |
| 15 | Hardness (Rockwell C) | $\text{HRC} = 100 - \dfrac{h}{0.002}$ | h = depth difference (mm) | Direct dial reading |

> **Critical instruction to self-check:** No `vert` or `mid` symbols needed since no absolute value bars appear inside the rows above — the table is safe.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility (Why Shipbuilders Need Both)

A modern shipyard does **not** choose between DT and NDT — it uses **both in a complementary manner**:

1. **Pre-construction (Steel mill / supplier stage):** Every plate batch is accompanied by **mill certificates** (3.1 / 3.2 per **EN 10204**) reporting tensile, impact, hardness, and chemical results — i.e., **DT data**.
2. **During construction (fabrication stage):** Every important weld (per class-approved WPS) is inspected by **NDT** — usually **UT 100% + RT spot check** for butt welds in strength deck, side shell, bottom.
3. **In-service (life of the ship):** Annual / Special / Continuous class surveys use **NDT** — primarily UT thickness gauging, MPI of crank webs, visual inspection of holds.

> **The phrase examiners love:** *"DT establishes material fitness for design, while NDT confirms component fitness for service."*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Numerical Solved Problems & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Numerical Problems in Module 4** typically test your ability to compute tensile properties, hardness conversions, UT thickness, and impact energy comparisons. Below are three fully solved problems with every algebraic step shown.

---

## 3.1 Solved Problem 1 — Tensile Test Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU University Exam – June 2022 style):**
> A mild steel specimen used for ship hull construction has an original gauge length of **50 mm** and an original diameter of **10 mm**. During a tensile test, the maximum load recorded is **30 kN**, and the specimen fractures at a load of **18 kN**. After fracture, the gauge length is **65 mm** and the fractured neck diameter is **7 mm**. Calculate:
> (i) Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS)
> (ii) % Elongation
> (iii) % Reduction in Area
> (iv) Whether the material passes the ship class requirement of %El ≥ 22% (IRS Rule for Grade A steel)

### Given Data
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_0 &= 50 \text{ mm} \\
d_0 &= 10 \text{ mm} \\
P_{\max} &= 30 \text{ kN} = 30{,}000 \text{ N} \\
P_{\text{fracture}} &= 18 \text{ kN} \text{ (not needed for UTS or \%El calculations)} \\
L_f &= 65 \text{ mm} \\
d_f &= 7 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1 — Compute Original Cross-Sectional Area (A₀)

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_0 &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times d_0^2 \\
A_0 &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times (10)^2 \\
A_0 &= \frac{3.1416}{4} \times 100 \\
A_0 &= 78.54 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS)

UTS is defined as the **maximum load** divided by the **original cross-sectional area**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{UTS} &= \frac{P_{\max}}{A_0} \\
\text{UTS} &= \frac{30{,}000 \text{ N}}{78.54 \text{ mm}^2} \\
\text{UTS} &= 381.97 \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
\text{UTS} &\approx 382 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** This UTS of ~382 MPa is consistent with **ordinary-strength mild steel** (Grade A per IRS / ASTM A36), which typically has UTS in the **400–490 MPa** range. The material passes.

### Step 3 — Compute % Elongation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{El} &= \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100 \\
\%\text{El} &= \frac{65 - 50}{50} \times 100 \\
\%\text{El} &= \frac{15}{50} \times 100 \\
\%\text{El} &= 30\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute % Reduction in Area

First, fractured area:
$$
\begin{aligned}
A_f &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times d_f^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (7)^2 = \frac{3.1416}{4} \times 49 \\
A_f &= 38.48 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Then, % reduction:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{RA} &= \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100 \\
\%\text{RA} &= \frac{78.54 - 38.48}{78.54} \times 100 \\
\%\text{RA} &= \frac{40.06}{78.54} \times 100 \\
\%\text{RA} &= 51.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Class Compliance Check

| Requirement | Observed | Pass / Fail |
|---|---|---|
| UTS ≥ 400 MPa (Grade A typical) | 382 MPa | ❌ Below (re-check spec — possibly a closer grade) |
| %Elongation ≥ 22% | 30% | ✅ Pass |

> **Conclusion:** The material **meets ductility requirements** but falls slightly short on strength; in KTU exams, the examiner expects you to flag both criteria separately, not just give a binary pass/fail.

---

## 3.2 Solved Problem 2 — Ultrasonic Thickness Measurement

**Problem Statement (KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 style):**
> During an in-service thickness survey of a bulk carrier's bottom plate, an ultrasonic probe detects a back-wall echo at **5.2 µs**. The probe uses a longitudinal wave with a nominal velocity of **5900 m/s** in carbon steel. Calculate:
> (i) The remaining plate thickness.
> (ii) If the original (as-built) thickness was **22 mm**, compute the corrosion allowance consumed.
> (iii) Will the plate fail the classification rule requiring minimum thickness of **18 mm**?

### Given Data
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t &= 5.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} \\
v &= 5900 \text{ m/s} = 5.9 \times 10^6 \text{ mm/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1 — Apply the UT Thickness Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{v \times \Delta t}{2} \\
T &= \frac{(5.9 \times 10^6 \text{ mm/s}) \times (5.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s})}{2} \\
T &= \frac{30.68}{2} \\
T &= 15.34 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Corrosion Consumed

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T &= T_{\text{original}} - T_{\text{measured}} \\
\Delta T &= 22 - 15.34 \\
\Delta T &= 6.66 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering note:** This is a serious finding — the plate has lost **~30%** of its original thickness. Surveyor will likely order **steel renewal** of this plate.

### Step 3 — Class Compliance

| Criterion | Required | Observed | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Minimum thickness | 18 mm | 15.34 mm | ❌ **FAIL** |

> **Conclusion:** The plate is below class minimum thickness. The surveyor must issue a **Condition of Class** requiring steel renewal before the next voyage.

---

## 3.3 Solved Problem 3 — Brinell Hardness to UTS Conversion

**Problem Statement:**
> A Brinell hardness test on a propeller shaft material gives an indent diameter of **3.2 mm** under a load of **3000 kgf** with a **10 mm tungsten carbide ball**. Compute:
> (i) Brinell Hardness Number (HB)
> (ii) Approximate Ultimate Tensile Strength using the empirical correlation.

### Given Data
$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 3000 \text{ kgf} \\
D &= 10 \text{ mm} \\
d &= 3.2 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1 — Apply the Brinell Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{HB} &= \frac{2P}{\pi D \left(D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}\right)} \\
\text{HB} &= \frac{2 \times 3000}{\pi \times 10 \times \left(10 - \sqrt{100 - 10.24}\right)} \\
\text{HB} &= \frac{6000}{31.416 \times \left(10 - \sqrt{89.76}\right)} \\
\text{HB} &= \frac{6000}{31.416 \times (10 - 9.4747)} \\
\text{HB} &= \frac{6000}{31.416 \times 0.5253} \\
\text{HB} &= \frac{6000}{16.500} \\
\text{HB} &\approx 363.6
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Approximate UTS

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{UTS (MPa)} &\approx 3.45 \times \text{HB} \\
\text{UTS} &\approx 3.45 \times 363.6 \\
\text{UTS} &\approx 1254 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** This is consistent with a **high-strength alloy steel** used for marine propeller shafts (e.g., **ASTM AISI 4340 quenched & tempered**, with UTS ~1250 MPa). The shaft material is correctly identified for marine duty.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Test

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is a high-yield KTU topic for 14-mark questions.**

| Question to Ask | Recommended Test | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Need absolute mechanical strength? | **Tensile Test** | Gives UTS, σ_y, %El — definitive |
| Will it face cold seas? | **Charpy Impact Test** | Measures low-temp toughness |
| Need quick material ID? | **Brinell Hardness** | Cheap, fast, on-site |
| Weld soundness — surface? | **LPT or MPT** | Reveals surface cracks |
| Weld soundness — sub-surface? | **UT or RT** | Reveals internal flaws |
| In-service thickness check? | **UT Thickness Gauge** | Non-intrusive, rapid |
| Austenitic SS weld inspection? | **LPT or UT** (NOT MPT) | MPT is useless on austenitic |
| Real-time crack monitoring? | **Acoustic Emission** | Detects active crack growth |
| Propeller blade subsurface cracks? | **Eddy Current** | Non-magnetic, fast scanning |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
