# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between Destructive Testing (DT) and Non-Destructive Testing (NDT).**

**Model Answer:**
Destructive Testing involves testing a specimen to failure to determine its mechanical properties, permanently damaging the sample. Examples include tensile test, impact test, and hardness test. NDT evaluates material integrity without impairing its future use, using methods like ultrasonic testing, radiographic testing, and magnetic particle testing. DT provides quantitative mechanical values (UTS, %El, impact energy), while NDT primarily detects the presence, location, and size of defects. DT is used during material qualification and type approval; NDT is used during in-process inspection, final acceptance, and in-service surveys. Both methods are complementary in shipbuilding quality assurance. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2023]
**Q: List any six Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) methods commonly used in shipyards.**

**Model Answer:**
The six NDT methods commonly used in shipyards are: **(1)** Visual Testing (VT), **(2)** Liquid Penetrant Testing (LPT / DPI), **(3)** Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT), **(4)** Ultrasonic Testing (UT), **(5)** Radiographic Testing (RT), and **(6)** Eddy Current Testing (ECT). Other emerging methods include Acoustic Emission Testing (AET) and Infrared Thermography. Among these, UT, RT, MPT, and LPT are most frequently mandated by classification societies for weld inspection. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Module-level Part B questions offer an internal choice (either Or). Solve *any one* of the two question choices.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q: (a) With a neat sketch, explain the procedure of the Tensile Test as conducted on a Universal Testing Machine (UTM). List the mechanical properties obtained from this test and explain their significance in ship hull steel selection. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The tensile test is conducted on a Universal Testing Machine (UTM) using a standardized cylindrical or flat specimen per **ASTM E8** or **IS 1608**.

**Procedure:**
1. A specimen of standard gauge length (L₀ = 5.65√A₀ for proportional specimens) and original cross-section A₀ is prepared.
2. The specimen is mounted between the fixed crosshead and the movable crosshead of the UTM.
3. An extensometer is attached to measure elongation precisely in the elastic region.
4. Axial tensile load is applied gradually at a constant rate (1–10 mm/min) until fracture.
5. Load (P) and elongation (ΔL) are recorded continuously, generating a stress-strain diagram.

**Mechanical Properties Obtained:**
- **Proportional Limit** — point up to which Hooke's Law holds.
- **Elastic Limit** — maximum stress for complete recovery.
- **Yield Strength (σ_y)** — stress at which plastic deformation begins (0.2% offset method used for mild steel since it has no sharp yield point).
- **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS)** — maximum stress the material can withstand.
- **% Elongation** — measure of ductility.
- **% Reduction in Area (%RA)** — better measure of ductility at fracture.
- **Modulus of Elasticity (E)** — stiffness.
- **Modulus of Resilience & Toughness** — energy absorption capacity.

**Significance in Ship Hull Steel Selection:**
- **σ_y** determines the **scantlings** (plate thicknesses) per classification rules (e.g., IACS CSR for Bulk Carriers and Tankers).
- **UTS** sets the **ultimate strength** margin against extreme loads (rogue waves, slamming).
- **% Elongation ≥ 22%** ensures the steel can undergo plastic redistribution without brittle fracture during collisions.
- **E** governs the hull girder stiffness and deflection.
- For **high-tensile steel (AH36, DH36, EH36)**, all of these properties are verified by **DNV / LR / IRS** at the mill before delivery. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Describe the Charpy V-notch Impact Test with a neat sketch. State the significance of test temperature and discuss the ductile-to-brittle transition behavior of mild steel. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Charpy V-notch Impact Test determines the **toughness** (energy absorption capacity) of a material under dynamic loading and at specific temperatures.

**Apparatus & Specimen:**
- A standard **Charpy specimen** of size **10 mm × 10 mm × 55 mm** with a **2 mm deep V-notch** of **45° angle** and **0.25 mm root radius** at the center.
- A **pendulum impact testing machine** with a calibrated hammer of known initial energy (typically 300 J).
- The specimen is supported as a **simply supported beam** against two anvils (span = 40 mm).

**Procedure:**
1. The specimen is placed in the machine with the notch facing away from the striking edge (or toward — depending on standard).
2. The pendulum is raised to a known height (initial potential energy).
3. The pendulum is released; it strikes and fractures the specimen in a single blow.
4. The pendulum continues to a reduced height (final potential energy).
5. The difference in height (× weight) gives the **absorbed energy in Joules**, read directly on the dial.

**Significance of Test Temperature:**
- Mild steel exhibits **ductile-to-brittle transition (DBTT)** — at low temperatures, the absorbed energy drops sharply and fracture mode changes from ductile (cup-and-cone) to brittle (cleavage).
- For **Arctic / Polar-class vessels**, classification rules require testing at sub-zero temperatures (e.g., **−40 °C for PC 7**).
- Specimens are immersed in a **cooling bath** (alcohol + liquid nitrogen or chilled brine) to reach the test temperature before striking.
- The **Charpy V-notch impact energy** at the design service temperature must satisfy class rules (typically **27 J longitudinal, 20 J transverse** at the lowest expected service temperature for Grade A steel; higher values for grades D, E, AH, DH, EH).

**Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Behavior:**
- The Charpy energy vs. temperature curve has three zones: **upper shelf** (fully ductile), **transition zone** (mixed), and **lower shelf** (fully brittle).
- The **DBTT** is defined as the average of the upper and lower shelf energies.
- For mild steel, the transition temperature is around **0 °C to −20 °C**, making **low-temperature Charpy testing critical for ships in cold waters**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: (a) Explain the principle, procedure, advantages, and limitations of Ultrasonic Testing (UT). Discuss its applications in ship hull inspection. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:**
Ultrasonic Testing uses **high-frequency mechanical sound waves (typically 1–25 MHz)** generated by a **piezoelectric transducer**. The sound waves are coupled into the test material via a couplant (gel, oil, or water). When the wave encounters an acoustic impedance mismatch (such as a flaw or the opposite surface of the material), a portion of the energy is reflected back to the transducer. The time-of-flight and amplitude of the reflected echo are analyzed to determine the **location, size, and orientation** of internal flaws.

**Procedure:**
1. **Surface preparation** — remove paint, rust, scale; apply couplant.
2. **Calibration** using a reference block (e.g., IIW V1 or V2 block) to set the velocity, sensitivity, and time base.
3. **Scanning** — the probe is moved across the surface in a systematic grid pattern.
4. **Detection** — echoes from flaws are recorded on the A-scan display.
5. **Evaluation** — flaw size is assessed using **DAC (Distance-Amplitude Correction)** curves or **DBP (DGS)** methods.
6. **Recording** — defects marked on the surface and a report generated.

**Advantages:**
- Highly sensitive to sub-surface defects.
- Provides accurate depth and size information.
- Portable, suitable for in-service inspection.
- No radiation hazard (unlike RT).
- Can detect **laminations** in incoming steel plates.

**Limitations:**
- Requires skilled, certified operators.
- Surface must be reasonably smooth and accessible.
- Difficult on **coarse-grained** materials (austenitic SS welds) due to high attenuation.
- Complex interpretation for non-specialists.

**Applications in Ship Hull Inspection:**
- **Lamination check** on incoming steel plates (mill stage).
- **Weld inspection** of butts in strength deck, side shell, bottom — both manual and **TOFD (Time-of-Flight Diffraction)** methods.
- **Thickness gauging** during in-service class surveys (corrosion mapping).
- Detection of **fatigue cracks** in hatch corners, bilge keels, rudder horns. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) With a neat sketch, explain the Radiographic Testing (RT) process. State the Image Quality Indicator (IQI) types used and explain the safety precautions required when performing RT onboard or in a shipyard. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:**
Radiographic Testing uses **penetrating electromagnetic radiation (X-rays or Gamma rays)** to produce a shadow image of internal defects on a radiographic film. Dense regions (sound metal) absorb more radiation, while voids or inclusions allow more radiation to pass through, darkening the corresponding area on the film.

**Setup & Procedure:**
1. A radiation source (X-ray tube for up to ~400 mm steel; **Ir-192** for 20–100 mm steel; **Co-60** for >100 mm steel) is positioned on one side of the test object.
2. A **film cassette** (containing film + lead screens + IQI) is placed on the opposite side.
3. The source is energized for a calculated **exposure time** based on source strength (Ci or Bq), source-to-film distance (SFD), material thickness, and density.
4. The film is developed in a darkroom.
5. The developed film is interpreted by a **Level II/III certified radiographer** using a viewing box with controlled luminance.

**Image Quality Indicator (IQI):**
- Also called **penetrameter**.
- **Wire-type IQI** (per ASTM E747) — a set of wires of different diameters (typically 1, 2, 3, 4, 5... up to 10) mounted in a transparent envelope.
- **Hole-type IQI / Duplex IQI** (per ASTM E1025) — a small plate with drilled holes.
- The IQI image must be visible on the radiograph to validate sensitivity (typically **2-2T** sensitivity, where T is the thickness).
- The IQI is placed on the **source side** of the object.

**Safety Precautions (Mandatory KTU point):**
1. **ALARA principle** — keep radiation As Low As Reasonably Achievable.
2. **Shielding** — RT performed inside shielded enclosures (concrete walls ≥ 2 m thick) or in **open-air exclusion zones** with controlled access (typically **minimum 30 m radius** for Ir-192).
3. **Personnel dosimetry** — every RT operator wears a **TLD (Thermoluminescent Dosimeter)** badge; records checked monthly.
4. **Warning signs & lights** — posted at entry points.
5. **Storage of sources** — Ir-192 / Co-60 stored in **locked, lead-shielded containers** when not in use; inventory log maintained.
6. **Emergency procedures** — recovery plan in case of source stuck in the exposure container; mock drills conducted quarterly.
7. **Approved by AERB (India) or equivalent national regulator** before any RT activity. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing DT with NDT applications:** Many students wrongly state that RT is used for surface defects — it is for **internal volumetric defects**. Surface defects use LPT or MPT.
> 2. **Forgetting safety in RT answers:** The 14-mark RT question **always expects safety precautions** (AERB, TLD, ALARA). Omitting these costs ~2 marks.
> 3. **Wrong UTS-HB formula:** The factor is **3.45**, not 3.0 or 4.0. Wrong constant = full marks lost.
> 4. **Skipping the VT step:** Any NDT procedure must begin with VT. Skipping VT loses a mark.
> 5. **Writing "UT uses X-rays":** UT uses **sound waves**, not X-rays. This is the most common silly mistake.
> 6. **Charpy vs Izod confusion:** Ship rules use **Charpy V-notch**, not Izod. Mentioning Izod in ship context loses marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Read this on the morning of the exam!)**

- **DT** = Destroys the specimen (Tensile, Impact, Hardness, Bend, Fatigue, Macro/Micro).
- **NDT** = Preserves the specimen (VT, LPT, MPT, ECT, UT, RT, AE).
- **UTS (MPa) = P_max / A₀**; **%El = (L_f − L₀)/L₀ × 100**.
- **UTS ≈ 3.45 × HB** (empirical for carbon steel).
- **Charpy energy** is read from the **dial in Joules**.
- **Charpy test temperature** is critical for **Arctic / Polar-class ships** (down to **−60 °C** for IACS PC 1).
- **Brinell formula** uses **P (kgf)**, **D (mm)**, **d (mm)** — beware unit trap.
- **UT thickness**: **T = (v × Δt) / 2**; **v in steel ≈ 5900 m/s**.
- **LPT works on non-porous materials only**; MPT works on **ferromagnetic only**; RT works on **all** but has radiation hazard.
- **Penetrameter (IQI)** is mandatory for **every RT exposure**.
- **AERB** regulates radioactive sources in India.
- **Sequence in any weld inspection**: **VT → LPT/MPT → UT/RT** (Surface first, then sub-surface).
- **Classification societies** (IRS, LR, DNV, ABS, BV) decide **which weld, which NDT, what acceptance level**.
- **Endurance limit for steel ≈ 0.5 × UTS**.
- **Failure of NDT** means condemnation; **failure of DT** in a single specimen triggers a **retest of two more specimens per ASTM A370** rules.
- **ASNT / ISNT Level I, II, III certification** is mandatory for NDT personnel.
- **TOFD (Time-of-Flight Diffraction)** is the modern UT upgrade for weld inspection.
- **Phase-Array UT (PAUT)** is replacing conventional UT in new shipyards for speed and coverage.

> **Final 1-line summary to memorize:** *"DT measures how strong a material is; NDT proves how good the part built from it actually is."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Launching - mechanical launching methods: Slip, lift, construction of slipways for end launch and side launch, launching curves, arresting arrangements, tipping, pivoting/plunging, drop off
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST631 - SHIP PRODUCTION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Launching - mechanical launching methods: Slip, lift, construction of slipways for end launch and side launch, launching curves, arresting arrangements, tipping, pivoting/plunging, drop off # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Launching in Ship Production — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Ship Launching** is the engineering process of transferring a newly built vessel from a land-based construction site (building berth or slipway) into the water, in a controlled, structurally safe, and hydrodynamically stable manner, such that the hull is neither overstressed nor capsized during the operation. In the context of **KTU PENST631 (Ship Production)**, launching belongs to the **outfitting-to-delivery transition** phase of shipbuilding and is the *first major wet-trial of the hull's structural integrity*. ### 1.2 Classification of Launching Methods The KTU 2024 syllabus groups launching broadly into **mechanical** and **non-mechanical (gravitational/free-flood)** techniques. This module exclusively covers the **mechanical launching methods**, which may be sub-classified as: | S.No | Mechanical Launching Method | Direction of Motion | Typical Vessel Size | |------|------------------------------|----------------------|----------------------| | 1 | **End Launch (Slip Launch)** | Bow-first or Stern-first along ship's centerline | Up to Very Large Crude Carriers (VLCCs) | | 2 | **Side Launch (Broadside Launch)** | Lateral roll across slipway | Medium-sized vessels | | 3 | **Mechanical Lift Launching** | Vertical lift from water | Small/medium vessels, repair docks | | 4 | **Tipping Launch** | Pivot about a fixed hinge | Barges, pontoons | | 5 | **Floating-Out (Pivoting/Plunging)** | Combination of pivot + float | Special vessels in graving docks | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sliding Ramp" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy**: Imagine a giant rectangular wooden block (the ship) resting on a wooden ramp (the *slipway*). To launch it, you grease the ramp with oil or soap, release a trigger pin, and let gravity pull the block down to a swimming pool at the bottom. To stop it, you attach drag chains (like bicycle hand-brakes). That, in essence, is **mechanical slip launching** — but scaled up to **300-metre-long, 200,000-tonne steel hulls** moving down a ramp angled typically at **1:12 to 1:24**. The "grease" in real shipbuilding is a thick tallow or grease applied to hardwood or steel ways, and the "trigger pin" is a **triggering mechanism** holding the ship until the precise moment of release. ### 1.4 Why Mechanical Launching? — The Engineering Necessity - **Gravity** is free, reliable, and provides a smooth acceleration profile (unlike explosive or explosive-charge methods). - The vessel's weight (**W in Tonnes**) supplies the kinetic energy needed to overcome sliding friction and hydrodynamic drag. - Controlled release enables prediction of stresses, trajectory, and final stopping distance using **launching curves**. - Mechanical launching is preferred over flooding graving docks for *commercial shipyards* because the **building berth is reusable immediately** after launch, giving higher throughput. ### 1.5 Standard KTU Metrics to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > Standard Slipway Inclinations: **1:12, 1:14, 1:16, 1:18, 1:20, 1:22, 1:24** (ratio of Vertical : Horizontal). The shallower the slope, the longer the slipway needed but the lower the final launch velocity. Standard launching friction coefficient (μ) on greased ways: **0.03 to 0.05**. Standard shipbuilding launch velocity at water entry: **4 to 6 m/s** for large vessels. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Mechanics of Slip Launching (End Launch) End launching is the **most common mechanical launch method** worldwide. The vessel slides longitudinally down an inclined slipway with its longitudinal axis aligned with the launch trajectory. ### 2.1.1 Major Structural Components of a Slipway | Component | Function | |-----------|----------| | **Ground Ways** | Fixed inclined timbers/steel beams embedded in the slipway foundation that bear the full weight of the vessel. | | **Sliding Ways (Launching Ways)** | Timber or steel members bolted to the hull's bottom; they slide over the ground ways. | | **Poppets (Fore & Aft)** | Wooden or steel upright supports that transfer hull weight to the sliding ways. Aft poppet is removed first to initiate launch. | | **Bilge Ways** | Outboard ways on each side of the keel, providing rotational stability and preventing tipping. | | **Keel Blocks** | Vertical supports under the keel during construction; removed before launch. | | **Shores & Slings** | Lateral supports preventing rolling; removed sequentially before launch. | | **Triggering Mechanism** | Hydraulic or mechanical release that frees the vessel at the planned instant. | | **Drag Chains & Chain Dishes** | Arrester system that decelerates and stops the vessel after water entry. | | **Camels & Dolphins** | Floating fenders that guide the vessel to its outfitting berth. | ### 2.1.2 The Step-by-Step Launch Sequence 1. **Pre-launch Inspection** — All shores, keel blocks, and stagings are removed except designated ones. 2. **Trigger Setting** — Trigger dogs engaged; sliding ways and ground ways greased with **tallow/grease (μ ≈ 0.03 to 0.05)**. 3. **Removal of Aft Poppet** — Releases the vessel's restraint at the stern. 4. **Trigger Release** — Hydraulic cylinders retract trigger dogs; the vessel begins to slide. 5. **Acceleration Phase** — Vessel accelerates down the inclined slipway under gravity. 6. **Water Entry** — Stern enters water; buoyant force increases rapidly. 7. **Bow Still on Ways** — Vessel rotates about bow as buoyancy lifts the stern (called the **"high point"** or **"peak"**). 8. **Bow Lift-Off** — Bow leaves the ways; vessel now fully waterborne but still moving. 9. **Deceleration Phase** — Drag chains and hydrodynamic drag stop the vessel. 10. **Mooring** — Tugs and mooring lines secure the vessel at the outfitting berth. ### 2.2 Side Launching (Broadside Launch) Side launching is used when: - Water depth at the end of the slipway is insufficient for end launch. - The vessel is too wide for the available slipway length. - The yard has a riverfront or canal-side configuration. > [!NOTE] > In side launching, the vessel's longitudinal axis is **perpendicular to the direction of motion**. The vessel slides sideways on **side ways** until the **drop-off point**, where it falls from the keel blocks onto the side launching ways. ### 2.3 Mechanical Lift Launching Lift launching systems include: - **Syncrolift** — A platform on hydraulic jacks that lifts vessels vertically out of water. - **Marine Travel Lift** — A mobile, self-propelled, wheeled gantry. - **Shiplift** — A larger, multi-platform dry dock system. These are predominantly used for **repair yards** rather than new-build launches but are covered in the KTU syllabus under mechanical methods. ### 2.4 The Launching Curves — KTU High-Yield Definitions Launching curves are **time-history plots** of critical parameters during the launch trajectory. The KTU 2024 module specifically identifies the following curves: | Curve Name | X-axis | Y-axis | Critical Use | |------------|--------|--------|--------------| | **Poppet Pressure Curve** | Distance along slipway | Reaction force at aft poppet | Ensures poppet load ≤ allowable | | **Hull Longitudinal Bending Curve** | Distance | Bending moment (kN·m) | Structural integrity check | | **Hull Transverse Pressure Curve** | Time/Distance | Local hull pressure (kN/m²) | Plate yielding check | | **Velocity Curve** | Distance/Time | Velocity (m/s) | Determines water-entry speed | | **Trim/Heel Curve** | Distance | Vessel trim angle (degrees) | Stability check | | **Drag Force Curve** | Distance | Drag force from chains (kN) | Arresting system design | ### 2.5 Arresting Arrangements > [!IMPORTANT] > **Arresting** (or **Check**) **Arrangements** are mandatory safety systems that decelerate and stop the vessel after launch. Without them, the vessel would overrun the slipway end, with catastrophic consequences. Common types: - **Drag Chains** — Heavy chains dragged over chain locks/dishes to dissipate kinetic energy via friction. - **Wire Rope Arresters** — Cables with hydraulic buffers. - **Wedge Arresters** — Mechanical wedges that engage sliding ways. - **Hydraulic Buffers** — Piston-cylinder devices that absorb energy. ### 2.6 Tipping, Pivoting, and Drop-off | Method | Description | |--------|-------------| | **Tipping** | Vessel pivots about a fixed hinge/forepoppet until it slides off into the water. Used for **narrow waterways** like rivers. | | **Pivoting** | Vessel rotates about a pivot point on the slipway before water entry. Used in **graving docks** when water depth is controlled by flooding. | | **Plunging** | Sudden vertical drop of the bow/stern during water entry — undesirable but inevitable in **side launch drop-off** events. | | **Drop-off** | In side launching, the vessel "drops off" the keel blocks (about **1.5 to 3.0 metres**) onto the side ways just before water entry, generating large impact loads. | ### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU formulas for Module 4 — all dimensions in SI units.** | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---------|---------|-------| | 1 | $W = m \cdot g$ | Weight of vessel | Newtons (N) | | 2 | $F_{gravity} = W \cdot \sin\theta$ | Component of weight along slipway | N | | 3 | $F_{friction} = \mu \cdot W \cdot \cos\theta$ | Frictional resistance on sliding ways | N | | 4 | $F_{net} = W(\sin\theta - \mu \cos\theta)$ | Net accelerating force | N | | 5 | $a = g(\sin\theta - \mu \cos\theta)$ | Acceleration of vessel on slipway | m/s² | | 6 | $v = \sqrt{2 \cdot a \cdot s}$ | Velocity after travelling distance **s** along slope | m/s | | 7 | $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$ | Kinematic identity | m²/s² | | 8 | $v^2 = 2gs(\sin\theta - \mu\cos\theta)$ | Launch velocity at water entry | m²/s² | | 9 | $p_{hull} = \dfrac{W_{block}}{A_{contact}}$ | Local hull pressure on sliding way | N/m² (Pa) | | 10 | $F_{drag} = \mu_c \cdot T \cdot n$ | Drag force from **n** chains, each of tension **T** with chain-coefficient **μ_c** | N | | 11 | $KE = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Kinetic energy at water entry | Joules (J) | | 12 | $E_{absorbed} = n \cdot \mu_c \cdot T \cdot d$ | Energy absorbed by drag chains over distance **d** | Joules (J) | | 13 | $\tan\theta = \dfrac{1}{N}$ | Slope ratio **1:N** | dimensionless | | 14 | $BM = \dfrac{I_{T}}{V_{disp}}$ | Metacentric radius for heel stability | m | | 15 | $F_{buoy} = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot V_{sub}$ | Buoyant force on submerged volume **V_sub** | N | > [!IMPORTANT] > Always use **θ in radians** for trigonometric functions in calculators, unless your calculator is in **DEG mode**. This is a common KTU exam pitfall. ### 2.8 Engineering Utility Beyond the Syllabus - **Shipyard Master Planning**: Slipway angle and length dictate the layout of the entire yard. - **Port Engineering**: Side-launch slipways require carefully dredged turning basins and outfitting berths. - **Insurance & Classification**: Det Norske Veritas (DNV), Lloyd's Register, and Bureau Veritas require launching curve approval before issuing classification certificates. - **Naval Architecture**: Launching is the first test of hull girder strength — the data feeds back into design rules for future vessels. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Launch Velocity on an Inclined Slipway We derive the **velocity of the vessel at water entry** from first principles using Newton's Second Law on an inclined plane. ### Step 1 — Free-Body Diagram of the Vessel on Slipway When the vessel rests on the inclined slipway (angle **θ** with horizontal), three forces act along the direction of motion: - **Gravity component along slope** (driving force): $F_g = W \sin\theta$ - **Frictional resistance** (opposing motion): $F_f = \mu W \cos\theta$ - **Hydrodynamic and aerodynamic drag** (negligible for slow launch): ignored here where **W = mg** is the displacement weight, and **μ** is the coefficient of friction between sliding and ground ways. ### Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law The net force along the slipway is: $$ F_{net} = W \sin\theta - \mu W \cos\theta $$ Dividing by mass **m = W/g** gives the acceleration: $$ a = \frac{F_{net}}{m} = \frac{W(\sin\theta - \mu \cos\theta)}{W/g} $$ Therefore: $$ a = g(\sin\theta - \mu \cos\theta) $$ ### Step 3 — Apply Kinematic Equation for Velocity Assuming the vessel starts from rest (**u = 0**) and travels a distance **s** along the slope, the kinematic equation **v² = u² + 2as** gives: $$ v^2 = 0 + 2 \cdot g(\sin\theta - \mu \cos\theta) \cdot s $$ Therefore: $$ v = \sqrt{2 g s (\sin\theta - \mu \cos\theta)} $$ This is the **fundamental launch-velocity equation** demanded by KTU problems. ### Step 4 — Verify Dimensional Consistency - $g$ → m/s² - $s$ → m - $\sin\theta$, $\cos\theta$ → dimensionless - $v^2$ → m²/s² ✓ - $v$ → m/s ✓ ### Step 5 — Physical Limitation Check For the vessel to actually launch (i.e., **a > 0**), we require: $$ \sin\theta - \mu \cos\theta > 0 $$ $$ \tan\theta > \mu $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Engineering Rule**: The slipway angle **θ must exceed the friction angle φ where tan φ = μ**. Otherwise the vessel will not slide at all. With μ = 0.05, we need **tan θ > 0.05**, i.e., **θ > 2.86°**, which corresponds to a slope steeper than **1:20**. This is why slipways are never shallower than **1:24**. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style) **Problem**: A vessel of mass **25,000 tonnes** is to be launched on a slipway inclined at **1 in 18**. The coefficient of friction is **μ = 0.04**. The vessel travels a total distance of **180 m** along the slope before water entry. Determine: (a) The acceleration of the vessel. (b) The velocity at water entry. (c) The minimum slope required for the vessel to launch. ### Given Data $$ m = 25{,}000 \text{ tonnes} = 25 \times 10^6 \text{ kg} $$ $$ \theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{18}\right) $$ $$ \mu = 0.04, \quad s = 180 \text{ m}, \quad g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 $$ ### Solution — Part (a): Acceleration $$ \theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{18}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.05556) = 3.180^\circ $$ $$ \sin(3.180^\circ) = 0.05551 $$ $$ \cos(3.180^\circ) = 0.99846 $$ Substituting: $$ a = g(\sin\theta - \mu \cos\theta) $$ $$ a = 9.81 \times (0.05551 - 0.04 \times 0.99846) $$ $$ a = 9.81 \times (0.05551 - 0.03994) $$ $$ a = 9.81 \times 0.01557 $$ $$ \boxed{a = 0.1527 \text{ m/s}^2} $$ ### Solution — Part (b): Velocity at Water Entry $$ v = \sqrt{2 g s (\sin\theta - \mu \cos\theta)} $$ $$ v = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 180 \times 0.01557} $$ $$ v = \sqrt{54.99} $$ $$ \boxed{v = 7.416 \text{ m/s}} $$ > [!NOTE] > The computed velocity (~7.4 m/s) is slightly above the standard recommended **4–6 m/s** for very large vessels, indicating that this slope may be too steep for the given length, or that more friction-reducing grease should be applied. ### Solution — Part (c): Minimum Slope For launch to occur: $$ \tan\theta_{min} = \mu = 0.04 $$ $$ \theta_{min} = \tan^{-1}(0.04) = 2.290^\circ $$ Slope ratio: $$ \frac{1}{N} = 0.04 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N = \frac{1}{0.04} = 25 $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Minimum slope} = 1 : 25} $$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Launching Methods | Parameter | End Launch (Slip) | Side Launch | Lift Launch (Syncrolift) | Tipping | |-----------|-------------------|--------------|--------------------------|---------| | **Direction** | Longitudinal | Broadside | Vertical | Pivot over hinge | | **Slope/Angle** | 1:12 to 1:24 | 1:14 to 1:24 | Vertical | Up to 30° tilt | | **Driving Force** | Gravity | Gravity | Hydraulic | Gravity + hinge torque | | **Vessel Size Limit** | Up to VLCC (~330 m) | Up to Panamax | Up to ~25,000 DWT | Barges/pontoons | | **Water Depth Required** | Large at slipway end | Moderate, broad area | Controlled in dock | Small | | **Site Constraint** | Long foreshore | Wide channel | Flat dock | Narrow river/canal | | **Risk of Plunging** | Low–Moderate | High (drop-off) | None | Low | | **Cost** | Moderate | Moderate | High | Low | | **Reusability of Berth** | Immediate (after clearing) | Immediate | Immediate | Immediate | | **KTU Frequency** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | --- ## 3.4 Drag Chain Arresting — Energy Balance Derivation When the vessel is waterborne and moving with velocity **v**, the drag chains must dissipate its kinetic energy. ### Step 1 — Kinetic Energy at Water Entry $$ KE = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 $$ ### Step 2 — Energy Dissipated by Drag Chains If **n** drag chains each of working tension **T** drag over chain locks for a distance **d**, with chain-friction coefficient **μ_c**: $$ E_{chain} = n \cdot \mu_c \cdot T \cdot d $$ ### Step 3 — Energy Balance (Vessel Stops) For complete arrest: $$ \frac{1}{2} m v^2 = n \cdot \mu_c \cdot T \cdot d $$ Solving for **n** (number of chains required): $$ n = \frac{m v^2}{2 \mu_c T d} $$ ### Worked Example **Problem**: A 15,000-tonne vessel enters water at **5 m/s**. Drag chains have a working tension of **200 kN** each, μ_c = 0.25, and the vessel must stop within **40 m**. Find the number of chains required. $$ m = 15{,}000 \text{ tonnes} = 15 \times 10^6 \text{ kg} $$ $$ KE = \frac{1}{2} \times 15 \times 10^6 \times 5^2 = 187.5 \times 10^6 \text{ J} $$ $$ E_{chain} = n \times 0.25 \times 200{,}000 \times 40 = n \times 2 \times 10^6 \text{ J} $$ Setting **KE = E_chain**: $$ 187.5 \times 10^6 = n \times 2 \times 10^6 $$ $$ \boxed{n = 93.75 \rightarrow 94 \text{ chains}} $$ > [!WARNING] > In practice, only **2 to 8 heavy drag chains** are used because each chain weighs several tonnes. The 94 chains in the example would be replaced by a smaller number of much heavier chains (or hydraulic buffers) in real shipyards. The KTU exam formula assumes idealized per-chain tension. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-Launch Slipway — Longitudinal Section ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph BERTH["BUILDING BERTH (Land)"] A1["Hull under construction

Name **two** common types. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Function (2 marks):** The arresting arrangement decelerates and stops the vessel after it becomes waterborne, preventing overrun of the slipway end, damage to the vessel or jetty, and ensuring safe transition to the outfitting berth. **Two Types (1 mark):** 1. **Drag Chains** — heavy chains dragged over chain dishes to dissipate kinetic energy by friction. 2. **Hydraulic Buffers** — piston-cylinder energy absorbers. (Alternatively: Wedge Arresters, Wire Rope Arresters.) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **Q:** With the aid of neat sketches, describe the construction of an **end-launching slipway**. Explain the launching sequence and derive the expression for the velocity of the vessel at water entry. A 30,000-tonne vessel is launched on a slipway of slope 1 in 20 with a coefficient of friction μ = 0.05. The vessel travels 150 m along the slope. Find: (a) The velocity at water entry. (b) The minimum slope for the vessel to launch. (c) Comment on whether the velocity is within the safe launching range of 4–6 m/s. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / KTU 2024 Scheme Model Paper]** #### Model Solution **(i) Construction of End-Launch Slipway (5 marks):** The end-launch slipway consists of: - **Ground ways** — fixed inclined beams (slope 1:18 to 1:24) embedded in a concrete foundation. - **Sliding ways** — greased timbers/steel members bolted to the hull bottom. - **Fore and aft poppets** — vertical supports taking the hull's weight; aft poppet is removed first. - **Bilge ways** — outboard sliding members providing lateral and rotational stability. - **Keel blocks** — central supports under the keel (removed before launch). - **Trigger mechanism** — hydraulic or mechanical release dogs. - **Chain dishes and drag chains** — arresting arrangement at the water-entry end. - **Camels and dolphins** — floating fenders for guiding the waterborne vessel. (Refer to Section 4.1 diagram for visual.) **(ii) Launching Sequence (3 marks):** 1. Pre-launch inspection and removal of shores and keel blocks. 2. Application of tallow/grease on ways. 3. Removal of aft poppet. 4. Trigger release. 5. Vessel slides down the slope, accelerating under gravity. 6. Stern enters water → buoyant force increases rapidly. 7. Bow lifts off the ways. 8. Drag chains arrest the vessel. 9. Tugs take over and berth the vessel at the outfitting berth. **(iii) Derivation of Velocity Expression (3 marks):** Net force along slope: $$ F_{net} = W\sin\theta - \mu W\cos\theta = W(\sin\theta - \mu\cos\theta) $$ Acceleration: $$ a = \frac{F_{net}}{m} = g(\sin\theta - \mu\cos\theta) $$ Using $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$ with $u = 0$: $$ \boxed{v = \sqrt{2gs(\sin\theta - \mu\cos\theta)}} $$ **(iv) Numerical Solution (3 marks):** Given: m = 30,000 t = $3 \times 10^7$ kg, slope 1:20, μ = 0.05, s = 150 m. $$ \theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{20}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.05) = 2.862^\circ $$ $$ \sin(2.862^\circ) = 0.04994, \quad \cos(2.862^\circ) = 0.99875 $$ **(a) Velocity at water entry:** $$ v = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 150 \times (0.04994 - 0.05 \times 0.99875)} $$ $$ v = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 150 \times (0.04994 - 0.04994)} $$ $$ v = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 150 \times 0.00000^{+}} $$ > [!WARNING] > **The above calculation shows v ≈ 0!** With μ = 0.05 and slope exactly 1:20, the friction almost equals the gravity component. This is a *borderline case*. The examiner's intent here is to show that **the vessel barely moves**. In a real exam, students should compute more precisely: More precisely: $$ \sin(2.8624^\circ) = 0.049938, \quad \mu \cos\theta = 0.05 \times 0.998752 = 0.049938 $$ So acceleration ≈ 0. Realistically, μ is slightly less than 0.05 due to the grease, and the vessel will accelerate very slowly. **Assuming effective μ = 0.045 for a typical greased condition:** $$ \sin\theta - \mu\cos\theta = 0.04994 - 0.045 \times 0.99875 = 0.04994 - 0.04494 = 0.00500 $$ $$ v = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 150 \times 0.00500} = \sqrt{14.715} = 3.836 \text{ m/s} $$ $$ \boxed{v \approx 3.84 \text{ m/s}} $$ **(b) Minimum slope:** For launch: $\tan\theta_{min} = \mu = 0.05$ $$ \theta_{min} = \tan^{-1}(0.05) = 2.862^\circ $$ Slope ratio = **1 : 20**. **(c) Comment on Safety:** The computed velocity of **3.84 m/s** is **below the safe range of 4–6 m/s**, indicating that the slipway slope is too gentle for the given conditions. To achieve safer launching, the slope should be steepened to **1:18** or higher, or the friction coefficient should be reduced further by more effective lubrication. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **Q:** Compare **end launching** and **side launching** methods of ships with respect to their constructional features, applications, advantages, and limitations. With neat sketches, explain the **drop-off phenomenon** in side launching and discuss the role of **drag chains** as an arresting arrangement. A 20,000-tonne vessel enters water at 5 m/s. If 6 drag chains of working tension 250 kN each are deployed with chain-friction coefficient μ_c = 0.20, determine the distance required to stop the vessel. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper / 2024 Scheme]** #### Model Solution **(i) Comparison: End Launch vs Side Launch (6 marks):** | Feature | End Launch | Side Launch | |---------|------------|-------------| | **Direction of motion** | Longitudinal | Lateral (broadside) | | **Slipway slope** | 1:12 to 1:24 | 1:14 to 1:24 | | **Vessel orientation** | Bow or stern first | Beam-first | | **Water depth needed** | Large at slipway end | Moderate, broad | | **Site suitability** | Long foreshore | Confined waterfronts | | **Typical vessels** | All sizes, incl. VLCCs | Up to Panamax | | **Advantages** | Higher control, lower impact | Lower slope, less dredging | | **Limitations** | Long slipway required | Drop-off, plunging risks | **(ii) Drop-off Phenomenon (3 marks):** In side launching, the hull initially rests on **keel blocks and bilge blocks**. Just before water entry, the **keel blocks terminate** and the vessel "drops off" by **1.5 to 3.0 m** onto the **side launching ways**. This sudden release: - Generates **impact forces** on the side ways (F_impact = m·g + ΔKE/Δt). - May cause **plunging** if the vessel bows into the water abruptly. - Requires **reinforced side ways** and **heavy shores** to control the descent. **(iii) Drag Chain Role (2 marks):** Drag chains are heavy chains (typically 6–8 chains, each weighing several tonnes) that: - Are paid out from chain locks as the vessel moves. - Drag over chain dishes, dissipating kinetic energy via friction (μ_c ≈ 0.15 to 0.30). - Provide controlled deceleration to stop the vessel within a safe distance (typically 30–60 m). **(iv) Numerical Solution (3 marks):** Given: m = 20,000 t = $2 \times 10^7$ kg, v = 5 m/s, n = 6, T = 250 kN = $2.5 \times 10^5$ N, μ_c = 0.20. KE at water entry: $$ KE = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 10^7 \times 5^2 = 2.5 \times 10^8 \text{ J} $$ Energy absorbed per unit distance: $$ \frac{E_{chain}}{d} = n \cdot \mu_c \cdot T = 6 \times 0.20 \times 2.5 \times 10^5 = 3 \times 10^5 \text{ J/m} $$ For complete arrest: $$ d = \frac{KE}{n \mu_c T} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^8}{3 \times 10^5} $$ $$ \boxed{d = 833.33 \text{ m}} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**: A distance of **833 m** is **impractical** for real shipyards (typical stopping distance is 30–60 m). The examiner's intent is to test the formula; the unrealistic answer indicates that **6 chains of 250 kN tension are grossly insufficient** for a 20,000-tonne vessel. In practice, **hydraulic buffers** or **wedge arresters** would supplement the chains. Students should comment on this practical limitation in the answer. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > > 1. **Slope Ratio Confusion**: Slope 1:18 means 1 m vertical per 18 m horizontal, NOT 18° angle. Use $\theta = \tan^{-1}(1/18)$. > 2. **Friction Direction**: Friction always **opposes motion** — directed UP the slope when vessel slides DOWN. > 3. **Units**: Use **SI units throughout** (m, m/s, N, J, kg). Convert tonnes to kg by × 1000. > 4. **Drag Chain Formula**: Forgetting the **number of chains n** in the energy balance — this is a 2-mark deduction in KTU papers. > 5. **Misnaming "Plunging"**: Plunging is specifically the **sudden vertical drop** during water entry, NOT the entire launching process. > 6. **Tipping vs Plunging**: Tipping is a *planned* pivoting launch method; plunging is an *undesirable* consequence of side-launch drop-off. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Launching** is the controlled transition of a vessel from construction site to water. - **Mechanical launching methods** include slip launch (end & side), lift launch (syncrolift, travel lift), tipping, and pivoting/plunging. - **End launching** is the most common — vessel slides longitudinally down an inclined slipway (1:12 to 1:24 slope). - **Side launching** is used when end-launch water depth is insufficient; involves drop-off and plunging risks. - **Launching curves** include poppet pressure, hull pressure, velocity, trim, and drag-force histories. - **Arresting arrangements** are mandatory: drag chains, hydraulic buffers, wedge arresters, wire ropes. - **Critical formula**: $v = \sqrt{2gs(\sin\theta - \mu\cos\theta)}$ — derive from Newton's 2nd law and kinematics. - **Minimum slope rule**: $\tan\theta_{min} = \mu$. With μ = 0.05, slope must exceed **1:20**. - **Drop-off** in side launching causes impact forces; vessel falls 1.5 to 3.0 m onto side ways. - **Drag chain energy balance**: $\frac{1}{2}mv^2 = n \mu_c T d$ — solve for any unknown. - **Practical launching velocity**: **4 to 6 m/s** for large vessels; beyond 7 m/s is unsafe. - **Bilge ways** prevent tipping during end launch. - **Fore poppet** holds the bow until trigger release; aft poppet is removed first. - **Tipping launches** use a hinged cradle; vessel pivots until CG passes beyond the hinge. - **Syncrolift / Shiplift** systems are vertical mechanical lifts, not gravity-based. - **KTU frequently tests**: derivation of launch velocity, drag chain calculation, comparison of methods, and identification of components from sketches.

topics.title: Other methods of launching: Launching by floating off (building dock, floating dock), launching using air bags
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST631 - SHIP PRODUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Other methods of launching: Launching by floating off (building dock, floating dock), launching using air bags

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Alternative Launching Methods

Conventional ship launching (side launch, end launch via slipways) is suitable for most commercial vessels. However, when vessels become **exceptionally large**, **wide-beam**, or **structurally unsuitable for the impact loads of a traditional slide**, shipbuilders must resort to alternative launching strategies. The three principal non-conventional methods covered in this module are:

1. **Launching by Floating Off (Building Dock)**
2. **Launching by Floating Off (Floating Dock)**
3. **Launching using Air Bags**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** These methods collectively belong to the category of **"Gravity-Free"** or **"Buoyancy-Assisted"** launching systems because they do not rely on the gravitational sliding of the hull down an inclined surface. Instead, they manipulate buoyancy, hydrostatic pressure, and rolling friction to bring the ship into the water.

---

## 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU Board-Standard Terminology)

### A. Launching by Floating Off
A launching method in which the ship is constructed in a dewatered chamber (or on a submersible platform), and water is subsequently admitted to flood the chamber, causing the vessel to **achieve positive buoyancy and float free** of its building berth without any longitudinal sliding motion.

### B. Building Dock (Graving Dock) Method
A permanent civil structure consisting of an enclosed basin with a removable caisson or flap gate. The dock is pumped dry, the ship is built (or partly built) inside it, and during launching, the dock is flooded so the vessel floats.

### C. Floating Dock Method
A portable, hollow, pontoon-shaped steel structure with ballast tanks and a U-shaped cross-section. The dock is submerged by ballasting, the ship is floated/dragged over the keel blocks, and then the dock is deballasted to lift the vessel clear of the water surface, after which it is towed away.

### D. Air Bag Launching
A non-slipway method where **heavy-duty inflated rubber bags** (cylindrical or rectangular) are placed under the hull. The vessel is rolled down a gentle slope (or off a beach) on the bags, with the bags acting as **low-friction rollers** between the hull and the ground.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — Building Dock:** Imagine building a toy boat inside an empty swimming pool. You finish painting it, then open the pool's drain to let water in. The boat lifts up and floats. That is exactly what a building dock does — it is just a much larger, engineered version of the same pool.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — Floating Dock:** Picture a giant rectangular life-jacket with a hollow middle. You push the boat into the middle (while the life-jacket is underwater), then squeeze the air out of the chambers so the whole thing rises — the boat is lifted on top. Now you pull the whole assembly away from shore.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 3 — Air Bags:** Think of an air mattress under a heavy suitcase. When you inflate the mattress, the suitcase glides smoothly because the contact is now between the mattress surface and the floor, not the suitcase itself. Multiply this concept to industrial scale and you have a ship-on-air-bag launch.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants (Bolded for KTU Reference)

| Metric | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Inflatable rubber bag diameter | **0.8 – 1.8** | m |
| Working pressure of air bag | **0.2 – 0.4** | MPa |
| Air bag launch slope | **1:10 to 1:20** | ratio |
| Friction coefficient (bag on ground) | **0.02 – 0.05** | dimensionless |
| Floating dock lift capacity | **Up to 100,000+** | tonnes |
| Building dock flooding time | **2 – 6** | hours |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Each Method

### 2.1.1 Why Floating-Off Methods Exist

A side launch subjects the hull to **high stern/bow reaction forces** and **bending moments** as the vessel rotates about the poppets. For vessels like:
- **FPSOs (Floating Production Storage and Offloading units)**
- **Drillships and semi-submersibles**
- **Aircraft carriers with very wide beams**
- **Jack-up rigs with delicate leg structures**

…the structural impact during a slide launch is unacceptable. The solution is to **eliminate sliding altogether** and instead use **buoyancy** to introduce the ship to water in a controlled, static, near-zero-velocity manner.

### 2.1.2 Why Air Bags Are Used

In many developing shipbuilding nations and for small-to-medium vessels (fishing trawlers, tugs, small coasters), **investing in a slipway is uneconomical**. Air bags provide:
- **Low capital cost**
- **Reusability** (one set of bags can launch many vessels)
- **Adaptability** to beach launching sites with poor infrastructure

---

## 2.2 Operational Breakdown — Building Dock Method

**Step 1 — Preparation:** The graving dock is pumped dry using main and drain pumps. Keel blocks and bilge blocks are laid.

**Step 2 — Construction:** The ship is built inside the dock. Hull, superstructure, and outfitting are completed up to the desired stage (often fully outfitted with engines, masts, and even painted).

**Step 3 — Closure Check:** All dock seals (caisson or flap gate) are inspected; dock pumps are secured.

**Step 4 — Flooding:** Seawater is admitted through **flooding valves** (typically in the caisson or dock floor). Water level rises in a controlled manner.

**Step 5 — Lift-off:** As water depth increases, the ship's displacement is reached. The vessel rises off the keel blocks with **zero sliding velocity** — only a gentle vertical lift.

**Step 6 — Removal:** Tugs maneuver the floating vessel out of the dock for fitting out, trials, or delivery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Point:** The ship must have sufficient **longitudinal and transverse stability** in the partially flooded dock to remain upright during lift-off. If not, the dock is flooded asymmetrically or in stages with intermediate stability checks.

---

## 2.3 Operational Breakdown — Floating Dock Method

A floating dock consists of a **pontoon** (lower hull) and two **wing walls** (upper side structures). The pontoon is divided into ballast tanks.

**Phase 1 — Submergence:** Ballast tanks are flooded. The dock submerges until the deck (pontoon top) is below water level, with the wing walls protruding above the surface.

**Phase 2 — Positioning:** The ship (often pre-built elsewhere or alongside) is towed over the submerged pontoon, or the dock is positioned under the partly-built vessel.

**Phase 3 — Deballasting:** Water is pumped out of the ballast tanks. The dock rises, lifting the ship clear of the water.

**Phase 4 — Towing:** The ship rests on the keel blocks of the floating dock. The entire assembly is towed to the fitting-out berth, where the ship is lowered into the water (by re-ballasting) or simply side-launched off the dock.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Aid:** Remember the acronym **"S-P-D-T"**: **S**ubmerge → **P**osition → **D**eballast → **T**ow.

---

## 2.4 Operational Breakdown — Air Bag Launching

**Step 1 — Site Preparation:** A gentle earthen or concrete slope is graded (typically 1:15 to 1:20). The surface must be smooth and free of sharp objects.

**Step 2 — Bag Placement:** Cylindrical rubber air bags (high-pressure, multi-layer) are arranged parallel to the launch direction beneath the hull. Spacing is typically **1.5 – 3 m**.

**Step 3 — Inflation:** Bags are inflated to working pressure using compressed air. The hull lifts off the building supports.

**Step 4 — Roll-out:** The vessel is gradually winched or pulled down the slope. As the leading bags emerge into the water, they are deflated and recovered; trailing bags are deflated and re-positioned at the bow in a continuous cycle.

**Step 5 — Water Entry:** The vessel slides into the water at the toe of the slope, supported by the remaining bags.

**Step 6 — Recovery:** All bags are deflated and retrieved for the next launch.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` notation instead of `\vert x \vert` pipes to avoid table breakage.

| Formula | Expression | Variables | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Buoyancy (Archimedes) | $F_b = \rho \cdot g \cdot V$ | $F_b$ = buoyant force (N); $\rho$ = water density (kg/m³); $V$ = displaced volume (m³); $g$ = 9.81 m/s² | Determines when ship floats off keel blocks |
| Required water depth for float-off | $h \geq \frac{\Delta}{\rho \cdot A_{wp}}$ | $\Delta$ = displacement (kg); $A_{wp}$ = waterplane area (m²); $h$ = water depth in dock (m) | Building/floating dock flooding height |
| Roll-out force (air bag) | $F_{roll} = \mu \cdot W$ | $F_{roll}$ = required pulling force (N); $\mu$ = rolling friction coefficient; $W$ = vessel weight (N) | Air bag launch design |
| Air bag working pressure | $P = \frac{W_{bag}}{L \cdot D}$ | $P$ = internal pressure (Pa); $W_{bag}$ = supported load (N); $L$ = effective length (m); $D$ = diameter (m) | Bag sizing |
| Floating dock lift capacity | $W_{dock} = \rho \cdot g \cdot V_{ponton}$ | $V_{ponton}$ = submerged pontoon volume (m³) | Selecting floating dock size |
| Side launch reaction (comparison) | $R = \frac{W}{2} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{x}{L}\right)$ | $R$ = reaction at poppets; $x$ = distance from CG to poppet | Why floating-off avoids impact |
| Kinetic energy during launch | $KE = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{W}{g} \cdot v^2$ | $v$ = launch velocity (m/s) | Energy that must be absorbed (slipway vs. air bag) |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Use Case:** The South Korean shipyards (Hyundai, Samsung, Daewoo) routinely use **floating docks** for VLCC (Very Large Crude Carriers) and ULCS (Ultra Large Container Ships) where the beam-to-length ratio would cause dangerous pendulum motion during a side launch. The **building dock method** is preferred for naval aircraft carriers (e.g., US Navy's Nimitz-class) because the controlled environment allows full outfitting before float-off.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Use Case:** The Chinese shipbuilding industry pioneered the use of **air bag launching** in the 1980s for small commercial vessels. Today, air bag launching is standard practice in many Southeast Asian, Chinese, and Indian shipyards for vessels up to 10,000 DWT.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Water Depth for Building-Dock Float-Off

When a ship sits on keel blocks in a dry building dock, the only force holding it up is the **block reaction**. The instant the ship floats off, this reaction becomes zero, and the **buoyant force equals the ship's lightweight weight**.

### Given
- Ship's lightweight (construction) displacement: $\Delta = W/g$ (in kg, with $W$ in Newtons)
- Waterplane area of the hull at float-off draft: $A_{wp}$ (m²)
- Density of dock water: $\rho$ (kg/m³), typically 1025 kg/m³ for seawater
- Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81$ m/s²

### Step-by-Step Derivation

The hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ in the dock exerts an upward force per unit area of the hull bottom equal to $\rho \cdot g \cdot h$ (neglecting any partial-block contact).

The total buoyant force on the hull when fully surrounded by water of depth $h$ covering the keel blocks is:

$$F_{buoy} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h \cdot A_{wp}$$

For the ship to **just lift off** the keel blocks, this buoyant force must at least equal the ship's weight:

$$F_{buoy} \geq W$$

$$\rho \cdot g \cdot h \cdot A_{wp} \geq W$$

Solving for the critical depth $h_{min}$:

$$h_{min} = \frac{W}{\rho \cdot g \cdot A_{wp}}$$

Since $W = \Delta \cdot g$, this simplifies to:

$$h_{min} = \frac{\Delta}{\rho \cdot A_{wp}}$$

**Interpretation:** If the dock can be filled to depth $h \geq h_{min}$, the ship will float free. The designer must include a safety margin of typically **10 – 15 %** above this theoretical minimum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In practice, dock flooding continues until the waterline is well above the keel blocks, ensuring the ship is fully supported by water and not still resting on blocks. The transit draft (float-off draft) of the ship must be **less than the navigable depth** of the dock sill and harbor approach.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Pulling Force Required for Air Bag Launching

A ship resting on air bags is effectively a **rolling-load problem**. Unlike sliding friction (where $\mu_s$ is high), rolling friction is governed by a much smaller coefficient.

### Given
- Vessel lightweight: $W$ (N)
- Number of bags supporting the vessel: $n$
- Rolling friction coefficient (bag on ground): $\mu_r$ ≈ 0.02 – 0.05
- Slope angle of the launching way: $\theta$ (in radians, for small slopes $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta$)

### Derivation (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Identify the forces along the slope:**

The component of weight along the slope (downhill) is:
$$F_{down} = W \cdot \sin\theta$$

The normal force on the bags from the hull is:
$$N = W \cdot \cos\theta \approx W \quad \text{(for small }\theta\text{)}$$

**Step 2 — Frictional resistance from the bags:**

Each bag contributes a rolling friction force $F_{r,bag} = \mu_r \cdot (W/n)$. For $n$ bags, total rolling resistance is:

$$F_{r,total} = \mu_r \cdot W \cdot \cos\theta$$

**Step 3 — Net force on the vessel:**

The vessel will roll on its own (assisted by gravity) if the downhill component exceeds friction:

$$W \cdot \sin\theta > \mu_r \cdot W \cdot \cos\theta$$

For self-launching: $\tan\theta > \mu_r$. With $\mu_r \approx 0.03$, a slope of $1:20$ ($\tan\theta = 0.05$) easily satisfies this.

**Step 4 — If winching is required (gentler slope):**

The required winch pull is:
$$F_{winch} = \mu_r \cdot W \cdot \cos\theta - W \cdot \sin\theta$$

If this is positive, the winch must pull uphill; if negative, a **brake/drag** is needed.

**Step 5 — Simplified flat-ground formula (for analysis):**

$$F_{roll} = \mu_r \cdot W$$

This is the **standard design equation** used in KTU problems.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Floating Dock Submergence and Lift

### Given
- Pontoon length: $L_p$ (m)
- Pontoon width: $B_p$ (m)
- Pontoon height: $H_p$ (m)
- Ship's weight to be lifted: $W_{ship}$ (N)
- Density of water: $\rho$ (kg/m³)

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Submerged volume of pontoon at submergence depth $d$:**

$$V_{sub} = L_p \cdot B_p \cdot d$$

**Step 2 — Buoyant force on the dock alone (no ship):**

$$F_{b,dock} = \rho \cdot g \cdot V_{sub} = \rho \cdot g \cdot L_p \cdot B_p \cdot d$$

**Step 3 — Lift condition (with ship on top):**

To lift the ship off the water surface, the dock's buoyant force must exceed the combined weight of the dock itself ($W_{dock}$) and the ship ($W_{ship}$):

$$\rho \cdot g \cdot L_p \cdot B_p \cdot d \geq W_{dock} + W_{ship}$$

Solving for the required submergence depth:

$$d_{min} = \frac{W_{dock} + W_{ship}}{\rho \cdot g \cdot L_p \cdot B_p}$$

**Step 4 — Freeboard check:**

After lifting the ship, the dock's deck is at waterline. The wing walls (height $H_{wing}$) must remain above water to keep the dock seaworthy:

$$H_{wing} \geq d_{max} - d_{min}$$

This is a critical stability and buoyancy check performed by naval architects.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Exam Favorite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **directly examinable** in KTU 2024 ESE questions.

| Parameter | Building Dock | Floating Dock | Air Bag |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Capital Cost** | Very High (civil works) | Very High (steel structure) | Low |
| **Maximum Vessel Size** | Unlimited (in theory) | Up to ~100,000+ tonnes | Up to ~10,000 tonnes |
| **Construction Environment** | Fully enclosed, weather-proof | Exposed on pontoon | Open beach/yard |
| **Launch Velocity** | Near-zero (controlled float-off) | Near-zero (controlled lift) | 1 – 5 m/s (rolling) |
| **Structural Impact on Hull** | Negligible | Negligible | Moderate (bag pressure points) |
| **Outfitting Before Launch** | 80 – 100 % possible | 60 – 90 % possible | 20 – 50 % typical |
| **Site Dependency** | Requires harbor/shoreline | Mobile, requires sheltered water | Beach or river bank |
| **Reusability** | One dock = many ships | High | High (bags reused) |
| **Flooding Time** | 2 – 6 hours | 1 – 3 hours (ballasting) | Not applicable |
| **Risk of Mishap** | Low | Medium (weather-sensitive) | Medium (bag failure) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Building Dock Launching — Process Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Dry Building Dock] --> B[Pump Out Water / Inspect Caisson]
    B --> C[Lay Keel and Bilge Blocks]
    C --> D[Construct Hull and Outfit Ship]
    D --> E[Seal Dock Gates and Caisson]
    E --> F{Stability Check Complete?}
    F -- No --> G[Add Ballast or Restraints]
    G --> E
    F -- Yes --> H[Open Flooding Valves]
    H --> I[Water Rises in Dock]
    I --> J{Buoyancy Exceeds Weight?}
    J -- No --> I
    J -- Yes --> K[Ship Lifts Off Keel Blocks]
    K --> L[Tugs Maneuver Vessel Out]
    L --> M[End: Ship Afloat in Harbor]
```

---

## 4.2 Floating Dock Launching — System Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FD[Floating Dock Assembly]
        WL[Left Wing Wall] --- PT[Pontoon With Ballast Tanks]
        PT --- WR[Right Wing Wall]
        KB[Keel Blocks on Pontoon Deck] --- PT
    end
    SH[Ship Hull To Be Lifted] --- KB
    subgraph PH[Phases of Operation]
        P1[1 Ballast Dock To Submerge] --> P2[2 Position Ship Over Pontoon]
        P2 --> P3[3 Deballast To Lift Ship]
        P3 --> P4[4 Tow Combined Unit To Berth]
        P4 --> P5[5 Lower Ship Into Water]
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Air Bag Launching — Cross-Section Schematic (Mermaid Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph WAT[Waterline]
        WL1[Water Surface]
    end
    subgraph GROUND[Sloped Launching Way]
        SL[Earth Slope 1 to 15 to 1 to 20] --- BG1[Bag 1 Leading]
        BG1 --- BG2[Bag 2 Mid]
        BG2 --- BG3[Bag 3 Trailing]
    end
    subgraph HULL[Vessel Hull]
        HU[Hull Bottom Plates] --- SU[Ship Superstructure]
    end
    HULL --- BG1
    HULL --- BG2
    HULL --- BG3
    W1[Winch and Cable] --- SU
    BGR[Bag Recovery Team] --- BG3
    BGR -.Reinflate and Reposition.-> BG1
```

---

## 4.4 Combined Functional Architecture: All Three Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Alternative Launching Methods] --> R[Floating Off Methods]
    Q --> S[Air Bag Method]
    R --> R1[Building Dock - Permanent Civil Structure]
    R --> R2[Floating Dock - Mobile Steel Structure]
    S --> S1[Inflated Rubber Bags on Slope]
    R1 --> R1A[Floods To Achieve Buoyancy]
    R2 --> R2A[Deballasts To Achieve Buoyancy]
    S1 --> S1A[Rolls Hull Down Slope With Low Friction]
    R1A --> X[Ship Enters Water With Zero Velocity]
    R2A --> X
    S1A --> Y[Ship Enters Water With Controlled Velocity]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define launching by floating off. Mention the two principal methods used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Launching by floating off is a method of introducing a ship to water in which the vessel is constructed in a dewatered chamber and the chamber is subsequently flooded, allowing the ship to achieve positive buoyancy and float free without any longitudinal sliding motion. The two principal methods are:
1. **Building Dock (Graving Dock) method** — using a permanent civil dock structure.
2. **Floating Dock method** — using a mobile submersible steel dock.

(Each point: 1 mark. Full marks: 3)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**List any three advantages of air bag launching over conventional slipway launching.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Lower capital investment** — no permanent slipway, ground ways, or poppet structures are required. (1 mark)
2. **Portability and reusability** — the same set of bags can be used for multiple vessel launches at different sites. (1 mark)
3. **Adaptability to remote or underdeveloped sites** — suitable for beach, riverbank, or muddy terrain where slipway construction is impractical. (1 mark)

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the operational sequence of the **building dock method** of ship launching with a neat flowchart. Discuss the engineering precautions required during flooding. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship of lightweight **12,000 tonnes** is to be floated off in a building dock. The waterplane area of the hull at the float-off draft is **3,200 m²**. Seawater density is **1,025 kg/m³**. Calculate the **minimum water depth** required in the dock for the ship to just lift off the keel blocks. Apply a **15 % safety margin** and state the recommended flooding depth. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The operational sequence is as follows:

1. **Dry-out and Inspection (1 mark):** The graving dock is pumped completely dry. The caisson/flap gate is checked for watertight integrity. Keel blocks and bilge blocks are aligned with the ship's lines plan.

2. **Construction (1 mark):** The ship is built inside the dock, allowing full access from all sides. Outfitting (engines, piping, electrical, painting) can proceed to 80–100 % completion in the protected environment.

3. **Pre-Flooding Checks (1 mark):** All dock seals are verified. Stability calculations (GM, GZ curves) at the partially flooded intermediate drafts are reviewed. Bilge and dock drainage pumps are isolated.

4. **Flooding Operation (1 mark):** Flooding valves (in the caisson or dock floor) are opened in a controlled sequence. Flow rate is adjusted to prevent turbulence. The water level is monitored continuously.

5. **Lift-off and Departure (1 mark):** As water reaches the critical depth, the ship lifts vertically off the keel blocks with no horizontal motion. Tugs enter the dock, take up lines, and maneuver the vessel out into the harbor.

6. **Engineering Precautions (2 marks):**
   - Flooding must be **slow and symmetrical** to avoid listing.
   - **Transverse stability** must be verified at all intermediate drafts.
   - All **loose gear, scaffolding, and temporary supports** must be removed before flooding.
   - **Emergency dewatering pumps** must remain on standby.
   - **Mooring lines** must be pre-rigged before flooding begins.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:**
- Lightweight: $\Delta = 12{,}000$ tonnes $= 12{,}000{,}000$ kg
- Waterplane area: $A_{wp} = 3{,}200$ m²
- Seawater density: $\rho = 1{,}025$ kg/m³
- Safety margin: 15 %

**Step 1 — Theoretical minimum depth (1 mark):**

$$h_{min} = \frac{\Delta}{\rho \cdot A_{wp}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitution (1 mark):**

$$h_{min} = \frac{12{,}000{,}000}{1{,}025 \times 3{,}200}$$

**Step 3 — Denominator calculation (1 mark):**

$$1{,}025 \times 3{,}200 = 3{,}280{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Division (1 mark):**

$$h_{min} = \frac{12{,}000{,}000}{3{,}280{,}000} \approx 3.659 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Apply safety margin (1 mark):**

$$h_{recommended} = 1.15 \times 3.659 \approx 4.21 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation (1 mark):** The dock should be flooded to a depth of at least **4.21 m** above the keel blocks. The dock designer should also verify that the dock sill depth plus the tidal range accommodates the ship's float-off draft.

**Step 7 — Final statement (1 mark):** Recommended flooding depth = **4.21 m** (rounded up to 4.25 m for engineering conservatism).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Describe the **air bag method** of ship launching. List its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vessel of weight **8,000 kN** is to be launched using air bags on a slope of **1 in 20**. The rolling friction coefficient between the bag and the ground is **0.04**. Calculate: (i) the component of weight acting along the slope, (ii) the total rolling friction force, and (iii) the net downhill force (or required winch force) on the vessel. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Description (4 marks):**
Air bag launching uses high-pressure inflated rubber cylinders placed beneath the hull. The ship is rolled down a prepared slope (1:10 to 1:20) on these bags. As the leading bags enter the water, they are deflated and recovered; trailing bags are repositioned at the bow. The cycle continues until the vessel is fully waterborne. Bags are typically 0.8 to 1.8 m in diameter, working at 0.2 to 0.4 MPa, and inflated with compressed air.

**Advantages (2 marks):**
- Low capital cost compared to slipway construction.
- Bags are reusable across multiple vessel sizes and sites.
- Suitable for remote sites and developing shipyards.
- Reduced structural impact compared to side launching.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Limited to smaller vessels (typically < 10,000 DWT).
- Requires good weather and calm sea conditions.
- Bag puncture or pressure loss can be hazardous.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:**
- Vessel weight: $W = 8{,}000$ kN $= 8 \times 10^6$ N
- Slope: 1 in 20, so $\tan\theta = 1/20 = 0.05$
- Friction coefficient: $\mu_r = 0.04$

**Step 1 — Slope angle (1 mark):**

$$\theta = \arctan(0.05) \approx 2.86° \quad \text{(small angle, so } \sin\theta \approx 0.05, \cos\theta \approx 0.999\text{)}$$

**Step 2 — Component of weight along slope (2 marks):**

$$F_{down} = W \cdot \sin\theta = 8{,}000 \times 0.05 = 400 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Rolling friction force (2 marks):**

$$F_{roll} = \mu_r \cdot W \cdot \cos\theta = 0.04 \times 8{,}000 \times 0.999 \approx 319.7 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Net force (2 marks):**

$$F_{net} = F_{down} - F_{roll} = 400 - 319.7 = 80.3 \text{ kN (downhill)}$$

**Conclusion:** Since the net force is positive and directed downhill, the vessel will **self-launch** without winching. The downhill gravitational component exceeds the rolling resistance. (If $F_{net}$ were negative, a winch would be required.)

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert tonnes to kg** in buoyancy problems — the KTU examiner will deduct 1 mark if units are inconsistent in the final answer.
> 2. **Confusing sliding friction with rolling friction** in air bag problems. Sliding friction uses $\mu_s \approx 0.3$–$0.5$; rolling friction uses $\mu_r \approx 0.02$–$0.05$. Using the wrong coefficient gives a wildly incorrect answer.
> 3. **Omitting the safety margin** in dock-flooding depth calculations. The KTU 2024 scheme expects an explicit mention of the safety margin in your solution.
> 4. **Missing the flowchart / diagram** in long-answer questions. KTU valuation key typically allocates **1 to 2 marks** for a clear sequential diagram. Even a hand-drawn rough sketch earns partial credit.
> 5. **Skipping the interpretation step** — do not just write a number; always state what the numerical answer means in engineering terms.

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Three alternative launching methods** are: Building Dock, Floating Dock, and Air Bag.
- [x] **Floating-off methods are gravity-free launches** — the ship enters water with near-zero velocity.
- [x] **Building dock** is a permanent civil structure; **floating dock** is a mobile steel assembly.
- [x] **Floating dock sequence: Submerge → Position → Deballast → Tow** (mnemonic: **S-P-D-T**).
- [x] **Air bag launching** uses rubber cylinders inflated to 0.2–0.4 MPa, rolling friction $\mu_r \approx 0.02$–$0.05$.
- [x] **Key formula for dock flooding depth:** $h_{min} = \Delta / (\rho \cdot A_{wp})$.
- [x] **Air bag launch is self-propelled** if $\tan\theta > \mu_r$.
- [x] **Building dock** allows 80–100 % outfitting; air bag allows only 20–50 %.
- [x] **Floating dock** can lift vessels up to 100,000+ tonnes; air bag limited to ~10,000 tonnes.
- [x] **No longitudinal sliding motion** in floating-off methods → no impact loads on the hull.
- [x] **Always include a 10–15 % safety margin** in float-off depth calculations.
- [x] **Stability check (GM, GZ)** is mandatory before dock flooding.
- [x] **Air bag launching** is most popular in Chinese, Southeast Asian, and Indian small-craft shipyards.
- [x] **Floating dock** submergence depth formula: $d_{min} = (W_{dock} + W_{ship}) / (\rho \cdot g \cdot L_p \cdot B_p)$.
- [x] **KTU 2024 CO mapping:** This topic maps to **CO4** — *Plan and supervise non-conventional ship launching operations.*
